translation: O mankind, fear your Lord, who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women. And fear Allah, through whom you ask one another, and the wombs. Indeed Allah is ever, over you, an Observer
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا**

*O mankind, have Taqwa of your Lord, who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women. And have Taqwa of Allah, through whom you ask one another, and the wombs. Indeed Allah is ever, over you, an Observer.*

### The Universal Call and Social Foundations

Surah Al-Imran concludes with a commandment directed specifically to the believing community: those who have iman should have patience, compete with each other in patience, compete with the enemy in perseverance, continue to march forward, remain vigilant, and have Taqwa of Allah ﷻ so that all of you may succeed. Surah An-Nisa takes that commandment of Taqwa and expands it to all of humanity from the very beginning. Instead of "O you who believe," Allah ﷻ calls out: **يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ** (O mankind).

This surah is the third major Medinan surah after Al-Baqarah and Al-Imran, and it pairs remarkably well in theme and structure with Surah Al-Ma'idah. Both surahs share similar subjects and syntax. While An-Nisa addresses multiple topics, they can be unified under one central theme: the social ethics of Islam, the social constructs of Islam, the foundations of an Islamic society. Sometimes Allah ﷻ makes very broad ethical remarks—golden rules, if you will—and that's how this surah begins. At other times, He ﷻ gets into very specific details.

The surah also deals extensively with one of the biggest problems within any society: troublemakers, specifically hypocrites. Perhaps no surah in the Quran deals in more detail with the subject of hypocrisy than this one, with approximately sixty ayahs dedicated to that subject. But in the beginning, we find Allah ﷻ establishing the basis of a sound Muslim community, laying the foundations for the early community in Medina.

### Created from a Single Soul

**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ** - O mankind, have Taqwa of your Lord. Our first relationship is not with each other but with Allah ﷻ as our Master and Creator. We must be cautious and conscious of Him ﷻ.

**ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ** - The One who created you from a single person. Whenever Allah ﷻ discusses creation from a single person in the Quran, there are profound social implications. When He ﷻ begins with "O people," our first relationship is with Him ﷻ as our Creator, and we must be cautious and conscious of Him ﷻ. But immediately after, we're told we have a single source as far as creation. We come from one single person; we share an ancestry.

This means that even though we are different peoples, different nations, different ethnicities, we only see each other as children of Adam (AS), and we are on equal footing in that sense. The only thing that sets us apart—beautifully in our religion—the only thing that gives one person a higher rank above another is Taqwa. And so the ayah begins with Taqwa: you are all created from a single source.

**وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا** - And He created from it its mate. The word "zawj" in Arabic means spouse. From that nafs (soul)—referring to Adam (AS)—Allah ﷻ created his spouse, Hawwa (AS).

**وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً** - And He drew out from them many men and many women. From that original couple, all of humanity has spread across the earth.

### Breaking Down Barriers

Even though racism and nationalism unfortunately exist in some parts of the Muslim world and certain areas of the United States, Alhamdulillah, in most parts of America, we don't find these attitudes at the levels found elsewhere. While we still sometimes segregate ourselves—with separate Arab masajid, Desi masajid, communities for Somalis, African Americans, Turks, and Indonesians each going in different directions—generally, we are far more united here than in other parts of the world.

But Allah ﷻ didn't say to the Muslims alone, "You are all one people"—that statement appears elsewhere in the Quran. Here, He ﷻ is speaking to all of humanity. One of the first things Islam destroyed was racism. One of the first things it eliminated, and yet today one of the greatest problems in the world is still racism, nationalism, and any form of superiority of one group over another.

It's not just about race. Sometimes it's about nationality. What happens in world politics is that lives lost in Africa or some unnamed country are considered less valuable than a life lost in the West, and so that makes more headlines. Depending on social status or wealth, a celebrity's death becomes a big deal, but when a nobody dies, who cares? A famous person or politician gets sick and it's major headlines, while children getting sick with the same disease every day go untreated and nobody cares. This is the consequence of people not looking at each other as equal.

Islam came to eliminate this view that one life is more important than another.

### The Second Command of Taqwa

**وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ** - And have Taqwa of Allah. In the beginning of the ayah, when Allah ﷻ says **ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ** (have Taqwa of your Lord), it was about our relationship with Him ﷻ alone. Now, the way we deal with each other is also a manifestation of Taqwa. Notice the second time, Allah ﷻ doesn't say "Ittaqu Rabbakum" but rather "Ittaqullah."

Which warning is stronger? **ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ** (Ittaqullah) is the stronger warning. **ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ** was a call to have Taqwa of your Master—the One who has done so much for you, the One who continues to sustain and provide for you. But now that Allah ﷻ has mentioned the humanity He ﷻ has offered you—which you haven't been able to respect—comes the stronger warning.

**ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ** - The One whose name you beseech, the One you call upon when you ask each other for things. "I swear to God, please stop!" "For God's sake, please let me borrow that money." "For Allah's sake..." You call on each other using His ﷻ name.

**وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ** - And the wombs. Be cautious of the wombs. Now that you know Allah ﷻ created you from a single parent and then from that parent's spouse, you should know that you must forever dignify and respect the womb—meaning the mother. What this means is that all relationships are, in the end, a byproduct of the womb.

I am related to my siblings because of my mother, and it keeps going back. A husband and wife share the relationship of the womb because through their union, a child is born, and then it keeps going further and further back. That's how all of our families get connected, eventually back to Adam and Hawwa (AS). The connection of the mother is the connection of humanity itself.

So be cautious of Allah ﷻ—that is to say, be cautious of the wombs. This is the surah that lays the foundations for social justice and social principles in Islam. The fundamental social principle is that the basic unit of society is the family, and the foundation of society is the womb of the mother. That relationship must be respected first and foremost in the highest form.

### Always Under His Watch

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا** - Indeed, Allah has always been watchful over you. The word "Raqib" comes from "raqabah," which means the back of the neck. A Raqib is someone who holds the back of your neck—like when you're walking with an animal that tends to wander everywhere. If your goat is a little wild, what do you do? You grab the back of its neck and walk with it. Every time it thinks about taking a turn, you hold it straight and keep it on course.

The image here is powerful: be fearful of Allah ﷻ. Allah ﷻ has always had His hand on your neck. He ﷻ has always been like this over you. Don't think you have free reign, that you're free to go wherever you want.

To put this in personal perspective, consider our relationship with our mothers. When we're little, we can't eat without her, can't sleep without her, depend on her for everything. Then we become fifteen or sixteen, and when Mom says, "Come home and have dinner," we respond, "No, Ma, I'm gonna eat with my friends. I'll come home later." We get older and more separated from our mothers. The time comes when we get married, and when she calls, we send her to voicemail. When we visit her, we just go to say salaam, stay a little while, and start getting uncomfortable. "Mom, I'll talk to you later. I gotta go."

That same relationship—we feel like our neck has been released. We can go and do whatever we want. We don't need Mom anymore. She's not that important anymore. We needed her when we were little, but now we don't see eye to eye. She doesn't understand. She's always upset.

Allah ﷻ says whether you think you're free or not, you're not free. Respect the womb.

translation: And give to the orphans their properties and do not substitute the defective [of your own] for the good [of theirs]. And do not consume their properties into your own. Indeed, that is ever a great sin
commentary:
**وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَبَدَّلُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بِٱلطَّيِّبِ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حُوبًا كَبِيرًا**

*And give to the orphans their properties and do not substitute the defective for the good. And do not consume their properties into your own. Indeed, that is ever a great sin.*

### From Family to Society

Respecting the womb—what does it manifest itself as practically? Beyond your own family, the social manifestation of respecting the womb is taking care of those who have lost that relationship. People who lost the connection of the womb because the mother passed away, the father passed away—you take care of the orphans.

Two groups of people are prominently mentioned in the beginning of this surah: women and orphans. Why orphans and why women? Because orphans lose the connection with their parents, and women end up in another household. When women marry, they move to another house and naturally, most of the time, become distant from their parents. They can visit when they can, but most of the time they have obligations in their new household, so they become separated.

The protection of a family for a child comes from where? From the parents. That's where protection comes from. When that shadow is removed, you have to replace it with another protective shadow. That's what Islamic society is supposed to do—provide a shadow to those who are out in the sun without that protection, to provide them that comfort.

### Guarding Orphans' Rights

**وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ** - Give orphans their properties. Immediately, directly, give them their wealth.

**وَلَا تَتَبَدَّلُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بِٱلطَّيِّبِ** - And do not replace the good with the filthy. Don't substitute the defective for the good. In other words, you're taking care of orphans who left some assets behind. Maybe the child's father passed away and owned a car or something like that. After he passed, the car came to you for safekeeping, but it's actually the child's property. 

Let's say it's a Lexus, but you own a Hyundai from 1987. You start thinking, "Maybe I should just park the Hyundai and drive the Lexus instead." Allah ﷻ says you don't get to do that—don't replace the good with the bad.

**وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ** - And don't mix your wealth with theirs. The word "ila" here carries the meaning of mixing. Don't mix their money with yours.

To some extent, it's impossible not to do that at all. Allah ﷻ told us about this in Surah Al-Baqarah. You can cook together, you can make meals together, because it won't be possible for you to keep everything completely separate. The Sahaba (RA) were so worried about this that they would make two separate pots—one for themselves and one for the orphan—because they feared that if they mixed the food together, they might inadvertently take a spoonful of the orphan's rights. Allah ﷻ said no, you don't have to go that far. You can mix that much—that much is fine. Allah ﷻ knows who intends corruption and who intends good.

But make sure their assets are protected. Some child whose responsibility you've taken over—their parents left $30,000 or $40,000 in an account. You're taking care of them, and you think, "Managing this account is going to be a little difficult. I'll just transfer the funds to my account. I'll write it down somewhere that this much is theirs." Then later on, the paper disappears. You need some gas, or your credit card is over the limit a little bit, and their debit card is in your wallet, so you just swipe it. "They won't feel it."

On Judgment Day, those swipes will come through. You don't feel them at the time—at the time it feels like nothing. That's why when you mix assets together, you don't even realize what's happening.

**إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حُوبًا كَبِيرًا** - Indeed, it has always been a great sin. The word "hub" comes from "haba." In old Arabic, you would say "indi haba," meaning "I have dependents"—close relationships like mother, father, sister, daughter that you take care of. Hub refers to everything that is forbidden intensely, and if you do it, you deserve to be scolded. It's an evil that if you commit, you deserve real scolding, a serious reprimand. It has always been something worthy of serious rebuke, something detestable, something that will land you in a lot of trouble with Allah ﷻ—and a huge one at that.

translation: And if you fear that you will not deal justly with the orphan girls, then marry those that please you of [other] women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one or those your right hand possesses. That is more suitable that you may not incline [to injustice]
commentary:
**وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا۟ فِى ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ فَٱنكِحُوا۟ مَا طَابَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ مَثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ فَوَٰحِدَةً أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعُولُوا۟**

*And if you fear that you will not deal justly with the orphan girls, then marry those that please you of other women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then marry only one or those your right hand possesses. That is more suitable that you may not incline to injustice.*

### Marriage as Protection for Orphans

**وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا۟ فِى ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ** - And if you fear that you will not be able to do justice with the orphans. This is a special case being discussed: girls—specifically a group of orphan girls under your care. They were children when they came under your wing, but now they've gotten older, beyond childhood, and you're worried that you won't be able to do justice among them.

**فَٱنكِحُوا۟ مَا طَابَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ** - Then marry those that please you from among the women. The provision is given: don't create an awkward situation in your home. If you think you won't be able to deal with them fairly, you can marry them. Remember, foster parents are not biological parents, so marriage is permissible. But notice the wording: "whatever you may find pleasing from among the women." In other words, if you actually like them and they like you, then go ahead and marry them. Don't do this as merely a charitable act—there should be genuine mutual affection.

### Understanding Polygamy in Context

**مَثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ** - Two, three, and four. Mathna means two per person, thulatha means three per person, ruba'a means four per person. This is the ayah concerning polygamy.

Unfortunately, there are seminars held at places like Harvard on Islamic reform and how we need to reinterpret laws on polygamy. First of all, polygamy doesn't even exist as a practice in the West, so why does it need reform? If it existed, then perhaps you could discuss reforming it. But where does it actually exist? Even in the Muslim world, it's rare. And where it does happen, it has become part of the social norms, so people there don't find it strange.

But the original context must be understood. The original context was about taking care of orphans. What is marriage in the social principle of Islam? Marriage is a means of offering women protection. It's a transfer of responsibility. The original wali (guardian) is the father. When a girl gets married, her new wali is the husband. The father was responsible for her education, her dignity, her provision, her security—those were his responsibilities. The moment the nikah happens, all the responsibilities that used to be on the father are now on the husband. Now he's the head of that household and must take care of her.

So the concept is: you're taking care of the orphan from a distance. But if you think you're not doing a good enough job, if you think that making the responsibility official through marriage would enable you to take better care of them, then go ahead. But the original idea was still to provide these women a proper household.

### The Condition of Justice

**فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ فَوَٰحِدَةً** - But if you fear that you will not be just, then marry only one. At the same time, someone might use this part of the ayah to say, "I'm really trying to do justice—that's why I want to marry three or four!"

Allah ﷻ responds: "If you're afraid you're not going to be able to do justice, then just one."

The words 'adl and qist both relate to justice, but there are nuances. 'Adl means to make the right verdict, to pass the right judgment. Qist means to make sure the prerequisites for justice are there. What do I mean by prerequisites? Make sure the witnesses have been interrogated and screened. Make sure the evidence isn't faked. Make sure the people presenting at trial all have proper background checks. That's the pre-trial screening process, because if any of those things are corrupt, even if you have a good judge, you won't have a good outcome.

So qist has three parts: First, there's the pre-trial—making sure all the prerequisites of justice are there. Second, the judgment must be made fairly after weighing all the evidence. Third, and most importantly, the judgment must actually be executed. Sometimes judgment is passed but not executed. You say this one gets fifty, this one gets twenty—judgment is passed, but later the money isn't delivered. Does that happen?

Qist means the entire justice process is complete from beginning to end. It's the more comprehensive term. 'Adl can be thought of as one-third of that model—just passing the fair verdict. When options are brought before you and you're put in a position of passing judgment, you do so with justice, with clear open justice.

In this ayah, Allah ﷻ says that if you're not able to make fair judgments between them—if you're mentally biased, emotionally biased toward one over the other—He ﷻ isn't even talking about executing justice yet. He's saying at the very start: then just restrict yourself to one.

**أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ** - Or those your right hands possess. We've discussed this concept in previous sessions.

**ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعُولُوا۟** - That is closer that you may not incline to injustice. "Adna" means closer, implying better in some sense—closer to avoiding injustice. The word "ta'ulu" comes from words related to injustice. Specifically, 'ala means to take more than you deserve. You might marry more than you deserve to marry, or give somebody more than they deserve to get, or take from one more than you deserve to take.

translation: And give the women [upon marriage] their [bridal] gifts graciously. But if they give up willingly to you anything of it, then take it in satisfaction and ease
commentary:
**وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ صَدُقَـٰتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً ۚ فَإِن طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَن شَىْءٍ مِّنْهُ نَفْسًا فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيٓـًٔا مَّرِيٓـًٔا**

*And give the women their dowries as a gift. But if they willingly give up to you some of it, then consume it with satisfaction and ease.*

**وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ صَدُقَـٰتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً** - Give women their dowries as a gift. The mahr (dowry) must be given to women as an outright gift, not as something owed that you're reluctantly paying.

**فَإِن طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَن شَىْءٍ مِّنْهُ نَفْسًا** - But if they willingly give up to you some of it from their own souls. If, from their hearts, with genuine willingness and without any pressure, they choose to give you back some portion of it.

**فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيٓـًٔا مَّرِيٓـًٔا** - Then consume it with satisfaction and ease. Only then may you take it and benefit from it in good conscience, with complete peace of mind and spiritual ease.

translation: And do not give the weak-minded your property, which Allah has made a means of sustenance for you, but provide for them with it and clothe them and speak to them words of appropriate kindness
commentary:
وَلَا تُؤْتُوا۟ ٱلسُّفَهَآءَ أَمْوَٰلَكُمُ ٱلَّتِى جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ قِيَـٰمًا وَٱرْزُقُوهُمْ فِيهَا وَٱكْسُوهُمْ وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا

This ayah establishes a protective framework for wealth management, particularly concerning those who lack the maturity to handle financial responsibility. Allah ﷻ commands: "And do not give the foolish your wealth which Allah has made for you a means of support."

The word السُّفَهَآءَ refers to those who lack sound judgment, particularly young people who haven't yet developed the maturity needed to manage assets responsibly. This applies especially to orphans who have inherited wealth from their parents but haven't reached an age where they can handle it wisely.

Allah ﷻ describes this wealth as something "which Allah has made for you a means of support" - قِيَـٰمًا. This word comes from the root meaning "to stand" or "to sustain." The wealth is described as belonging to the community's collective well-being, not just the individual. In modern terms, we might call this the idea of fiduciary responsibility - certain assets must be protected for the benefit of society and future generations.

The ayah then provides clear guidance on how to handle this situation: وَٱرْزُقُوهُمْ فِيهَا وَٱكْسُوهُمْ - "Provide for them from it and clothe them." The guardian isn't instructed to withhold everything from those under their care. Rather, they should use the wealth appropriately to meet the needs of those who own it - their food, clothing, shelter and other necessities. The key is responsible stewardship, not deprivation.

Finally, وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا - "And speak to them words of appropriate kindness." Just because someone is young or hasn't yet been given full access to their inheritance doesn't give anyone permission to speak to them disrespectfully. The phrase قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا emphasizes decent, honorable speech. This protects the dignity of the young person even while their access to funds is being managed by others.

This comprehensive approach addresses both the material and emotional needs of those under guardianship, establishing that Islamic law recognizes the importance of both financial protection and psychological respect.

translation: And test the orphans [in their abilities] until they reach marriageable age. Then if you perceive in them sound judgement, release their property to them. And do not consume it excessively and quickly, [anticipating] that they will grow up. And whoever, [when acting as guardian], is self-sufficient should refrain [from taking a fee]; and whoever is poor - let him take according to what is acceptable. Then when you release their property to them, bring witnesses upon them. And sufficient is Allah as Accountant
commentary:
وَٱبْتَلُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغُوا۟ ٱلنِّكَاحَ فَإِنْ ءَانَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًا فَٱدْفَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوهَآ إِسْرَافًا وَبِدَارًا أَن يَكْبَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن كَانَ غَنِيًّا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ فَقِيرًا فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِذَا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا

This ayah introduces a sophisticated system for determining when young people are ready to manage their own wealth. The command وَٱبْتَلُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ - "Test the orphans" - reveals that reaching a certain age alone isn't sufficient. True maturity must be assessed and demonstrated.

The testing should continue حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغُوا۟ ٱلنِّكَاحَ - "until they reach the age of marriage." This phrase indicates physical maturity, but the ayah immediately adds another requirement: فَإِنْ ءَانَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًا - "then if you perceive in them sound judgment."

The word ءَانَسْتُم is particularly meaningful. It comes from a root meaning to perceive or observe something from the outside. This acknowledges a fundamental truth about human relationships: we can never know someone's inner state with absolute certainty, especially as children mature into adults. Parents and guardians can only judge based on what they observe. The word رُشْدًا means uprightness, sound judgment and maturity in decision-making.

The practical implication is clear: give young people responsibilities with money before handing over everything. Let them manage smaller amounts, see how they handle it. Give them jobs or tasks that require judgment. Observe whether they make wise choices. This is a beautifully thoughtful social construct that recognizes both the importance of age-appropriate development and individual differences in maturity.

Once sound judgment is confirmed, فَٱدْفَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ - "then give them their wealth." But Allah ﷻ immediately warns against a temptation guardians might face: وَلَا تَأْكُلُوهَآ إِسْرَافًا وَبِدَارًا أَن يَكْبَرُوا۟ - "And do not consume it excessively and hastily before they grow up."

The word إِسْرَافًا means excessiveness, going over the top. A guardian might justify lavish spending by claiming it's all "operational expenses" - buying an expensive car "to drive the child around" or dining at costly restaurants because "the orphan needs to eat anyway." This is forbidden.

The word بِدَارًا carries the meaning of rushing ahead of time, trying to get ahead of something before it arrives. Some guardians, knowing the child will soon reach maturity and claim their inheritance, rush to spend as much as they can beforehand. This too is explicitly prohibited.

The ayah then addresses the guardian's own financial situation with remarkable balance: وَمَن كَانَ غَنِيًّا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ - "And whoever is wealthy should refrain entirely." The word فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ means to protect oneself completely from temptation, to keep far away from something harmful. If the guardian has their own wealth, they shouldn't touch the orphan's money at all.

But Allah ﷻ recognizes that not all guardians are wealthy: وَمَن كَانَ فَقِيرًا فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ - "And whoever is poor, let him take according to what is acceptable." Notice that no specific percentage or dollar amount is mentioned. Why? Because what is "acceptable" varies by society, time period and economic conditions. The principle is fairness and reasonableness - whatever compensation would be considered just by the standards of that community.

Finally, when the transfer of wealth does occur, فَإِذَا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ - "when you give them their wealth, take witnesses in their presence." This is a legal protection for both parties. The young person might later claim they didn't receive everything. The guardian needs documentation that the transfer was completed properly. This is sound financial and legal practice.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا - "And sufficient is Allah as Accountant." The word حَسِيبًا means the One who keeps perfect accounts, the Ultimate Auditor. Even if disputes arise in this world, even if witnesses fail or records are lost, Allah ﷻ has maintained a flawless record. This should instill both fear in those who might abuse their position and comfort in those who are wronged.

translation: For men is a share of what the parents and close relatives leave, and for women is a share of what the parents and close relatives leave, be it little or much - an obligatory share
commentary:
لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ مِمَّا قَلَّ مِنْهُ أَوْ كَثُرَ ۚ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا

This ayah represents a revolutionary shift in inheritance law and women's rights. Allah ﷻ declares: "For men is a share of what the parents and close relatives leave, and for women is a share of what the parents and close relatives leave."

To understand the magnitude of this statement, we must appreciate the historical context. In much of pre-Islamic Arabia, women themselves were considered property. When a man died, his wives could be inherited by his sons from other marriages - a practice called nikah al-maqt, which Islam abolished completely. Women had no right to own property, inherit wealth or conduct independent financial transactions.

This ayah establishes unequivocally: لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ - "For men is a share" - and وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ - "and for women is a share." The parallel structure emphasizes equality of right to inheritance, even though the specific portions may differ based on circumstances and responsibilities.

The word نَصِيبٌ is significant. It comes from a root meaning something that has been marked or designated specifically for someone. This isn't charity or a favor being done to women - it's their marked, designated right.

The ayah specifies the sources of this inheritance: مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ - "from what the parents and close relatives leave." This applies to estates from parents and other close family members.

Then comes a crucial phrase: مِمَّا قَلَّ مِنْهُ أَوْ كَثُرَ - "whether it is little or much." Some might have thought inheritance laws only apply to large estates, but Allah ﷻ clarifies that even small inheritances must be distributed according to these principles. Justice in inheritance isn't only for the wealthy.

The ayah concludes with powerful emphasis: نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا - "a legal obligation." The word مَّفْرُوضًا indicates something mandated, made obligatory, carved into law. This isn't a suggestion or a cultural preference that can be changed based on social trends. It's a divine mandate.

This was revolutionary in its time. If these verses had been revealed in 7th century Arabia and someone had complained about them, the complaint would have been: "Why is Islam favoring women so much? Why are you giving them so many rights?" Fast forward to modern times, and critics ask the opposite question: "Why doesn't Islam give women more?" The answer depends entirely on the lens through which one views the law - but the historical fact remains that Islam established women's property rights centuries before such concepts appeared in Western legal systems.

translation: And when [other] relatives and orphans and the needy are present at the [time of] division, then provide for them [something] out of the estate and speak to them words of appropriate kindness
commentary:
وَإِذَا حَضَرَ ٱلْقِسْمَةَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينُ فَٱرْزُقُوهُم مِّنْهُ وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا

This ayah addresses a specific social situation with remarkable sensitivity. The word ٱلْقِسْمَةَ refers to the division or distribution of inheritance - specifically, the formal occasion when the estate is being divided among the legal heirs.

Picture the scene: a wealthy person has died, and there's a gathering where the will is being executed. Family members who are designated heirs are sitting together as portions are announced and distributed. Perhaps a lawyer or estate executor is reading out: "To this person goes this amount, to that person goes that property."

Allah ﷻ says: وَإِذَا حَضَرَ ٱلْقِسْمَةَ - "And when relatives and orphans and the needy are present at the division." Notice who shows up at this gathering: أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ (relatives who aren't inheritors), وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ (orphans), وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينُ (those in desperate need).

These people aren't legally entitled to a share of the inheritance. They're not named in the will. But they heard that this division was taking place, and they showed up at the gate. Maybe they're hoping for some generosity. Perhaps they're distant relatives going through hard times. These are orphans who have nothing, or people so stuck in poverty that they've had to humble themselves by coming to ask.

Understand how difficult this is for them. No decent person wants to beg. No one with any other option would show up at someone's inheritance distribution asking for help. Relatives especially find it deeply embarrassing to have to put their hands out in front of other family members. The fact that they're there at all indicates the severity of their situation.

Allah ﷻ commands: فَٱرْزُقُوهُم مِّنْهُ - "Then provide for them from it." Don't turn them away. Don't act annoyed that they showed up. Don't make them feel worse than they already do. The heirs sitting inside, receiving wealth they didn't personally earn but inherited, should remember that this is divine provision being distributed. It shouldn't be hard to share some of it with those in genuine need.

The ayah continues: وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا - "And speak to them words of appropriate kindness." Don't humiliate them further. Don't question why they came or make them justify their need. Don't treat them as if they're being greedy. Speak with respect and gentleness. Remember that they're already in a vulnerable position, and you have the power to either ease their burden or compound their humiliation.

This ayah teaches profound social ethics. It reminds those receiving unexpected wealth through inheritance that they should be generous and kind-hearted toward others who are struggling, because this provision came to them as a gift from Allah ﷻ, not through their own efforts.

translation: And let those [executors and guardians] fear [injustice] as if they [themselves] had left weak offspring behind and feared for them. So let them fear Allah and speak words of appropriate justice
commentary:
وَلْيَخْشَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَـٰفًا خَافُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ فَلْيَتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْيَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا

This ayah appeals directly to the conscience in one of the most powerful ways imaginable. It connects the situation of orphans and vulnerable people seeking help to one's own children.

Allah ﷻ says: وَلْيَخْشَ ٱلَّذِينَ - "And let those fear" - fear meaning to have consciousness of consequences, to be mindful and concerned. Who should fear? لَوْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَـٰفًا خَافُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ - "Those who, if they left behind weak offspring, would fear for them."

The ayah asks you to imagine: what if you died right now? What if you left this world today, and your children - weak, unable to care for themselves, dependent - were left behind? The word ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَـٰفًا emphasizes children who are vulnerable, without strength or resources.

خَافُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ - "They would fear for them." You would be terrified for your children. How will they survive? Who will take care of them? Will they have food, shelter, education? Will people treat them kindly or take advantage of them? This natural parental fear exists in every heart.

Now look at the orphan standing in front of you asking for help. That orphan is someone's child. Some parent died fearing exactly what you would fear - what will happen to my children when I'm gone?

This is the profound empathy Allah ﷻ is cultivating. When you see that orphan, don't see a stranger or a burden. See your own child. See what could happen to your own children if circumstances were different. This should transform your entire perspective.

Having established this emotional connection, Allah ﷻ gives two commands:

فَلْيَتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - "Then let them fear Allah." Be conscious of Allah's awareness, because He ﷻ can make your fears become reality if you don't treat orphans with justice and kindness. The protection of your own children may depend on how you treat someone else's orphaned children.

وَلْيَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا - "And let them speak words of appropriate justice." The word سَدِيدًا is particularly rich. It comes from a root meaning to block up holes, to patch cracks, to repair breaks in a wall or dam. Figuratively, it means speech that is sound, with no cracks or flaws - direct, honest, clear communication that prevents misunderstanding.

When an orphan asks for help, don't give them runarounds or vague promises. Don't say: "Well, it's complicated, and there are legal proceedings, and we'll have to see..." Just be straightforward: "Yes, here's what I can give you" or "I'd like you to have this." Treat them with the dignity and directness you would want someone to show your own children.

These ayahs establish comprehensive social ethics: financial protection for the vulnerable, legal frameworks for inheritance, women's property rights, and above all, a call to profound empathy. The question this raises for contemporary Muslims is: how do we know who the orphans and vulnerable people are in our communities?

Often, we don't. We go to the masjid, pray and leave, without ever knowing what the families around us are going through. True community means knowing each other - inviting people to dinner, visiting homes, learning about each other's challenges. When families know each other, we become aware of who has lost a parent, who is struggling with debt, who needs support. We can help them before they ever have to ask.

This is the level of social sensitivity and community cohesion these ayahs are calling us toward - not just as individuals, but as an Ummah that genuinely cares for its most vulnerable members.

translation: Indeed, those who devour the property of orphans unjustly are only consuming into their bellies fire. And they will be burned in a Blaze
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ ظُلْمًا إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا ۖ وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيرًا

This ayah concludes the opening passage of Surah An-Nisa with a severe warning: "Indeed, those who consume the wealth of orphans wrongfully—they are only consuming fire into their bellies, and they will burn in a blazing fire."

The imagery here is deliberately frightening. Allah ﷻ describes fire inside their bellies and fire outside them in the Hereafter. They will be engulfed from every direction. The person who thinks they've gotten away with stealing from orphans is literally consuming fire for themselves, even in this world.

Some scholars have suggested another dimension to this punishment: there will be no *barakah* (blessing) in anything they do. At the end of the day, we earn money so we can sustain ourselves with food. Those who wrongfully consume the wealth of orphans will find no good coming from anything they eat. Sickness, disease, and afflictions will pursue them because they ate money that was haraam.

The word سَعِير refers specifically to a fire with tall, intense flames. This reinforces the completeness of their punishment—fire within and fire without, leaving no escape.

translation: Allah instructs you concerning your children: for the male, what is equal to the share of two females. But if there are [only] daughters, two or more, for them is two thirds of one's estate. And if there is only one, for her is half. And for one's parents, to each one of them is a sixth of his estate if he left children. But if he had no children and the parents [alone] inherit from him, then for his mother is one third. And if he had brothers [or sisters], for his mother is a sixth, after any bequest he [may have] made or debt. Your parents or your children - you know not which of them are nearest to you in benefit. [These shares are] an obligation [imposed] by Allah. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
يُوصِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ ۖ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۚ فَإِن كُنَّ نِسَآءً فَوْقَ ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثَا مَا تَرَكَ ۖ وَإِن كَانَتْ وَٰحِدَةً فَلَهَا ٱلنِّصْفُ ۚ وَلِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ إِن كَانَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَوَرِثَهُۥٓ أَبَوَاهُ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلثُّلُثُ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُۥٓ إِخْوَةٌ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِى بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ لَا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعًا ۚ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

Allah ﷻ opens this ayah with يُوصِيكُمُ, which comes from the root وصية —a will or testament left at the time of death. But when Allah ﷻ uses this verb, it carries the weight of a divine command, a strongly emphasized instruction that cannot be taken lightly.

"Allah instructs you concerning your children: for the male, what is equal to the share of two females." The word حَظّ is used here, which means a good portion or share. This is the beginning of the inheritance laws that would reshape Arabian society completely.

The ayah continues with detailed proportions: if there are only daughters—two or more—they receive two-thirds of the inheritance. If there is only one daughter, she receives half. For the parents of the deceased, each receives one-sixth if the deceased had children. If there are no children and only the parents inherit, then the mother receives one-third. But if the deceased had siblings, the mother's share is reduced to one-sixth.

All of this distribution happens مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِى بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ—"after any bequest that was made or debt that must be paid." The scholars have established that approximately one-third of a person's wealth can be freely distributed according to their wishes through a will. But the remaining majority must be distributed according to the divine proportions Allah ﷻ has established. This protects family members from being disinherited unjustly.

These ayahs are brief, yet entire volumes of Islamic jurisprudence have been written to address the intricate scenarios that arise from them. The Quran provides the foundational principles, and the scholars work out the implications and details.

Then comes a profound statement: "Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them are closer to you in benefit." The word لَا تَدْرُونَ is particularly strong. It comes from the root *daraya*, which means to know even a little bit. When used in the negative, it means you don't have even the slightest clue. You have no idea who will die first, who will bring you more benefit, whose wealth will help whom. 

This is a warning against obsessing over inheritance—whether you're a parent calculating what you'll leave behind, or a child wondering what you'll receive. Don't worry about it. You don't know what Allah ﷻ has planned.

The ayah concludes: فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ —"This is an obligation from Allah." This could be understood as both an emphasis and a warning: this is non-negotiable. Then Allah ﷻ reminds us: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا—"Indeed, Allah is Ever-Knowing and All-Wise." He knows what we don't know, and His distributions contain wisdom we may not immediately perceive.

translation: And for you is half of what your wives leave if they have no child. But if they have a child, for you is one fourth of what they leave, after any bequest they [may have] made or debt. And for the wives is one fourth if you leave no child. But if you leave a child, then for them is an eighth of what you leave, after any bequest you [may have] made or debt. And if a man or woman leaves neither ascendants nor descendants but has a brother or a sister, then for each one of them is a sixth. But if they are more than two, they share a third, after any bequest which was made or debt, as long as there is no detriment [caused]. [This is] an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is Knowing and Forbearing
commentary:
۞ وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ أَزْوَٰجُكُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُنَّ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌ فَلَكُمُ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْنَ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِينَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۚ وَلَهُنَّ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّكُمْ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ فَلَهُنَّ ٱلثُّمُنُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُم ۚ مِّنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ تُوصُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ وَإِن كَانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلَـٰلَةً أَوِ ٱمْرَأَةٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أَخٌ أَوْ أُخْتٌ فَلِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ فَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فِى ٱلثُّلُثِ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصَىٰ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ غَيْرَ مُضَآرٍّ ۚ وَصِيَّةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ

Now the address shifts to men: "And for you is half of what your wives leave if they have no child. But if they have a child, then for you is one-fourth of what they leave." This could refer to polygamous marriages where that particular wife had no children, or to children in general from any marriage. After the bequest and debt are settled, the husband receives his portion.

Then the reverse: "And for them [the wives] is one-fourth of what you leave if you have no child. But if you have a child, then for them is one-eighth of what you leave." Again, this is after the will and debts have been executed.

The ayah then addresses a special category: الكَلَالَة. This term refers to someone who dies leaving neither parents above them nor children below them—no direct lineage. If such a person has a brother or sister, each receives one-sixth. If there are more siblings than that, they share one-third collectively.

Classical scholars have discussed whether "brother or sister" here refers to full siblings (same mother and father), paternal half-siblings (same father, different mothers), or maternal half-siblings (same mother, different fathers). The distinction matters in Islamic inheritance law, and the scholars have worked out these details through careful analysis of this and related texts.

The phrase غَيْرَ مُضَآرٍّ is crucial: "without intending harm." This means the person making the will should not use their final testament as a weapon to harm their heirs—cutting someone out out of spite, or manipulating the distribution to hurt certain family members. Even in death, we must be just.

The ayah ends: وَصِيَّةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ—"This is a commandment from Allah, and Allah is All-Knowing and Most Forbearing." The attribute حَلِيم is beautiful here. Halim means forbearing, understanding, patient with His servants even when they struggle. Allah ﷻ knows that matters of inheritance can be emotionally charged, filled with grief and family tension. He is Halim—He understands what we're going through and shows us mercy in our difficulty.

## Ayah 13-14

تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ نَارًا خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

"Those are the limits set by Allah." The word حُدُود refers to boundaries that must not be crossed. These inheritance laws aren't suggestions—they are divine boundaries.

"And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger, He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally." Notice something significant here: the ayah begins in the singular—"whoever obeys"—but then shifts to the plural: خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا, "they will abide eternally therein." This is a beautiful literary shift called *iltifat* in Arabic rhetoric. Why the change? Because one of the joys of Paradise is that we won't be alone. We'll be reunited with family, with friends, with other believers. Even though the command is individual, the reward is collective. Paradise is a place of community.

"And that is the great success"—الْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ.

Then comes the contrast: "And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger and transgresses His limits, He will admit him into Fire, wherein he will abide eternally."

These ayahs are not about belief and disbelief—they're about obedience and disobedience. This is crucial. When we think of Hellfire, we usually think of disbelievers. But here, the contrast is between those who comply with Allah's commands and those who don't. This echoes the famous hadith of the Prophet ﷺ: "All of you will enter Paradise except those who refuse." The Companions (RA) asked in confusion, "Who would refuse, O Messenger of Allah?" He replied ﷺ: "Whoever obeys me enters Paradise, and whoever disobeys me has refused."

Paradise and Hellfire are tied directly to obedience. That's the lesson here.

Notice another difference: for the one who obeys, the plural form khalidina indicates companionship in Paradise. But for the one who disobeys, the singular خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا is used—"he will abide therein" alone. This is solitary confinement, the worst kind of imprisonment. Beyond the physical torture, there's the psychological torture of knowing that no one else is suffering with you. You're utterly alone in your punishment.

To understand this contrast, imagine if a teacher were upset with an entire class versus singling out just one student. The experience of being singled out is far more crushing. That's what خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا conveys—isolation in punishment.

Then the ayah adds: وَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ—"and for him is humiliating punishment." This is striking because when you're alone, you can't be humiliated, right? Humiliation requires an audience. Yet even in that solitude, there will be humiliation. The punishment of Hellfire is complete—physical, psychological, and social.

translation: These are the limits [set by] Allah, and whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger will be admitted by Him to gardens [in Paradise] under which rivers flow, abiding eternally therein; and that is the great attainment
And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger and transgresses His limits - He will put him into the Fire to abide eternally therein, and he will have a humiliating punishment

Those who commit unlawful sexual intercourse of your women - bring against them four [witnesses] from among you. And if they testify, confine the guilty women to houses until death takes them or Allah ordains for them [another] way
commentary:
وَٱلَّـٰتِى يَأْتِينَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مِن نِّسَآئِكُمْ فَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنكُمْ ۖ فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْبُيُوتِ حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّىٰهُنَّ ٱلْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا

This ayah addresses الْفَـٰحِشَة —shameless, indecent behavior among women. The Quran doesn't explicitly name *zina* (adultery/fornication) here because that subject will be addressed in more detail later. For now, it's simply called "the act of shamelessness," and everyone understood what was meant.

"Then seek against them four witnesses from among you." Four Muslim witnesses who saw the act itself. How possible is that? It's nearly impossible by design, and that's precisely the point.

This requirement serves multiple purposes. First, if such behavior is going to occur in society—and it's a reality in any society, Muslim or otherwise—then it must remain hidden. Once it comes out into the open and becomes public, it becomes normalized. And once it becomes the norm, there's no end to the corruption that will follow.

Our modern world is a case study in this progression. The sexual revolution of the 1960s and the perpetuation of promiscuity through entertainment media have normalized behaviors that were once universally condemned. The result? Society has had to completely redefine right and wrong. Now anthropology and sociology professors teach that marriage is merely a "human construct," that in nature there are no restrictions, that animals simply do what feels good—and therefore so should we.

This is where unchecked fahishah leads: to the denial of human dignity, the reduction of people to their base impulses, the argument that we're just evolved animals with no souls, no accountability, no higher purpose. These are the consequences of allowing shamelessness to become public and normal.

The second benefit of requiring four witnesses is that it eliminates the culture of scandal and accusation. No more "I heard that so-and-so did such-and-such." No more rumors destroying reputations and families. This is critically important because these ayahs were revealed before the incident of *al-ifk*—the false accusation against Aisha (RA). The instruction not to spread scandals was already in place, which makes what happened later even more serious.

For our communities today, this means: if you see a brother from the MSA with a sister at the mall or movie theater, the benefit of the doubt is that she's his sister. Don't take a picture, don't spread it on social media, don't create a scandal. We cover these matters; we deal with them at an individual level if necessary, but we don't humiliate people publicly. Why? Because humiliating them means humiliating their families. And we don't do that. This goes back to the concept of covering others' faults—*satr*.

There's also a problem in some circles where speakers, trying to make their lectures more impactful, will describe in detail the sins that "youth are up to nowadays." But sharing explicit details from the pulpit doesn't help. What helps is prevention, guidance, general warnings. Explicit details belong in private counseling sessions, not in public speeches where they can normalize or trivialize serious matters.

These matters should never be trivialized. Unfortunately, we find Muslims today making jokes about homosexuality, about zina, using crude language casually. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that what comes out of your mouth is an indication of what's in your heart. If these are the jokes constantly coming out of you, it reveals what you've filled your heart with. If you're disturbed by such talk, that means your heart is still alive, your shame is still intact. We must protect that.

We've been deceived into thinking that the only kind of humor is filthy humor. But there's plenty of good, clean fun that requires a higher intellect. Animal-like humor is base and degrading. We shouldn't endorse it, watch it, or share it—no matter how "funny" the clip might be.

This has practical implications. Family Guy, South Park, and similar shows that mock the sacred—these shouldn't be part of our entertainment. "But this one clip is hilarious!" No. These are the same shows that mock prophets. We get outraged when they mock the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, but what about when they mock Ibrahim (AS)? What about when they depict Allah ﷻ as a cartoon character? What about when they constantly mock Isa (AS)? These are all our prophets, all honored before Allah ﷻ, and we must honor them.

If a show mocked our mother, our sister, our wife, we'd never watch it again. Yet when they mock Ibrahim (AS), Musa (AS), or our Master ﷺ, we'll watch it for a laugh? How much is a laugh worth to us?

Now, back to the text itself: "If they testify, then keep them [the women] confined to their homes until death takes them or Allah ordains for them another way." The word فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ means to hold back something you already possess—in this case, house arrest. This was the early ruling: women found guilty of fahishah through proper testimony were to be kept under house arrest until death.

But then comes the crucial phrase: أَوْ يَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا—"or until Allah makes for them another way." This indicates that further revelation would come with updated instructions. These were the early stages of social discipline, marital discipline, discipline in relationships between men and women. This was the starting point for a community being trained in how to behave according to divine standards.

For us today, this is a reminder that we're often back at square one. We have remnants of the Ummah's dignity and values, but much has been washed away by the dominant culture around us. We have to bring these standards back, revitalize them in our homes and communities, and teach ourselves and our children what it means to carry ourselves with Islamic dignity in a world that has lost its shame.

translation: And the two who commit it among you, dishonor them both. But if they repent and correct themselves, leave them alone. Indeed, Allah is ever Accepting of repentance and Merciful
commentary:
وَٱلَّذَانِ يَأْتِيَـٰنِهَا مِنكُمْ فَـَٔاذُوهُمَا ۖ فَإِن تَابَا وَأَصْلَحَا فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ تَوَّابًا رَّحِيمًا

This ayah addresses a second case, distinct from the previous one. While the earlier ruling concerned a woman who committed an immoral act with a non-Muslim man, this ayah deals with a situation where both parties are Muslim - والذان يأتيانها منكم (and the two who commit it from among you).

The prescribed response is فآذوهما (then cause them pain). The language here is deliberately left ambiguous regarding the exact form of punishment, whether it involves physical reprimand, public humiliation, or other corrective measures. This ambiguity allows for appropriate judgment based on circumstances. These were early rulings in Islamic law, when the community was still being disciplined and shaped.

However, the ayah immediately follows with a crucial provision: فإن تابا وأصلحا فأعرضوا عنهما (but if they both repent and correct their behavior, then leave them alone). Once they have been reprimanded and have genuinely turned back to Allah ﷻ, the matter is closed. The community must move forward. There were no severe corporal punishments at this early stage - no amputation, no whipping. After the penalty was served and repentance was made, they were to be left in peace.

The ayah concludes with the profound reminder: إن الله كان توابا رحيما (Indeed, Allah has always been accepting of repentance and Merciful). These two divine names - At-Tawwab and Ar-Rahim - frame the entire discussion, reminding us that while sin must be addressed, the door to Allah's mercy remains open.

The spirit of this text carries important lessons for our contemporary context. Many young Muslims today lack fundamental Islamic education about modesty and boundaries, often due to the entertainment they're exposed to, the language they hear, and the environments they navigate in schools and social settings. When serious mistakes occur later in life, our response must balance accountability with compassion. We must help people understand the gravity of their actions while also providing a path back to spiritual health.

This is not about trivializing major sins - these are indeed serious transgressions. However, ostracizing people permanently serves no purpose. Many young Muslims have made significant mistakes and only later come to fully understand what they've done. For them, the door to tawbah must remain open and visible.

translation: The repentance accepted by Allah is only for those who do wrong in ignorance [or carelessness] and then repent soon after. It is those to whom Allah will turn in forgiveness, and Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
إِنَّمَا ٱلتَّوْبَةُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِن قَرِيبٍ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

Allah ﷻ now clarifies the conditions under which tawbah is truly accepted: إنما التوبة على الله (repentance is only mandated upon Allah) - meaning Allah ﷻ has obligated Himself to accept the repentance - للذين يعملون السوء بجهالة (for those who do wrong deeds in a state of ignorance).

The word جهالة here requires careful understanding. The ayah uses يعملون, which indicates they knowingly did the deed - they were aware, using their faculties. So this "ignorance" doesn't mean they didn't know it was wrong. Rather, it means they were overwhelmed by emotion, desire, or circumstance. Their hormones, temptations, or the heat of the moment overtook their better judgment. They knew it was haram, but they couldn't help themselves in that moment. This is the human condition - we know better, yet we sometimes fail.

The critical phrase is ثم يتوبون من قريب (then they repent soon after). They don't persist in the sin, they don't justify it, they don't celebrate it. Soon after committing it, they turn back to Allah ﷻ with genuine remorse. فأولئك يتوب الله عليهم (those are the ones whose repentance Allah will accept).

Notice the word السوء is used here - something hideous and ugly that scars the person who commits it. The previous ayah used the singular form, while here the emphasis is on the individual wrong deed that burdens the conscience. This sin weighs on them, and that weight drives them back to Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes: وكان الله عليما حكيما (and Allah has always been All-Knowing, All-Wise). Allah ﷻ knows what He's doing in accepting such repentance. When we wonder how Allah ﷻ could forgive such serious transgressions, we must remember that He is Al-Aleem and Al-Hakeem. The matter is between the servant and their Lord, and His wisdom encompasses what our limited understanding cannot grasp.

translation: But repentance is not [accepted] of those who [continue to] do evil deeds up until, when death comes to one of them, he says, "Indeed, I have repented now," or of those who die while they are disbelievers. For them We have prepared a painful punishment
commentary:
وَلَيْسَتِ ٱلتَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ قَالَ إِنِّى تُبْتُ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ وَلَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

Having established whose repentance is accepted, Allah ﷻ now clarifies whose repentance is rejected: وليست التوبة للذين يعملون السيئات (but repentance is not acceptable for those who continue to do evil deeds).

Note the shift - here the word السيئات is in the plural form, indicating persistent, repeated sins. The previous ayah spoke of someone who commits a wrong deed and quickly repents. This ayah addresses those who become addicted to sin, who keep doing it repeatedly, yet reassure themselves with thoughts like, "At least tomorrow is Friday," or "Ramadan is coming soon, I'll repent then."

This continues حتى إذا حضر أحدهم الموت (until death approaches one of them). Imagine the scene: you're on a plane, engrossed in something inappropriate on your device, and suddenly severe turbulence hits. The oxygen masks drop, the flight attendants are shouting emergency procedures, people are screaming, and you're bracing for an emergency landing. In that moment of terror, what happens? The headphones come off, the device is put away, and suddenly: إني تبت الآن (I repent now! I'll never do this again! I'll break this device as soon as we land!).

Then the plane stabilizes. "False alarm, folks. Sorry about that." The masks go back up, everyone relaxes, and... where were you? Oh yes, let me just unpause and continue. This is the deathbed repentance that holds no weight.

The ayah continues: ولا الذين يموتون وهم كفار (nor those who die while they are disbelievers). The word كفار can mean those who die in a state of disbelief, but it also encompasses those who die in a state of complete ingratitude to Allah ﷻ, having rejected His guidance and bounties throughout their lives.

أولئك أعتدنا لهم عذابا أليما (Those are the ones for whom We have prepared a painful punishment). The word أعتدنا means "We have prepared" - it's already ready, specially prepared for this category of people who refused to turn to Allah ﷻ sincerely while they had time.

translation: O you who have believed, it is not lawful for you to inherit women by compulsion. And do not make difficulties for them in order to take [back] part of what you gave them unless they commit a clear immorality. And live with them in kindness. For if you dislike them - perhaps you dislike a thing and Allah makes therein much good
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَرِثُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ كَرْهًا ۖ وَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا۟ بِبَعْضِ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ ۚ وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِن كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا

The surah now transitions to address the rights of women directly, beginning with a powerful call: يا أيها الذين آمنوا (O you who believe). This is a continuation of establishing proper social laws.

لا يحل لكم أن ترثوا النساء كرها (It is not permissible for you to inherit women by force). The historical context helps illuminate this ruling. In some pre-Islamic Arab tribes, when the head of the household died leaving behind wives - perhaps two, three, or four - his son from another wife would essentially "inherit" these stepmothers. To preserve tribal cohesion and prevent women from leaving the household (taking wealth with them), the son would forcibly marry his father's widows. This practice was called نكاح المقت - the marriage of disgust. Even they recognized it as repugnant, but tribal values superseded individual dignity.

Allah ﷻ categorically prohibits this. While the immediate historical context is specific, the language is universal: don't inherit women by force, don't compel women into marriage against their will. This establishes a fundamental principle - there is no forced marriage in Islam under any circumstance. You cannot treat women as property to be signed over, arranged, or traded.

Unfortunately, Muslims have sometimes adopted practices from other cultural traditions that contradict this principle. Whether it's elaborate business arrangements sealed by marrying off children, rigid requirements to marry within specific communities or families, or forcing marriages between cousins regardless of compatibility - none of this has sanction in our deen. A son raised in the West who speaks no Urdu cannot be forced to marry a girl from the village who speaks no English, no matter whose word was given or what family honor supposedly demands.

وَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ (And do not pressure them). The word عضل means to scold, to hold back, to restrain someone through anger and force. لِتَذْهَبُوا۟ بِبَعْضِ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ (so that you may take back part of what you have given them). When a marriage ends, men are prohibited from pressuring their ex-wives to return the mahr, jewelry, furniture, or any gifts given during the marriage. "She's leaving the family, but her wardrobe stays, her jewelry stays" - this is completely forbidden.

إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ (except in the case where they commit an open, clear indecency). The exception is when the wife has done something publicly shameful and disgraceful to bring deliberate humiliation upon the family. فاحشة مبينة - both words emphasize that it must be open, public, and beyond any doubt. You cannot make accusations; it must be clearly established. Only in such extreme cases might there be grounds to withhold gifts.

Now comes the transformative guidance: وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُفِ (and live with them in kindness). The word عاشروا comes from عشرة, which carries connotations of companionship, family life, intimate partnership. It's derived from عشر, the number ten, suggesting completeness and abundance. Live together as a complete family unit, in a manner that is ma'roof - recognized as good, decent, honorable.

فَإِن كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ (Then if you dislike them) - and here Allah ﷻ addresses a reality of marriage. Sometimes couples reach a point where irritation becomes chronic. Everything the spouse does becomes annoying. A simple look triggers an argument. "Don't start." "Start what?" "You always say that." "I don't always say that." "You always say 'always.'" "I never say 'always.'" "You always say 'never!'"

The fights become rehearsed scripts, replayed endlessly. But listen to what Allah ﷻ says: فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا (perhaps you dislike something, but Allah has placed abundant good in it).

This is profound. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "maybe you dislike something and there's good in it." He says Allah Himself has placed - يجعل الله - put good in it. This transforms the entire perspective. It becomes a matter of faith, not just feelings. As a believer, you're being called to recognize: yes, there are things I don't like, but Allah ﷻ has placed good here. Make a list. What good has Allah ﷻ placed in your spouse? What good has He placed in this marriage?

This isn't about giving your spouse credit - though that's also important. This is about giving Allah ﷻ credit for the good He has placed in your life. It forces reflection and gratitude. A husband might respond, "I've checked thoroughly - there's nothing good." But Allah ﷻ has already declared: I have placed good in it. Who are you to contradict your Creator?

translation: But if you want to replace one wife with another and you have given one of them a great amount [in gifts], do not take [back] from it anything. Would you take it in injustice and manifest sin
commentary:
وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمُ ٱسْتِبْدَالَ زَوْجٍ مَّكَانَ زَوْجٍ وَءَاتَيْتُمْ إِحْدَىٰهُنَّ قِنطَارًا فَلَا تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أَتَأْخُذُونَهُۥ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا

وإن أردتم استبدال زوج مكان زوج (And if you decide to replace one spouse with another) - if you've made up your mind to divorce and remarry. وآتيتم إحداهن قنطارا (and you had given one of them a qintar). A qintar represents an enormous amount of wealth - everything you owned might have been put in her name. The house, the accounts, the jewelry - all of it, because you loved her so much and planned to be together forever.

Until you didn't. Until the love turned to resentment and you started thinking, "Everything is in her name!"

فَلَا تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا (do not take from it anything). Not anything at all. Don't even touch a portion of it. The prohibition is absolute. You cannot show up one day with a bag, gathering all her jewelry, saying, "I gave this to you when I loved you. Now I don't love you, so I'm taking a love refund."

أَتَأْخُذُونَهُۥ بُهْتَـٰنًا (Would you take it through slander?) - through false accusations, lies, manipulations to justify taking it back? The word بهتان refers to a serious, damaging false claim. On the Day of Judgment, she will make this allegation against you, and it will stick. You will have no defense.

وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (and a clear sin). There's no ambiguity here, no room for interpretation or justification. مبين - openly, clearly, undeniably sinful. There is no other way of looking at it.

translation: And how could you take it while you have gone in unto each other and they have taken from you a solemn covenant
commentary:
وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُۥ وَقَدْ أَفْضَىٰ بَعْضُكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَأَخَذْنَ مِنكُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا

وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُۥ (And how could you take it?) - how dare you even consider it? The question itself expresses divine incredulity at such behavior.

وَقَدْ أَفْضَىٰ بَعْضُكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ (when you have been intimate with one another). The word أفضى comes from فضاء, meaning open space, air. It suggests letting go, being completely unguarded. أفضى إلى means to go to someone and share your breath with them, share your secrets, let yourself be completely vulnerable and exposed.

This intimacy described here is unlike any other relationship. You don't behave this way even in front of your mother. With your spouse, you're completely comfortable, completely yourself, guards down, barriers removed. This is a closeness that exists in no other human relationship.

How can you just erase all of that? How can years of intimacy, vulnerability, partnership, and shared life be reduced to nothing just because you've had conflicts or grown apart? That doesn't erase all the good that existed, all the closeness that was shared.

وَأَخَذْنَ مِنكُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (and they have taken from you a solemn covenant). Here is a beautiful and weighty description of marriage. The word ميثاق refers to a covenant where both parties are fully aware of all provisions and obligations. Both enter with complete knowledge and consent.

But notice the specific adjective: غليظا - intense, strong, unbreakable, unyielding, not budging. This exact phrase, ميثاقا غليظا, is used elsewhere in the Quran to describe the covenant Allah ﷻ took from the prophets that they would deliver His message faithfully. That same terminology is now applied to the marriage contract.

Reflect on this parallel. The covenant between Allah ﷻ and His prophets - the most serious undertaking imaginable - uses the same description as the covenant between husband and wife. This tells us how seriously marriage is taken in the sight of Allah ﷻ. This is not a casual agreement. This is not something to mess with.

Furthermore, the structure of this covenant is significant. Who is the contract in favor of, and who bears the liability? It is in favor of the wife and is a liability upon the husband. Just as when you sign a loan agreement, the contract favors the lender and places liability on the borrower, this marriage contract places responsibility squarely on the husband's shoulders.

On the Day of Judgment, it is the husband who will be questioned about this covenant. The wife holds the advantaged position in this contract - أخذن منكم (they have taken from you). They are in the position of having received a binding promise, and you are in the position of having given it.

Understanding this makes the gravity clear: How dare you take back their wealth when this covenant exists? How dare you violate the terms of an agreement that Allah ﷻ describes with the same intensity as the prophetic covenant?

translation: And do not marry those [women] whom your fathers married, except what has already occurred. Indeed, it was an immorality and hateful [to Allah] and was evil as a way
commentary:
وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ مَا نَكَحَ ءَابَآؤُكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً وَمَقْتًا وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا

وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ مَا نَكَحَ ءَابَآؤُكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ (And do not marry women whom your fathers married). This is an explicit, unambiguous prohibition. Do not marry your stepmother - a woman who was married to your father.

إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ (except what has already occurred) - meaning whatever happened before this revelation came down. Those marriages that existed in the period of ignorance are forgiven, but from this point forward, such unions are absolutely forbidden.

Then come three devastating descriptions of this practice:

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً (Indeed, it has always been an immorality) - فاحشة, something shameless, indecent, crossing all boundaries of propriety. The phrase كان (has always been) indicates this wasn't just wrong after the revelation came - it was always wrong, even when people practiced it. The human fitra has always recognized this as wrong.

وَمَقْتًا (and hateful) - مقت means something absolutely disgusting, revolting, impossible to stomach. It's the kind of disgust that makes you recoil in revulsion. Even the people practicing it knew it was maqt - detestable. But they prioritized tribal cohesion and family honor over basic human dignity.

وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا (and an evil way). سبيل means a path, a tradition, something that is followed. This was a social practice, a cultural pattern that was being perpetuated. And what an evil tradition to follow.

These ayahs address adult content, sensitive subjects that some might prefer to avoid. But the Quran doesn't shy away from addressing real problems in society. Why? Because when the fitra - the natural disposition toward goodness that Allah ﷻ placed in every human - becomes corrupted, there is no limit to the depths of evil that can result. Human beings have potential for immense good, but when that nature is twisted and corrupted beyond a certain point, unimaginable evil becomes possible.

This is why Islamic law must address not just the ideal but also the problematic. Laws aren't written for people who naturally do the right thing - they're written to restrain those who don't. We need boundaries, limits, clear prohibitions. The Quran establishes these boundaries not to restrict our freedom but to protect our dignity, preserve our families, and safeguard our communities from the corruption that humans are capable of when we abandon divine guidance.

These ayahs - from laws about repentance to laws about marriage, from rights of women to prohibitions of shameful practices - form a cohesive framework. They acknowledge human weakness while calling us toward excellence. They address our mistakes while keeping the door to mercy open. They establish rights while emphasizing responsibilities. This is the balance of Quranic guidance - realistic about human nature, yet uncompromising in its call to rise above our base inclinations and live according to the dignity Allah ﷻ has granted us.

translation: Prohibited to you [for marriage] are your mothers, your daughters, your sisters, your father's sisters, your mother's sisters, your brother's daughters, your sister's daughters, your [milk] mothers who nursed you, your sisters through nursing, your wives' mothers, and your step-daughters under your guardianship [born] of your wives unto whom you have gone in. But if you have not gone in unto them, there is no sin upon you. And [also prohibited are] the wives of your sons who are from your [own] loins, and that you take [in marriage] two sisters simultaneously, except for what has already occurred. Indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهَـٰتُكُمْ وَبَنَاتُكُمْ وَأَخَوَٰتُكُمْ وَعَمَّـٰتُكُمْ وَخَـٰلَـٰتُكُمْ وَبَنَاتُ ٱلْأَخِ وَبَنَاتُ ٱلْأُخْتِ وَأُمَّهَـٰتُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ وَأَخَوَٰتُكُم مِّنَ ٱلرَّضَـٰعَةِ وَأُمَّهَـٰتُ نِسَآئِكُمْ وَرَبَـٰٓئِبُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِى فِى حُجُورِكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِى دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ فَإِن لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَحَلَـٰٓئِلُ أَبْنَآئِكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلَـٰبِكُمْ وَأَن تَجْمَعُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلْأُخْتَيْنِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

This ayah establishes clear boundaries within family relationships, defining who is permanently forbidden in marriage. The Arabic language provides simple, elegant terms for these relationships. Your mothers (أُمَّهَـٰتُكُمْ), daughters (بَنَاتُكُمْ), and sisters (أَخَوَٰتُكُمْ) are forbidden. The word عَمَّـٰتُكُمْ refers to your paternal aunts—your father's sisters—while خَـٰلَـٰتُكُمْ refers to your maternal aunts—your mother's sisters. These aunts hold a station similar to your mother, deserving the same respect and care you would give to your own mother.

The ayah continues with بَنَاتُ ٱلْأَخِ (the daughters of your brother) and بَنَاتُ ٱلْأُخْتِ (the daughters of your sister)—in other words, your nieces from both sides.

A remarkable extension of these prohibitions comes through the institution of breastfeeding.
