The women who nursed you (أُمَّهَـٰتُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ) become like mothers to you, and their daughters become your sisters through nursing (أَخَوَٰتُكُم مِّنَ ٱلرَّضَـٰعَةِ). When a woman breastfeeds a child, she forms a maternal bond recognized by Islamic law, creating permanent prohibitions just as blood relations do.

The ayah addresses mothers-in-law (أُمَّهَـٰتُ نِسَآئِكُمْ)—the mothers of your wives—establishing them as permanently forbidden. The term رَبَـٰٓئِبُكُمُ refers to stepdaughters, specifically those who live in your household (ٱلَّـٰتِى فِى حُجُورِكُم), from wives with whom you have consummated the marriage (ٱلَّـٰتِى دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ). However, Allah ﷻ provides a clarification: if you married a woman but did not consummate the marriage and then divorced, there is no prohibition on you marrying her daughters from a previous marriage.

The phrase حَلَـٰٓئِلُ أَبْنَآئِكُمُ refers to the wives of your sons, specifically those who are from your actual lineage (ٱلَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلَـٰبِكُمْ)—from your genes, your biological sons. This distinction is important because sometimes people call others "son" as a term of affection or adoption, but this ruling applies to biological sons only.

Finally, the ayah forbids combining two sisters in marriage simultaneously (وَأَن تَجْمَعُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلْأُخْتَيْنِ). The exception إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ (except what has already passed) refers to practices before this revelation. Whatever happened in the past before this law was established is forgiven. The ayah concludes with the beautiful reminder that Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful (غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا).

These are the permanently forbidden relationships. The ayah that follows addresses temporary prohibitions.

translation: And [also prohibited to you are all] married women except those your right hands possess. [This is] the decree of Allah upon you. And lawful to you are [all others] beyond these, [provided] that you seek them [in marriage] with [gifts from] your property, desiring chastity, not unlawful sexual intercourse. So for whatever you enjoy [of marriage] from them, give them their due compensation as an obligation. And there is no blame upon you for what you mutually agree to beyond the obligation. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
۞ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۖ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَأُحِلَّ لَكُم مَّا وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُم مُّحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ ۚ فَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْهُنَّ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا تَرَٰضَيْتُم بِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْفَرِيضَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

The word الْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ has significant meaning in this context. It refers to women who are "protected"—protected within the fortress of marriage or within the protection of a decent household. These are married women, and they are forbidden to you. The exception إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ (except what your right hands possess) refers to a specific historical context where women were taken as captives in war, some of whom were previously married. Their previous marriages were not recognized, and they entered into new relationships within Muslim households. This was the practice in that era.

The phrase كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ (This is the decree of Allah upon you) emphasizes that these are divine mandates, not cultural preferences. Then Allah ﷻ opens the door of permission: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُم مَّا وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكُمْ (And lawful to you are all beyond that)—meaning any woman besides those who are forbidden is potentially permissible for marriage.

The crucial condition follows: أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُم (that you seek them with your wealth). This establishes that marriage requires financial capability. You must have the means to support a household. The word مُّحْصِنِينَ describes the state you should be in when seeking marriage—you should be someone who wants to establish a decent, protected household, a family built on respectability.

Then comes the critical contrast: غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ (not as those who commit unlawful sexual relations). The word مُسَـٰفِحِينَ comes from the root related to سَفْح, which means to pour out or spill. It refers to someone whose only concern is fulfilling their lust, pouring out their desires without restraint or responsibility. This is not the Islamic model of marriage.

Some young people mistakenly quote examples from the time of the Companions (RA), where minimal mahr was given—one Companion gave dates as mahr, another's wife memorized a surah as her mahr. But we must understand the context. Those Companions (RA) were living through the mission of establishing Islam alongside the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Everything else in their lives was secondary to that overwhelming priority. They had given up everything for the sake of the Deen. Their situation cannot be simplistically compared to ours. That's why Allah ﷻ establishes in this ayah a principle that applies for all time: you must pursue marriage بِأَمْوَٰلِكُم (with your wealth), meaning you need financial means.

This addresses a critical issue in our times. Some young people feel they must get married immediately due to hormonal urges, but hormones alone are not a sign of maturity or readiness for marriage. Allah ﷻ earlier commanded in this surah to test the orphans until they reach marriage age, and if you find in them rushd (maturity and sound judgment), then release their wealth to them. Marriage age isn't just about physical maturity—it's about having the wisdom and capability to manage a household.

In many Muslim communities, particularly in suburban America, young people mature very late. Their tuition is paid, their rooms are cleaned by their mothers, their meals are prepared for them. They live comfortable lives where everything is taken care of. Then they think they're ready for marriage simply because they've reached a certain age or because they have desires. But marriage requires the ability to run a household, to take responsibility, to be self-sufficient. If someone hasn't learned to manage their own living space or handle basic life responsibilities, they're not ready for the far greater responsibility of marriage.

This isn't to say that young people can never be mature enough to marry—some certainly are. But it must be evaluated on a case-by-case basis. Parents must have honest conversations with their children about readiness for marriage. We live in times of unprecedented temptation and easy access to haram. College years can be particularly challenging. We cannot simply delay marriage until someone becomes a doctor with graying hair, nor can we rush into it without genuine maturity. The balance lies in honest assessment of readiness—financial, emotional, and spiritual.

The destructive pattern of instant gratification in our society makes this even more challenging. When people become conditioned to immediately fulfilling every craving—whether for food, entertainment, or anything else—they lose the capacity for sabr (patience and restraint). Marriage to a human being is not like accessing a device. Your spouse has their own feelings, needs, and timing. If someone has been conditioned to expect immediate fulfillment of every desire, they will struggle immensely in marriage. One of the defining qualities of a believer is sabr—the ability to exercise restraint and patience. This quality is essential for a successful marriage.

The ayah continues: فَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْهُنَّ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً (And for the enjoyment you have from them, give them their compensation as an obligation). The mahr is not optional or negotiable—it's a divine obligation. Then Allah ﷻ shows flexibility: وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا تَرَٰضَيْتُم بِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْفَرِيضَةِ (And there is no blame upon you for what you mutually agree upon beyond the obligation). If both parties agree to give or receive more than what was initially set, that is permissible.

The ayah closes with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise)—He ﷻ knows what is best for His creation and has legislated these laws with perfect wisdom.

translation: And whoever among you cannot [find] the means to marry free, believing women, then [he may marry] from those whom your right hands possess of believing slave girls. And Allah is most knowing about your faith. You [believers] are of one another. So marry them with the permission of their people and give them their due compensation according to what is acceptable. [They should be] chaste, neither [of] those who commit unlawful intercourse randomly nor those who take [secret] lovers. But once they are sheltered in marriage, if they should commit adultery, then for them is half the punishment for free [unmarried] women. This [allowance] is for him among you who fears sin, but to be patient is better for you. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنكُمْ طَوْلًا أَن يَنكِحَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ فَمِن مَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُم مِّن فَتَيَـٰتِكُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِكُم ۚ بَعْضُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۚ فَٱنكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ وَءَاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ مُحْصَنَـٰتٍ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحَـٰتٍ وَلَا مُتَّخِذَٰتِ أَخْدَانٍ ۚ فَإِذَآ أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ فَعَلَيْهِنَّ نِصْفُ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِىَ ٱلْعَنَتَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَأَن تَصْبِرُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

This ayah addresses those who lack the financial capacity for marriage. The word طَوْلًا comes from a root meaning expanse or height, and here it refers to having the means—financial, social, or physical capacity. The primary meaning in this context is financial capability.

The ayah presents two tiers of marriage options. The first and preferred option is to marry الْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ (protected, believing women from decent families)—women who come from established Muslim households, who were raised with Islamic values and a sense of propriety. If a man wants to marry such a woman but they ask for a reasonable mahr that he cannot afford, then Allah ﷻ provides a second option.

The alternative is مِّن مَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُم مِّن فَتَيَـٰتِكُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ (from what your right hands possess among your believing young women). In the historical context, these were women who had been taken into Muslim households, either as captives or through other means, and had accepted Islam. They might not have come from established families with Islamic upbringing.

Why was there a distinction between these two categories? Women who grew up in decent, structured households—even in pre-Islamic Arabian society—had been conditioned with certain standards of behavior and modesty. They had a sense of shame and social expectation, even if wrongdoing sometimes occurred behind closed doors. In contrast, women from backgrounds where there were no clear moral boundaries regarding relationships—essentially a dating culture—had not received this conditioning. Since one of the primary purposes of marriage in Islam is to protect one's chastity and honor, women from families with that training were naturally better suited for this role.

However, Allah ﷻ immediately elevates the dignity of all believing women: وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِكُم (And Allah is most knowing of your faith). Don't assume you know the state of someone's iman based on their background. Then comes the powerful statement: بَعْضُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ (You are all from one another). Despite different social backgrounds, all believers are equal before Allah ﷻ. They are all descendants of Adam (AS). No one is inherently superior.

The instruction for marrying women of the second category is noteworthy: فَٱنكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ (Then marry them with the permission of their families). Allah ﷻ doesn't say "with the permission of their owners" or "masters"—He says their families. This linguistic choice immediately honors these women by recognizing them as part of a family structure, not merely as property.

The command وَءَاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ (and give them their compensation in a good manner) establishes that these women also deserve mahr, and it must be given in a respectable way—not as a discounted amount, but as something appropriate and honorable.

The description مُحْصَنَـٰتٍ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحَـٰتٍ وَلَا مُتَّخِذَٰتِ أَخْدَانٍ means these women should be seeking chaste marriage, not illicit relationships or secret affairs. When they enter into marriage, they are entering into a protected relationship.

Then comes a significant legal distinction: فَإِذَآ أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ فَعَلَيْهِنَّ نِصْفُ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ (But once they are sheltered in marriage, if they commit immorality, then for them is half the punishment of free women). If these women, who are new to Islamic values and social structures, commit adultery after marriage, their punishment is half of what would be given to women who were raised with these values. Why? Because women from established families who commit such acts have crossed many more internal barriers to do so—they knew it was wrong from their earliest conditioning. Women new to these concepts are still learning and adjusting. It's still wrong, but there's recognition of their journey toward understanding.

The ayah specifies: ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِىَ ٱلْعَنَتَ مِنكُمْ (This is for those among you who fear harm). This second option—marrying women without established family backgrounds—should only be pursued if a man genuinely fears for his own character and ability to remain chaste. If the struggle is so intense that it threatens his moral standing, then this option exists. But it's clearly the second choice.

The ayah concludes with powerful guidance: وَأَن تَصْبِرُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (But if you are patient, it is better for you). If you can exercise sabr and wait for the right spouse, for better circumstances, for greater maturity and readiness—that patience is superior. This patience isn't merely waiting passively; it's actively developing yourself, building your financial capability, strengthening your character, and preparing properly for the responsibility of marriage.

Finally, وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful). Whatever mistakes have been made in the past, whatever struggles you face with desire and temptation, Allah ﷻ is ready to forgive. His mercy encompasses all who turn to Him sincerely, seeking to live according to His guidance.

translation: Allah wants to make clear to you [the lawful from the unlawful] and guide you to the [good] practices of those before you and to accept your repentance. And Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَيَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

After addressing marriage laws and social ethics, the Quran now shifts to universal teachings that answer a fundamental question many believers ask: What does Allah ﷻ truly want from us? These ayahs provide a comprehensive response to that question, dedicating three full verses to exploring what Allah ﷻ desires for humanity.

The ayah begins with يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ - "Allah intends." The structure here is significant because the لِ indicates there is another implied verb. Allah ﷻ intends—by means of this revelation, by means of these instructions, by means of sending us a messenger—to accomplish something specific. He gave us all of this in order to clarify for you.

The first purpose is clarification itself: لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ - that He may make clear to you. Allah ﷻ wants us to have clarity, to understand, to see things as they truly are without confusion or doubt.

The second purpose follows: وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ - "and that He may guide you through the pathways and the legacies of those who came much before you." This is profound. Allah ﷻ is teaching us that guidance comes not just through direct instruction, but through learning from history. Previous nations received instructions too, and we can learn from how they responded to those instructions. We learn to avoid the traps they fell into and to follow in the footsteps of those who did well.

Then comes the most beautiful part: وَيَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ - "and He intends to accept your repentance." This is what Allah ﷻ wants. He wants you to be qualified for tawbah. Notice that this comes after all the discussion of violations, of Zina, of transgressing boundaries. Even after all of that, Allah ﷻ doesn't want you to lose hope. He wants you to be qualified for tawbah.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ - "and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise." He knows our struggles, our weaknesses, our circumstances, and His wisdom encompasses all of creation. His laws reflect both His perfect knowledge and His perfect wisdom.

translation: Allah wants to accept your repentance, but those who follow [their] passions want you to digress [into] a great deviation
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ أَن تَمِيلُوا۟ مَيْلًا عَظِيمًا

Now Allah ﷻ repeats the message but with a crucial emphasis. وَٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ - "Allah, in fact, is the one who intends that He accept your repentance." By mentioning Allah's name first this time, making it the subject at the beginning of the sentence, there's an emphasis being made. This is a reality check: Allah ﷻ really does want to accept your repentance. He truly desires this for you.

But then comes the contrast: وَيُرِيدُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ - "and those who follow their desires, their temptations." Note the word used here: الشَّهَوَٰتِ, not just desires but strong temptations. This is هَوَىٰ intensified - not just any temptation, but powerful, overwhelming ones.

These people who follow their strong temptations, what do they want? أَن تَمِيلُوا۟ مَيْلًا عَظِيمًا - "that you incline totally, that you become completely tilted away from the truth, that you distance yourself from what is right."

This contrast is crucial to understand. Allah ﷻ is not talking here about a division between believers and disbelievers. This is a contrast between elements within the believing community itself. There will always be those who want to obey Allah ﷻ and His Messenger, and there will be an element who, when they don't obey Allah ﷻ and the Messenger, what do they obey instead? They obey their temptations.

There will always be a rank and element within the Muslim Ummah whose biggest priority is to fulfill what they want. Their wants come before everything else. And when it comes to the laws of Allah ﷻ, all they want is for you to incline just a little bit, to bend just a little, to move over slightly, to show some compromise, to demonstrate some leniency.

The Quran deals with explicit instructions. On one hand, we shouldn't try to apply them without consulting someone knowledgeable in fiqh who understands how these matters apply to our personal situations. But on the other hand, there's another dangerous scenario: constantly listening to Allah's instructions while searching for reasons why they don't apply to us. Finding contexts where we become the exception. Looking for scenarios where this is no longer applicable to us personally, to our family, to our community.

That's not the attitude of a Muslim. We're not looking for reasons to not apply the text. We're trying to find ways to apply the text. We've accepted this as valid guidance. The entire spirit of that mentality is violated when we're constantly thinking, "How do we make this not applicable to us? How do we become the exception to what's being said?"

translation: And Allah wants to lighten for you [your difficulties]; and mankind was created weak
commentary:
يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ ۚ وَخُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ضَعِيفًا

After two ayahs describing what Allah ﷻ wants, now comes the summary: يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ (Yureedu Allahu an yukhaffifa 'ankum) - "Allah intends to lighten your burden from you."

This is transformative. Instructions have come, and we might think they're going to place a burden on us. But later in the Quran, we'll read something beautiful about how Allah ﷻ came to remove the chains, those heavy chains with huge links where even a single link is too heavy to carry. These chains were placed on top of people because of their cultures, because of their established practices. Allah ﷻ brought the law to remove those things from them.

The purpose of Islamic law, the purpose of Sharia, the purpose of these instructions is to make life easier. Consider this: we want to do weddings our way, but there's a Sunnah way of performing weddings. We want to earn money our way, but there's a halal way of earning money according to Islamic guidelines. When you follow your way in violation of Islamic principles, you'll find your life becoming difficult. Your existence becomes constricted and uncomfortable. You'll encounter all kinds of problems.

But follow the simple way, follow the way of Sharia, and life becomes easier. Take the common example of extravagant weddings. These have become a major problem that sets marriages on the wrong foot from the beginning. We violate multiple rules of Islam in conducting them—angels wouldn't even be found in the same area because of the kinds of weddings we organize. Forget barakah, forget any blessing at such weddings.

These celebrations become so overpriced, so extravagant, so over the top—just because your cousin had an impressive wedding, you feel you have to outdo them. Then the next person comes along and has to outdo what you did, and so it continues. It creates a culture of competition where one after another, families must compete with those who came before them. This manufactures a culture of oppression.

People feel they're not decent unless they overspend. These are the chains placed on top of people. Consider how many families put themselves in financial and emotional difficulty, all wondering, "What are people going to say?" Just because of that one phrase: "What will people say? What will your aunt say? What will your cousin say? What will those people say?" Just because of reputation. The Deen came to remove us from all that burden. All you have to worry about is what Allah ﷻ will say. Everything else will be fine.

Then comes the explanation: وَخُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ضَعِيفًا - "and the human being was created weak."

It's beautiful that Allah ﷻ mentions lightening the burden immediately followed by mentioning weakness. Why? Because a weak person can't carry a heavy load. The parallel is intentional: you're weak, you can't hold much, so I made sure I gave you something you can handle, something easy for you.

This continues what Allah ﷻ told us at the end of Surat Al-Baqarah: لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا - "Allah does not burden any person beyond what they can bear, beyond their capacity." Everything He has commanded is within our capacity, and the purpose is to make life easier for us—if only we could see it that way.

We tend to think of someone who tries to abide by the rules of Islam as overly strict. You hear terms like "That's a strict Muslim" or "They're a little too Muslim." But this entire idea is the exact opposite of what Allah ﷻ wants us to think. That person is actually making their life easy. They know what it means to live a simple, comfortable life. Our understanding needs to shift toward recognizing that following Allah's guidance makes life easier, not harder.

translation: O you who have believed, do not consume one another's wealth unjustly but only [in lawful] business by mutual consent. And do not kill yourselves [or one another]. Indeed, Allah is to you ever Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا

Now we transition from social laws to economic principles. Economics has already been mentioned in this surah—the prohibition against consuming the wealth of orphans in sneaky ways, and the importance of delivering inheritance money to rightful recipients. These economic hints have been building up throughout the surah because these two things go hand in hand: a healthy society and a healthy economy, an ethical economy.

When I say "healthy economy," I don't mean rising stock market indices. I mean an ethical economy where transactions happen based on mutual appreciation of justice, where both buyer and seller are interested in maintaining certain ethical standards before they engage in their transactions.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - "O you who have believed," لَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ - "don't consume your wealth among yourselves using falsehood." Don't use lies, don't use cunning tactics, don't engage in false advertising. Don't sell people defective goods while pretending they're perfect. Don't use bait-and-switch schemes. Don't hide important information in fine print.

Modern forms of falsehood include using so much legal terminology in contracts that no human being can understand them. Even lawyers would have to discuss it with judges to understand what the contract actually says. If you've ever looked at the fine print in credit card agreements, it's eight pages of text designed to protect the company while trapping you. Even if you know English well, you won't fully understand what it's saying. You could sit there and try to read it before filling out the form, but you'll get a headache more than anything else.

This is also a modern form of باطِل. It's a new form of one party interested in protecting themselves at the expense of the other. Credit card companies set up tables on college campuses, offering free candy if you sign up. Students have ten minutes between classes—nobody's going to read the fine print. This is clearly designed to trap people.

The only acceptable kind of transaction? إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنكُمْ - "except that it be a trade based on mutual agreement from both of you." The word تَرَاضٍ doesn't just mean agreement; it means both parties are pleased, both are happy. The buyer is happy he bought it, the seller is happy he sold it. They're both content with the transaction.

Then comes a statement that seems sudden: وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ - "and don't kill yourselves." What's the connection? When business goes bad, the economy suffers. When the economy suffers, what comes next in society? Crime. Crime is directly related to the economic situation of any society. The toughest neighborhoods are the violent crime neighborhoods—this is a documented fact. The most violent places in the world are also the most economically struggling places in the world. One leads to the other.

Corruption emerges when the haves take everything and forget about the have-nots. The have-nots will resort to desperate measures, and crime is born. When economic difficulty creates crime, people ask, "Why is Allah ﷻ letting this happen? Why are grocery prices going up? Why are gas prices increasing?" In struggling parts of the world, people sometimes pay three or four times what we pay for the same groceries. Their expenses are way more than ours while they earn way less. This economic disparity can make people desperate, asking, "How can Allah ﷻ want this for people? Why would He allow this?"

Allah ﷻ answers at the end: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا - "Indeed, Allah has always been merciful to you." This is not about Allah's mercy failing. This is about us not sticking to principles. When we abandon justice and fairness in our economic dealings, these consequences follow.

translation: And whoever does that in aggression and injustice - then We will drive him into a Fire. And that, for Allah, is [always] easy
commentary:
وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ عُدْوَٰنًا وَظُلْمًا فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ نَارًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا

وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ عُدْوَٰنًا وَظُلْمًا - "And whoever does this"—cheats in business, murders out of animosity, wrongs others—فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ نَارًا - "soon We will cast him into a fire."

Then comes a sobering reality: وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا - "and that is very easy upon Allah." 

In the world, you might have a lenient judge. You could appeal to him, ask him to go easy on the criminal, and he might show mercy. But don't assume you'll manipulate Allah's mercy when you've been cheating people and violating their rights. The criminal who is Muslim might think, "I've done Hajj three times already. Yes, I might be thrown into Hell, but I'll pull out my Hajj card. Allah ﷻ will take it easy on me."

This ayah warns: it's very easy for Allah ﷻ to throw such a person into Hellfire and not listen to any of his excuses. Don't assume you can play games with Allah's mercy while deliberately harming others.

translation: If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remove from you your lesser sins and admit you to a noble entrance [into Paradise]
commentary:
إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًا كَرِيمًا

This ayah introduces a fundamental concept in our religion: a sense of proportion between major and minor sins. This subject appears three times in the Quran—here, in Surat Ash-Shura, and in Surat An-Najm. Understanding this proportion is critical for spiritual development.

إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ - "If you avoid the major sins of what you've been forbidden from."

The fundamental idea is this: in our religion, there are big things we must absolutely stay away from. Cheating in business, lying, riba (interest), violating the rights of parents, abandoning the prayers, neglecting Zakah—these are fundamentals. There are no ifs, ands or buts about them. You have to stay away from the major prohibitions; you have to fulfill the major obligations.

Then there are things that diminish us—character flaws we shouldn't have because they make us lesser people. But these are minor in comparison. Maybe once in a while something slips out without realizing it. Maybe a small white lie here and there to get out of trouble. Maybe someone didn't make their wudu properly because they were in a hurry. Maybe someone wasn't paying full attention in Salah. Maybe somebody didn't lower their gaze right away when they saw something inappropriate, and they were tempted to look again. These are things that can happen on a daily basis.

There's a sense of proportion here. The first priority in a Muslim's life is to take care of the major problems. Guard yourself from the major sins so well that you don't even think about them anymore. They're so far removed from your life that they're not a daily concern. Once those constants are established and you've protected yourself from the major violations, what can you concern yourself with? The smaller mistakes that are more easily committed. Now you can focus your attention on those. This is a healthy, proper sense of proportion.

But what happens when there's a corrupted sense of proportion? A person won't give their daughter her rightful inheritance. They won't pay their wife her mahr. They continue earning money from questionable sources. But they'll perform Hajj every year. They'll donate generously to the hifz program at the masjid. Every time they come to the masjid, they'll point out, "Your pants are too low" or "Your beard isn't long enough." They're ready to correct minor issues immediately. But the major violations right under their nose? Those slip right by.

There's an expression about a foolish hunter who spent the entire day chasing a mosquito while an elephant escaped. That's corrupted proportion. This is actually criminal psychology. When you're engaged in major sins, you don't want to face that reality, so you rationalize it away. You concern yourself with smaller matters and make them your entire religion. Meanwhile, everything major that you're violating, you conveniently forget about and never even bring up.

The ayah promises: نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ - "We will bury away from you your sins, We will remove from you your mistakes"—the day-to-day errors. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that الحسنات يذهبن السيئات (good deeds remove bad deeds). The daily acts of worship—your adhkar, your Salawat upon the Prophet ﷺ, your charity, your smile to another believer—these things will remove the smaller mistakes you're working on anyway. But the major sins (الكَبَائِر) must be dealt with first and foremost.

The ayah concludes: وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًا كَرِيمًا - "and We will enter you into an entrance that is noble." 

Notice that a sinful person is also humiliated. But Allah ﷻ says that when you stay away from major sins and work on purifying yourself from the minor ones, He will admit you into a place where the entrance itself is noble. Only noble people enter through there. The entrance itself carries dignity. This is the promise for those who maintain proper proportion in their spiritual development—focusing first on avoiding major sins, then working diligently on the smaller mistakes that are part of our daily struggle as weak human beings.

translation: And do not wish for that by which Allah has made some of you exceed others. For men is a share of what they have earned, and for women is a share of what they have earned. And ask Allah of his bounty. Indeed Allah is ever, of all things, Knowing
commentary:
وَلَا تَتَمَنَّوْا۟ مَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ ۖ وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبْنَ ۚ وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا

Allah ﷻ created each of us differently—with different heights, weights, ethnicities, races, intellectual capacities, skills and abilities. We are fundamentally different from one another by divine design. Yet most psychological problems people face—depression, sadness, aggression—stem from comparing themselves to others. Why couldn't I be taller? Why am I not as smart? How come they accomplished that and I couldn't?

This comparison happens at every level. A child memorizing Quran watches another student finish two juz in a month while they complete only half a page, and thinks, "How come I can't be like that? I hate myself." Parents often compound this problem by asking their child, "Why can't you be like them?" There's constant comparison to coworkers, peers, college students, cousins, siblings, athletes—and it becomes a real burden.

Allah ﷻ commands: **وَلَا تَتَمَنَّوْا۟ مَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ** - "Do not wish for what Allah has favored some of you over others with." Some people are exceptionally intelligent but difficult to look at. Others are very good-looking but limited in intellect. Allah ﷻ wants you not to wish for what someone else has. He wants you to be grateful for what you have. The underlying premise is clear: if you're wishing for what somebody else has, you have no appreciation for your own blessings. This directly opposes the concept of shukr (gratitude).

If there's anything worth comparing with others, it's in good deeds: **لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟** - "For men there is a portion of what they have earned." **وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبْنَ** - "And for women there is a portion of what they have earned." The real competition is in time spent doing righteous deeds. It's not about quantity—how many students sit in your class, how many people attend your lecture, how many read your book. It's about the sincere time you spend for Allah's sake during your limited adult life on this earth, the years when you have ability and haven't yet become senile. What did you accomplish with that sincere time, whether helping others, helping yourself, performing ibadah, or anything else? How did you make the best use of your time? That's the only real competition.

Young women sometimes struggle with this: "Why was I born a girl? I can't do this or that. My brother gets to do everything." Within households, husbands and wives have different roles and responsibilities. When a woman focuses on what her husband gets to do that she doesn't, jealousy and aggression can build. This isn't about abuse or neglect—it's about the natural division of responsibilities that men and women are meant to fulfill.

In our society, we're constantly told, "I can do anything. I can do everything better than you." When a young woman becomes a mother, takes leave from work or quits her job to care for her child while her husband continues working, she might think, "I want to go to work. Why can't I do that? Why don't you stay home?" Personal compromises are fine, but if the thought becomes, "I'm stuck with this. Why me? Why is this my problem and not someone else's?"—that's the problem of wishing for what another has been given and not appreciating your own blessing.

This is the ayah addressing jealousy. A son thinking, "I hate my brother. My parents always favor him. He always gets away with everything. They paid for his tuition but told me I have to work. They told me I'm not good enough but they're always patting him on the back." Even if this is true—and often it's mostly in your head—you're not here to earn favor from parents. You're here to earn favor from Allah ﷻ. The competition is only about who's doing more good deeds.

Everyone works with what they've been given. If you've been given more, you're answerable for more. If you're more intelligent, more capable, if things come easier to you, that means you could have done more with your life. You're more answerable because Allah ﷻ knows what He made each of us capable of.

Consider this: someone with only $100 donates $1. Someone with $100 million also donates $1. They both report a $1 donation, but before Allah ﷻ, are those dollars the same? Whose dollar counts for more? Whose is actually a crime? Allah ﷻ knows not just the quantity of what we give, but the quality relative to what He's made us capable of and what we must work against our own weaknesses to accomplish.

**وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ** - "Ask Allah from His favor." If you want something better, ask Allah ﷻ for better. Ask Him for His favor. **إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا** - "Indeed, Allah has always been fully aware of everything, fully knowledgeable." He knows exactly what you're going through, exactly what you're capable of, exactly what you think your problems are. He knows all of it.

translation: And for all, We have made heirs to what is left by parents and relatives. And to those whom your oaths have bound [to you] - give them their share. Indeed Allah is ever, over all things, a Witness
commentary:
وَلِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مَوَٰلِىَ مِمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَـَٔاتُوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا

**وَلِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مَوَٰلِىَ** - "For everyone We have made guardians"—those who take over **مِمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ** - "from what the parents and close relatives left behind."

**وَٱلَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ** introduces a social practice new to the Arabs: close friends. If you had a friend who wasn't financially well off and you wanted to leave something for him when you died, you could make a promise. This promise could only be made while you were still alive—you would hand something over or put it in their name, but this wasn't part of the formal inheritance. It was something agreed to beforehand.

**فَـَٔاتُوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ** - "Then give them their portion." **إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا** - "Certainly Allah has always been a witness over everything."

translation: Men are in charge of women by [right of] what Allah has given one over the other and what they spend [for maintenance] from their wealth. So righteous women are devoutly obedient, guarding in [the husband's] absence what Allah would have them guard. But those [wives] from whom you fear arrogance - [first] advise them; [then if they persist], forsake them in bed; and [finally], strike them. But if they obey you [once more], seek no means against them. Indeed, Allah is ever Exalted and Grand
commentary:
ٱلرِّجَالُ قَوَّٰمُونَ عَلَى ٱلنِّسَآءِ بِمَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَبِمَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ ۚ فَٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٌ حَـٰفِظَـٰتٌ لِّلْغَيْبِ بِمَا حَفِظَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱلَّـٰتِى تَخَافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَّ فَعِظُوهُنَّ وَٱهْجُرُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْمَضَاجِعِ وَٱضْرِبُوهُنَّ ۖ فَإِنْ أَطَعْنَكُمْ فَلَا تَبْغُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ سَبِيلًا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيًّا كَبِيرًا

### The Meaning of Qawwamun

This ayah requires careful understanding. The word **قَوَّٰمُونَ** comes from qama, which in Arabic means to have authority over something, to watch over something, to be in charge of something. That's its linguistic meaning. Qawwam by itself, qama bi something, qama ala something—each has its distinct meaning.

**ٱلرِّجَالُ قَوَّٰمُونَ عَلَى ٱلنِّسَآءِ** - "Men have been made maintainers and in authority over women." Maintenance is part of it, from the same root. But there's also an element of authority. This connects directly to the previous ayah about not wishing for what another has and who is more answerable before Allah ﷻ, because men have taken on a responsibility through the marriage contract—the mithaq—discussed earlier in this surah.

How can men abuse this? By saying, "I'm the boss of everything. Come here." By demanding, "Hang up the phone—I don't care if you're talking to your mother." That's not how this works. Men haven't been made absolute bosses in the household.

Consider that even Allah ﷻ—the ultimate authority—establishes principles for how authority functions. In every organization, there's management and there are employees. Both are employees to a higher authority. Without hierarchy, you cannot function. If everyone is on equal footing and a manager tells someone to do something and they respond, "Who are you? You're not the owner, just a manager"—you can't run an organization like that.

The most fundamentally important organization in the world is the family. There must be some hierarchy. This hierarchy is not absolute. There is shura (mutual consultation), there is back-and-forth discussion, there are the ethics of how the Prophet ﷺ dealt with the Mothers of the Believers (RA). All of that exists. But fundamentally, you cannot escape the underlying premise: there is a head of household, and that responsibility has been given to the man.

If men don't act like men—if they don't fulfill this responsibility—that's a different situation requiring counseling and guidance. But this ayah describes the ideal situation, how a household is meant to function. Difficult decisions—financial, career-related, family matters—must be made after shura. In the end, you have to go with one decision. You can't go with two, because constant pulling in opposite directions means nothing gets accomplished in the household.

The husband must understand: I don't have absolute authority. The wife must understand: he does have some authority, and I must respect that. Otherwise the household cannot function. In our times, everyone is highly opinionated. We're undermining the institution of shura—actually taking counsel, considering matters from both sides before deciding. We dislike leadership in any capacity—our bosses, police, government. Any sign of authority, we resist it. When Islam says authority has been given to the husband, it's not easily accepted nowadays. Yet it's something we must revive because it's part of the health of the household.

### Two Critical Lessons

This ayah teaches us about the mutaba (the followed) and the muta'a (the follower). These principles exist in any organization. After Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ—whom we follow—the Messenger ﷺ appointed people to be followed. The imam leading salah is followed; we are the followers. There are principles governing the responsibilities of the leader and principles governing the responsibilities of the follower. These principles are universal.

Earlier in Surah Ali Imran, we encountered qualities of leadership. Those are the qualities any leader in any capacity should have. Similarly, there are qualities of the follower—whether in a community, classroom, household, or children under parents. All are followers in some context, and we must understand what that entails.

### Why This Authority?

**بِمَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ** - "Because Allah has given preference to one over the other in this matter." This is the first justification, listed in order of priority. Why does this responsibility in the household rest with the man rather than the woman? Not because men are smarter, bigger, stronger, or make more money. The number one reason: Allah ﷻ decided. That's it. There's no other justification needed.

**وَبِمَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ** - "And because of what they spend from their wealth." This is the secondary reason. Men are financially responsible for the entire household. Remember the mahr and the mithaq discussed earlier? Men carry that financial responsibility.

### The Qualities of Righteous Women

**فَٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٌ** - "Then righteous women are qanitat"—subservient, ready and willing to obey, supportive. Note that "righteous" (salihat) refers to their relationship with Allah ﷻ. Allah ﷻ has made their obedience and support in the household a matter of their relationship with Him.

This pattern repeats throughout the Quran: our relationships with each other are directly connected to our relationship with Allah ﷻ. I must be good to my mother—not only because she deserves it (though she does)—but first because Allah ﷻ wants me to. I will be good to my husband not only for his sake, but first because Allah ﷻ wants me to. Everything else is secondary.

**حَـٰفِظَـٰتٌ لِّلْغَيْبِ بِمَا حَفِظَ ٱللَّهُ** - "Guarding in the unseen what Allah has guarded." When the husband is gone, they guard secrets, dignity, conversations. They don't call friends to complain for three hours: "My husband said this today. He's so difficult sometimes..." then when he comes home, "Gotta call you back!"

The most significant thing guarded is the wife's chastity. But there are other matters too. Men have flaws—obviously. Nowhere are those flaws more apparent than at home. You're yourself at home. You don't have to dress up or maintain appearances. Your wife knows everything about you—how you sound when you sleep, what you do in the morning, your breakfast habits, everything. She could expose all of this to the world, but she doesn't, because she is **حَـٰفِظَـٰتٌ لِّلْغَيْبِ** - "guarding the unseen."

He has opened himself up to her, trusting that she will guard these things. The same is true in reverse. This is why, when people ask in question-and-answer sessions, "There's something really wrong with my husband" or "My wife is really difficult—here's her email address, could you talk to her?"—don't expose your spouse like that. Don't. Have some respect for your spouse. Even in counseling sessions, you should be careful, because in the end, that's your spouse.

**بِمَا حَفِظَ ٱللَّهُ** - "On account of what Allah has guarded." Even if your spouse successfully hides your embarrassing secrets, it's only because Allah ﷻ has allowed them to be hidden. In the end, she's not guarding anything—Allah ﷻ is using her to guard.

### Dealing with Nushuz (Serious Rebellion)

**وَٱلَّـٰتِى تَخَافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَّ** - "And those women from whom you fear nushuz." Nushuz is serious rebellion—when a wife becomes completely out of control, throwing objects, breaking plates, becoming violent. This is not normal disagreement like "I didn't like what you said the other day." Nushuz is unusual, extreme rebellion that's out of control.

What should you do?

**فَعِظُوهُنَّ** - "Then counsel them." Sit them down and give them advice. This doesn't necessarily mean delivering a sermon to them. It could mean getting them to a counselor, having them talk to someone about their anger, sitting down with their parents to try to figure things out.

The man has to be the one who takes the beating—the verbal abuse. He has to take it and keep taking it. His response? Counsel them. This may go on for years. This isn't something that happens once or twice—it can continue for a long time.

If after an extended period it's not working anymore and she's gotten even worse over time, even crazier, while you've been tolerating it, trying to get her help, trying to advise, keeping your cool, never raising your voice, never getting angry (and if you did, you start over), and you maintained that for a long time and it still didn't work, what's next?

**وَٱهْجُرُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْمَضَاجِعِ** - "And leave them in their beds." Sleep separately. Leave their bed and sleep on your side. This is an expression of extreme displeasure. She can hit you, say mean things to you, but she knows you'll come back to bed. When you don't come back to bed the next night, it will bother her. It will disturb her. Maybe that will help her rethink what she's doing.

This is not one conversation where you advised her in one sentence and immediately moved to stage two, sleeping on the couch. Each stage is a prolonged phase. Only when you know for certain that one phase is absolutely not working do you move to phase two.

When you move to phase two, if she has any decency, she'll feel remorse. She'll say, "Okay, just come back to bed." But if there's too much pride, too much rage, too much anger, and she says, "Fine, go sleep at your mother's house—you love her more anyway," and you try to maintain this for a while (while continuing to counsel her), and still nothing changes...

**وَٱضْرِبُوهُنَّ** - "And strike them."

### Understanding the Command to Strike

Let me be clear about what this ayah says. It does say "strike them." But we must understand this properly.

First, this ayah, like many in the Quran, has been profoundly abused in the Muslim world. Women suffer abuse under the shadow of this beautiful, magnificent ayah. When we think about the relationship between husband and wife, we think of the general principles revealed in the Quran and the specific application in the Sunnah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

There's a movement within the Muslim community that wants to completely discard hadith literature. When they approach this ayah, they perform all kinds of interpretive gymnastics to translate it in a politically correct way. As Sunni Muslims, all we have to say is: look at how the Prophet ﷺ implemented this. Let's see what he did.

The Messenger ﷺ was so loving, so gentle. This is the same Messenger who saw a donkey one time with a branded face and said, "Cursed is the person who did this." He couldn't stand abuse of animals. At the beginning of this surah, we were commanded to have taqwa regarding relationships and the wombs—to be cautious in dealing with the mother of your children, even if she has developed psychological problems, schizophrenia, multiple personality disorder. Even if one moment she's nice and the next she's unstable—she still bore your children, she still stayed with you all this time. If she's going through difficulty now, you must show restraint.

The Prophet's ﷺ application was minimal—a light tap with two fingers, just to say "stop." He never, ever raised his hand harshly at his wives (RA).

**فَإِنْ أَطَعْنَكُمْ فَلَا تَبْغُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ سَبِيلًا** - "Then if they start following you, don't pursue a case against them." Don't say, "Now that we've reached stage three, everything is stage three from now on." Don't make everything about that. Let bygones be bygones. Don't pursue a case against them anymore. Make a fresh start.

This is critically important for married couples to understand: once you go through a problem and resolve it, you must start fresh. You cannot bring old issues up again. It's done. It's finished. It's as if you've gotten newly married again. No more past issues. Don't remember what happened in April 2002. Let it go.

Both husband and wife must learn to do this—to let things go. It's the hardest thing to do, but if you're worried about your future, you have to let past wounds heal. Otherwise you cannot move forward.

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيًّا كَبِيرًا** - "Indeed, Allah has always been the Most High, the Greatest." You think you're the authority in the household? You're nothing. Allah ﷻ has always been the true authority. It's profoundly important that He placed His greatness, His superiority at the end of this ayah, so the husband would never get the idea that he has the ultimate upper hand. He should never forget that Allah ﷻ is above all.

translation: And if you fear dissension between the two, send an arbitrator from his people and an arbitrator from her people. If they both desire reconciliation, Allah will cause it between them. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Acquainted [with all things]
commentary:
وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ شِقَاقَ بَيْنِهِمَا فَٱبْعَثُوا۟ حَكَمًا مِّنْ أَهْلِهِۦ وَحَكَمًا مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن يُرِيدَآ إِصْلَـٰحًا يُوَفِّقِ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَهُمَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا خَبِيرًا

When marital conflict threatens to create an irreparable divide between husband and wife, Allah ﷻ provides a practical solution through community intervention. The word شِقَاق describes a schism or crack that seems impossible to repair. This ayah addresses the extended families and the community at large, instructing them to appoint wise representatives to mediate the dispute.

The command is clear: appoint حَكَمًا - a wise decision-maker or counselor - from his family, and another hakam from her family. This approach recognizes a profound truth about marital conflict: sometimes couples love each other deeply but struggle with communication, especially when emotions run high. They say things they don't mean, take words the wrong way, and find themselves trapped in cycles of misunderstanding.

When communication breaks down, intentions become suspect. She says something, he interprets it as an attack on his character. He responds defensively, she hears rejection. Neither party is actually trying to hurt the other, but the emotional turbulence makes clear communication nearly impossible. This is where the wisdom of having level-headed representatives from both families becomes evident.

These mediators serve as translators of true intentions. They can explain to him that when she became upset about his arriving five hours late without calling, it wasn't about control - she had prepared dinner with care and was dealing with a severe headache, but her anger prevented her from articulating these details. They can help her understand his perspective, creating space for genuine apology and reconciliation. Without such intervention, minor misunderstandings escalate into threats to leave or return to parents' homes.

However, the success of this mediation depends entirely on the intentions of those involved. If the hakam themselves harbor bias or secretly desire the divorce to happen, they will make matters worse rather than better. This is why Allah ﷻ follows the instruction with a crucial condition: إِن يُرِيدَآ إِصْلَـٰحًا - if they both want reconciliation. The "they" here can refer to either the two mediators or the husband and wife themselves.

When the intention for reconciliation is sincere, something remarkable happens: يُوَفِّقِ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَهُمَآ - Allah ﷻ will create an ability between them, will empower them, will grant them success in their efforts. The divine assistance removes miscommunication and misunderstanding. Allah ﷻ enables the successful negotiation to take place, but only when the intention for unity exists.

The ayah concludes with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا خَبِيرًا - certainly Allah ﷻ has always been fully knowledgeable, fully aware. He knows the true state of the marriage, the real intentions of all parties involved, and the best path forward. His knowledge encompasses both the visible and the hidden dimensions of the conflict.

translation: Worship Allah and associate nothing with Him, and to parents do good, and to relatives, orphans, the needy, the near neighbor, the neighbor farther away, the companion at your side, the traveler, and those whom your right hands possess. Indeed, Allah does not like those who are self-deluding and boastful
commentary:
۞ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا وَبِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱلْجَارِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْجَارِ ٱلْجُنُبِ وَٱلصَّاحِبِ بِٱلْجَنۢبِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ مُخْتَالًا فَخُورًا

From specific guidance about resolving marital disputes, the surah now expands to universal principles of conduct. The ayah begins with the foundational command: وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - enslave yourselves to Allah ﷻ, submit completely to Him. This is followed immediately by the prohibition against shirk: وَلَا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا - and don't associate anything with Him in any way, shape or form.

After establishing the rights of Allah ﷻ, the ayah moves to the rights of people, beginning with parents: وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا - and to both parents, show excellence in conduct. The word إِحْسَان signifies the highest level of beautiful conduct - not merely being good, but being excellent, going above and beyond.

This ihsan then extends outward in concentric circles of social responsibility. First comes ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ - those who possess closeness, beginning with close family members but extending to neighbors and relatives. Then ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ - the orphans, those without parental protection. Then ٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ - those who are financially stuck, unable to help themselves, the destitute.

The ayah then addresses different categories of neighbors with remarkable specificity. First, ٱلْجَارِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ - the neighbor who lives right by, especially the neighbor who is close to you. This refers to the neighbor who knows a lot about you, with whom you have frequent interaction. These are often the neighbors with whom tensions can arise precisely because of proximity. Yet Allah ﷻ commands excellence toward them.

Then comes ٱلْجَارِ ٱلْجُنُبِ - the neighbor on the side, right next to you. Some scholars understand this as the neighbor who is not a relative, distinguishing them from the previous category.

The command extends even to temporary companionship: وَٱلصَّاحِبِ بِٱلْجَنۢبِ - the companion by your side. This refers to someone traveling with you - a person sitting next to you on a bus or flight, fellow travelers in a caravan, anyone who happens to be beside you temporarily. Even to strangers in transit, you must show courtesy and excellence. The same ihsan that began with parents applies here too, though perhaps not at the same intensity level since parents were specifically highlighted.

وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - the traveler, literally "the son of the road," someone far from home who may need assistance. And finally, وَمَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ - those whom your right hands possess, referring to servants or those under your authority. The ayah specifically mentions them because no one will come asking whether you're being kind to them or not - they're under your command. Yet ihsan applies to them as well.

Imagine a society built on this principle - where everyone's driving motivation is to find reasons to be excellent to others. Consider how this would transform public spaces: people on trains offering seats to one another, helping with luggage, showing genuine concern for strangers. In some places, remnants of this culture still exist, where strangers greet each other warmly and offer help. Yet in many modern societies, this has become rare or even suspect behavior.

Even in our masajid, we sometimes fail to embody this spirit. The security guard at an event, simply trying to make a living, receives cold treatment instead of appreciation. The police officer directing traffic outside deserves thanks for facilitating our gathering. The person traveling next to us deserves our courtesy and kindness.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement about what prevents people from living this way: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ مُخْتَالًا فَخُورًا - certainly Allah ﷻ does not love the one who is arrogant and boastful.

This ending reveals something profound about human psychology. The ayah wasn't directly about arrogance - it was about speaking nicely to people and showing them excellence. Yet Allah ﷻ identifies arrogance as the root problem. If someone is incapable of being kind, if they get angry easily, lose their cool, or find everything and everyone annoying, the underlying issue is arrogance.

Two words describe this arrogance: مُخْتَالًا and فَخُورًا. The first, mukhtalan, describes someone who maintains an inflated self-image. They see themselves in lofty, unrealistic terms - they must wear certain brands with visible logos, walk a certain way, maintain a carefully constructed facade. They live in an imaginary world where they are the star of their own commercial.

The second word, fakhur, describes someone with excessive pride in their accomplishments. They believe they've achieved great things and therefore deserve tremendous credit and recognition. These two qualities - the imaginary inflated self-image and the excessive pride in real or perceived accomplishments - are what lead people to look down on others, to judge them, to be unable to show them ihsan.

People judge others by clothing, skin color, seating arrangements, associations, neighborhoods, the cars they drive. But believers cannot engage in such judgment because we recognize it as an indication of pride. And when pride exists, what cannot coexist with it? Iman. True faith and arrogance cannot occupy the same heart.

translation: Who are stingy and enjoin upon [other] people stinginess and conceal what Allah has given them of His bounty - and We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ وَيَكْتُمُونَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا

This ayah continues describing these arrogant, prideful people, exposing the reality they desperately try to hide. ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ - those who are stingy, who are cheap. But their miserliness doesn't end with themselves: وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ - they command others to be stingy too.

Understanding this behavior reveals the psychology of the arrogant miser. When calls for charity are made, people with even a little iman get motivated - they think about building their house in Jannah and contribute generously. But the one filled with pride isn't moved by such appeals. He has wealth, but pride has filled the space in his heart where iman should be. True iman of Allah ﷻ cannot exist where pride dominates.

Here's where the dynamic becomes interesting: everyone around him is giving, and everyone knows he has substantial wealth. How do they know? Because he's mukhtalan and fakhur - he shows it off. So when everyone else contributes and he doesn't, his stinginess gets exposed. He needs a strategy to avoid giving while simultaneously avoiding the exposure of his cheapness.

His solution? Position himself as the wise financial analyst. "Be smart about this. It's a tough economy. Why are you writing them such a large check? Sure, the masjid needs money, but not right now. You need to be strategic with your finances." He comes across as offering prudent financial advice, but the real goal is to hide his own miserliness and to discourage others from giving, which would make his non-contribution less noticeable.

Then comes the next level: وَيَكْتُمُونَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ - they hide what Allah ﷻ has given them from His bounty. When someone comes to their mansion asking for a donation, suddenly they claim financial hardship: "Right now I have so many medical problems, all my money is going to that. Actually, the house is foreclosed. I'm going through a difficult time." They conceal the blessings Allah ﷻ has bestowed upon them, telling elaborate lies to avoid spending.

All these efforts serve one purpose: to save themselves from humiliation, from being exposed as cheap. But Allah ﷻ declares: وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا - and We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment. The word كَـٰفِرِينَ here carries the meaning of "ungrateful ones" as well as disbelievers. They go through all this effort to avoid humiliation in this world, but Allah ﷻ has prepared for them the very thing they feared - humiliation - in a punishment they cannot escape.

translation: And [also] those who spend of their wealth to be seen by the people and believe not in Allah nor in the Last Day. And he to whom Satan is a companion - then evil is he as a companion
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۗ وَمَن يَكُنِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَهُۥ قَرِينًا فَسَآءَ قَرِينًا

Now Allah ﷻ describes another category - not those who refuse to spend, but those who do spend with corrupt intentions. وَٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ - those who spend their wealth to show off to people, for the sake of being seen and praised by others.

Their spending is generous, even lavish, but the motivation is entirely wrong: وَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ - they don't believe in Allah ﷻ, nor do they believe in the Last Day. The only incentive driving their generosity is the desire for people to say, "MashaAllah, look at that generous check he wrote! What a big-hearted person!"

Imagine such a person at a fundraising event. Shaytan becomes his constant companion and advisor: "Okay, fine, write the check. But make sure you get one of those oversized ceremonial checks for the photo opportunity. Make sure the amount is visible in the picture. Make sure it gets posted on the website. Insist on a plaque with your name. Make sure everyone knows." 

This is what the ayah means by وَمَن يَكُنِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَهُۥ قَرِينًا - whoever has Shaytan as a constant associate, a constant friend. Shaytan doesn't just whisper once; he becomes the qarīn, the ever-present companion reinforcing the inflated ego at every turn.

After the generous donation and the public recognition, the person might momentarily question their motives. But Shaytan immediately steps in: "Come on, you really are generous. You really are that good. Don't be so hard on yourself - you deserve the recognition." The imaginary ego gets inflated further and further, until the person genuinely believes their own propaganda.

The ayah concludes: فَسَآءَ قَرِينًا - what a horrible companion that is, what a terrible friend to have as your constant associate.

translation: And what [harm would come] upon them if they believed in Allah and the Last Day and spent out of what Allah provided for them? And Allah is ever, about them, Knowing
commentary:
وَمَاذَا عَلَيْهِمْ لَوْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمْ عَلِيمًا

Allah ﷻ now poses a rhetorical question that cuts to the heart of the matter: وَمَاذَا عَلَيْهِمْ - what was wrong with them? What would have been so hard for them? This is a figure of speech in Arabic expressing bewilderment at someone's failure to do something beneficial and easy.

لَوْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ - had they just truly believed in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day. وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ - and spent from what Allah ﷻ had provided them. If their spending had been based on genuine iman, with the intention to please Allah ﷻ and build assets for the Akhirah, everything would have been different.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمْ عَلِيمًا - and Allah ﷻ would have been knowledgeable of them. The One who needs to know about their spending would have known. This statement carries profound implications: when you spend sincerely for Allah's sake, Allah ﷻ knows. You don't need human witnesses or public recognition. The only One whose knowledge matters already knows.

The surah continues with a powerful promise that contextualizes everything that came before: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ - certainly Allah ﷻ will not wrong anyone even the weight of a speck, even the tiniest particle. Nothing you do goes unnoticed or unrecorded.

وَإِن تَكُ حَسَنَةً يُضَـٰعِفْهَا - and if there be a good deed, He will multiply it. First, Allah ﷻ assures us He won't wrong us by even a speck. Then He promises that any good deed will be multiplied many times over.

But the generosity doesn't end there: وَيُؤْتِ مِن لَّدُنْهُ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - and He will give, especially from His Own presence, a tremendous reward. Look at the structure of this promise: what you do, Allah ﷻ will multiply. And on top of that multiplied reward, He will give you something extra, something special held just for you. And it's not just any reward - it's أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا, a tremendous compensation.

The phrase مِن لَّدُنْهُ - from His Own presence, from Him specially - indicates something beyond the earned reward. He could have simply said "He will give you additional compensation," but instead He said He will give you something special from His Own treasures, something He's holding specifically for you.

This is how generous Allah ﷻ is willing to be with you. The ayah poses the implicit question through its structure: How generous, then, are you willing to be for Allah's sake? That's the contrast being drawn. Allah's generosity is infinite and multi-layered - He doesn't wrong you by even a speck, He multiplies your good deeds, and then He adds tremendous rewards from His special treasures. In response, all He asks is that you believe in Him sincerely and spend from what He has already provided you.

translation: Indeed, Allah does not do injustice, [even] as much as an atom's weight; while if there is a good deed, He multiplies it and gives from Himself a great reward
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ ۖ وَإِن تَكُ حَسَنَةً يُضَـٰعِفْهَا وَيُؤْتِ مِن لَّدُنْهُ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

This ayah opens with a powerful declaration: certainly Allah ﷻ is not the one who wrongs even the weight of a speck. The grammatical structure itself contains profound meaning. When Allah ﷻ says لَا يَظْلِمُ (does not wrong), the passive construction implies that someone else does commit this wrong. The ayah distinguishes between divine justice and human injustice. People wrong each other, but Allah ﷻ never does.

The second part of the ayah introduces an interesting grammatical feature. The phrase وَإِن تَكُ حَسَنَةً (and if there be a good deed) uses an abbreviated form. Grammatically, we would expect وَإِن تَكُونَ حَسَنَةٌ with a noon at the end. The removal of the noon, while linguistically acceptable with the verb كان, creates a particular emphasis: even if there be the smallest good deed, something minuscule and barely worth mentioning.

What follows demonstrates Allah's infinite generosity: يُضَـٰعِفْهَا (He will multiply it). But the mercy doesn't end there. وَيُؤْتِ مِن لَّدُنْهُ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - and He will grant, above and beyond that multiplication, from His own behalf, a tremendous compensation. This is Allah's character: He multiplies our meager efforts and then adds His own generous gift on top of that multiplication.

Notice how the ayah flows from justice to mercy and back to justice. The Surah as a whole addresses the theme of justice, both in this world in terms of giving people what they deserve, and in the Hereafter where no exception will be made for anyone.

translation: So how [will it be] when We bring from every nation a witness and we bring you, [O Muhammad] against these [people] as a witness
commentary:
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شَهِيدًا

After establishing the principle of divine justice, Allah ﷻ now reveals one of the most powerful scenes of the Day of Judgment. The ayah begins with فَكَيْفَ (How will it be?) - left incomplete to emphasize the overwhelming nature of what follows. The question hangs in the air: how will people handle this moment?

إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِشَهِيدٍ - when We bring a witness from every single nation. Every community that ever received guidance will have their messenger stand as a witness. But then comes the most significant part: وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شَهِيدًا - and We will bring you as a witness against these people.

The pronoun used here is هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ (these), which indicates something nearby. Had Allah ﷻ said أُولَـٰئِكَ (those), it would suggest distance. The choice of "these" makes the scene immediate and present, as if pointing directly at the community of Muhammad ﷺ.

This concept of the Prophet ﷺ bearing witness affected him deeply. Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) narrates that the Messenger of Allah ﷺ once asked him to recite Quran. Ibn Mas'ud (RA) was surprised - how could he recite to the one upon whom it was revealed? But the Prophet ﷺ said, "I love to hear it from others." So Ibn Mas'ud (RA) began reciting Surah An-Nisa. When he reached this ayah - when he came to the words "and We will bring you as a witness against these" - he heard the Prophet ﷺ say, "Enough, stop." Ibn Mas'ud (RA) looked up to find tears streaming down the blessed face of the Messenger ﷺ.

The Prophet ﷺ understood what this meant. On the Day of Judgment, the scene resembles a trial, and in a trial, a prosecution witness must testify. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ will have to stand and testify about his nation - whether they carried the message, whether they took it seriously, whether they delivered it to humanity as they were supposed to. Whether he wants to or not, he must give this testimony.

This testimony extends to us. The Messenger ﷺ will testify whether we upheld the trust he left with us. May Allah ﷻ qualify us to be on the right side of that testimony.

It's important to note that while the Quran emphasizes the Prophet ﷺ testifying against people, the concept of his intercession and testifying in favor of believers appears primarily in the Sunnah. This balance is deliberate. Other nations fell into deviation by believing their messengers would automatically testify in their favor regardless of their deeds. They forgot that messengers don't just bear witness for you - they also bear witness against you.

Today, many Muslims believe strongly in the intercession (شفاعة) of the Messenger ﷺ, but they don't balance it with awareness of his testimony (شهادة). We must believe in both and live in a way that qualifies us to be on the right side of that testimony.

translation: That Day, those who disbelieved and disobeyed the Messenger will wish they could be covered by the earth. And they will not conceal from Allah a [single] statement
commentary:
يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَعَصَوُا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ لَوْ تُسَوَّىٰ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَلَا يَكْتُمُونَ ٱللَّهَ حَدِيثًا

This ayah describes the state of those who face the testimony described in the previous ayah. يَوْمَئِذٍ (on that Day) - the Day when witnesses are brought forth - يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (those who disbelieved will wish). Then Allah ﷻ adds another category: وَعَصَوُا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ (and those who disobeyed the Messenger).

Some scholars understand the و (and) here as explanatory, meaning that those who disbelieved are, by definition, those who disobeyed the Messenger. The two descriptions are intertwined - disbelief manifests as disobedience.

لَوْ تُسَوَّىٰ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ - they will wish that the earth had been leveled with them. The verb سَوَّىٰ means to make something even or level. They will wish they had been buried in the earth so completely that no one could tell they were ever there, so hidden that the earth's surface was smooth above them. They want to disappear entirely, to be erased from existence rather than face this testimony.

وَلَا يَكْتُمُونَ ٱللَّهَ حَدِيثًا - and they will not be able to hide from Allah even a single statement. Despite their wish to disappear, not a single word, not a single deed will remain hidden. Complete transparency before divine justice is inescapable.

These two ayahs complete a powerful sequence about justice on the Day of Judgment, taking us from the guarantee of Allah's justice, to the mechanism of testimony through messengers, to the desperate state of those who must face that testimony.

translation: O you who have believed, do not approach prayer while you are intoxicated until you know what you are saying or in a state of janabah, except those passing through [a place of prayer], until you have washed [your whole body]. And if you are ill or on a journey or one of you comes from the place of relieving himself or you have contacted women and find no water, then seek clean earth and wipe over your faces and your hands [with it]. Indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and Forgiving
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنتُمْ سُكَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا۟ مَا تَقُولُونَ وَلَا جُنُبًا إِلَّا عَابِرِى سَبِيلٍ حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا

After discussing the justice of the Hereafter, the Surah returns to practical laws for this life. يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - O you who have believed. This address to the believers introduces guidance about approaching salah properly.

لَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنتُمْ سُكَـٰرَىٰ - don't go near the salah while you are intoxicated. This ayah was revealed during a transitional period when alcohol had not yet been completely prohibited. Some companions would drink and then attempt to perform salah, leading to mistakes in their recitation. There are narrations of someone reciting Surah Al-Kafirun incorrectly while intoxicated, saying "I worship what you worship" instead of "I do not worship what you worship" - inadvertently making a statement of disbelief.

حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا۟ مَا تَقُولُونَ - until you know what you are saying. This condition is crucial. The Companions (RA) were not like many of us who only make salah at the five prescribed times. They would make salah for every small matter - for dua, for gratitude, seeking guidance. Salah permeated their day. Practically speaking, if they had to remain sober enough to know what they were saying at any moment they might pray, there was no time left for drinking. This was an intelligent hint, a transitional step toward the complete prohibition that would come later.
