The intelligent person understands hints without needing everything spelled out explicitly. Earlier in this Surah, Allah ﷻ had already commanded قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا - say something clear, direct and proper. How can someone who is intoxicated and doesn't know what they're saying fulfill this command?

وَلَا جُنُبًا إِلَّا عَابِرِى سَبِيلٍ حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا۟ - and don't approach salah in a state of major ritual impurity, except when passing through, until you have bathed. This addresses the requirement of purification before prayer.

The ayah then addresses circumstances where normal purification may be difficult or impossible: وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ - if you are sick or on a journey. Some illnesses prevent bathing - whether due to fever, wounds, or other conditions. In the time of revelation, hot water wasn't readily available, so bathing during illness or in harsh conditions could be dangerous or even lethal.

أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ - or one of you comes from relieving himself. This addresses minor impurity requiring wudu.

أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ - or you have had physical intimacy with women. This euphemistic expression addresses major impurity.

فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً - and then you couldn't find water. The verb used is لَمْ تَجِدُوا۟, which means not just that water isn't immediately available, but that you looked for it and couldn't find it. There's an effort implied.

فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا - then perform tayammum with pure earth. The word تَيَمَّمُوا۟ comes from يَمَّمَ, meaning to intend toward something, to head for something. The word صَعِيدًا refers to dried earth or dust from the ground.

فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ - then pass over your faces and your hands. This describes the simple action of tayammum - using clean earth to wipe the face and hands when water is unavailable.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا - certainly Allah is extremely pardoning and extremely forgiving. This conclusion is essential to understanding the concession. The ideal is to perform full ablution with water, to be completely clean when standing before Allah ﷻ. But Allah ﷻ, in His mercy, allows this lighter form of purification when circumstances demand it. The words عَفُوًّا (pardoning) and غَفُورًا (forgiving) reassure us that Allah ﷻ overlooks the shortcoming and opens this door of ease for His servants.

translation: Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture, purchasing error [in exchange for it] and wishing you would lose the way
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يَشْتَرُونَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ

Here the Surah transitions to its second major theme, shifting focus to the People of the Book. Remember that three major themes run through Surah An-Nisa: social laws and justice, the People of the Book, and the hypocrites. We now enter a sequence of ayahs dealing with the second theme.

أَلَمْ تَرَ - don't you see, haven't you looked? This rhetorical question draws attention to an observable reality. إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ - toward those who were given a portion of the Book. They received revelation, they were entrusted with scripture, yet look at what they've done with it.

يَشْتَرُونَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ - they purchase misguidance. The metaphor of purchasing suggests a deliberate transaction, an active choice. They trade away truth for falsehood, guidance for error. It's not passive confusion - it's an active acquisition of misguidance.

وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ - and they want that you lose the path. Their corruption isn't limited to themselves. They actively desire that Muslims also go astray. The verb used is تَضِلُّوا۟, which means to lose the path, to wander further and further away until you don't even remember where the path is. They want believers to be completely off track, thoroughly lost.

translation: And Allah is most knowing of your enemies; and sufficient is Allah as an ally, and sufficient is Allah as a helper
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِأَعْدَآئِكُمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ نَصِيرًا

This ayah provides divine reassurance in the face of the opposition described in the previous ayah. وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِأَعْدَآئِكُمْ - and Allah is best knowledgeable about your enemies. You may not fully recognize who opposes you or the extent of their enmity, but Allah ﷻ knows completely. His knowledge encompasses not just their outward actions but their hidden intentions.

وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا - and sufficient is Allah as a protective friend. The word وَلِيّ indicates someone who takes care of your affairs, who protects you, who is there for you as a guardian and ally. Allah ﷻ is enough. You don't need to fear the plots of those who wish you astray when Allah ﷻ is your Wali.

وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ نَصِيرًا - and sufficient is Allah as one who aids. The word نَصِير refers specifically to help that comes in difficult circumstances, in hard times. The term نَصْر (help) in Arabic particularly means assistance when things are tough, when you're facing challenges. Allah ﷻ is enough as your helper in those moments of difficulty.

The repetition of وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ emphasizes sufficiency. You need no one else. Allah ﷻ alone is enough as both protector and helper.

translation: Among the Jews are those who distort words from their [proper] usages and say, "We hear and disobey" and "Hear but be not heard" and "Ra'ina," twisting their tongues and defaming the religion. And if they had said [instead], "We hear and obey" and "Wait for us [to understand]," it would have been better for them and more suitable. But Allah has cursed them for their disbelief, so they believe not, except for a few
commentary:
مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ وَرَٰعِنَا لَيًّۢا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْنًا فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ وَٱنظُرْنَا لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَقْوَمَ وَلَـٰكِن لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

This ayah provides specific examples of the corruption described previously. مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ - among those who are Jewish. Not all, but among them are those who engage in these practices.

يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ - they distort words from their proper places. The root ح ر ف means to change, to tilt, to edit. They alter revelation, changing words from where they belong, twisting meanings to suit their purposes.

The ayah then describes their verbal mockery: وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا - and they say "we hear and we disobey." Rather than the proper response of سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا (we hear and we obey), they openly declare defiance.

وَٱسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ - and they say "listen" while meaning "may you not hear," essentially cursing the Prophet ﷺ while pretending to be respectful.

وَرَٰعِنَا - and they say "ra'ina." This is particularly clever mockery. The proper respectful word would be انظُرْنَا (unthurna), which means "look toward us" or "give us your attention" or "teach us." But instead they would twist their tongues and say رَاعِنَا (ra'ina), which can mean "our shepherd" in a demeaning way, as if the Prophet ﷺ was their hired servant, or it sounds like a Hebrew insult.

لَيًّۢا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ - twisting with their tongues. They deliberately manipulate pronunciation to insert mockery while maintaining plausible deniability.

وَطَعْنًا فِى ٱلدِّينِ - and as an attack on the religion. The word طَعْن means to poke, to stab, to insult. Their word games constitute an assault on the faith itself.

وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ وَٱنظُرْنَا - and had they said "we hear and we obey, and listen, and look toward us." This would have been the straightforward, honest, respectful way to communicate.

لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَقْوَمَ - it would have been so much better for them and more upright. The word أَقْوَمَ means more correct, more proper, more right.

وَلَـٰكِن لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ - however, Allah has cursed them because of their disbelief. After describing their twisted speech, Allah ﷻ speaks directly and clearly with no twisted words: they are cursed.

فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا - then they will not believe except very few. This phrase has two possible meanings: either very few among them will come to believe, or the quantity and quality of their faith will be very small.

translation: O you who were given the Scripture, believe in what We have sent down [to Muhammad], confirming that which is with you, before We obliterate faces and turn them toward their backs or curse them as We cursed the sabbath-breakers. And ever is the decree of Allah accomplished
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِهَآ أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ كَمَا لَعَنَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّبْتِ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا

The previous ayah spoke about the People of the Book in third person - "they twist their tongues." But Allah ﷻ knows they are listening. He knows that when He declared لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ (Allah has cursed them), they heard it. So now He addresses them directly.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ - O you who have been given the Book. Those who are listening right now, this is for you.

ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا - believe in what We have sent down. The People of Faith are being called to have true faith. This might seem redundant - why call people of faith to faith? Because faith without believing in the final messenger is not complete faith. You cannot be selective about which prophets and scriptures you accept.

مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُم - as a confirmation of what you already have with you. The Quran doesn't contradict the original Torah and Gospel - it confirms the truth they contained.

مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا - before We obliterate faces. The verb طَمَسَ means to deform, to erase features, to change something's shape completely. This is a severe warning.

فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِهَآ - then turn them backward. After deforming the faces, they would be turned to face backward. The imagery is disturbing and meant to be.

Notice that halfway through the ayah, Allah ﷻ switches from second person back to third person. He was speaking to them directly (you who were given the Book, believe), but then shifts to speaking about them (We will curse them). This sudden shift is deliberate - Allah ﷻ spoke to them briefly, gave them the warning directly, then turned away from them. The brief direct address followed by turning away shows they're no longer worthy of sustained direct address.

أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ كَمَا لَعَنَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّبْتِ - or We will curse them just as We cursed the people of the Sabbath. This refers to those who violated the Sabbath laws and were transformed as punishment. It's not all Jews who are referenced here, but specifically those who violated the sacred commandment regarding the Sabbath.

وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا - and the command of Allah is bound to be carried out. What Allah ﷻ decrees will certainly come to pass. This is both a warning and a statement of divine power - His decisions are not empty threats.

translation: Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills. And he who associates others with Allah has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا

This ayah establishes one of the most fundamental principles in Islamic theology. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ - indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners be associated with Him. Shirk - associating partners with Allah ﷻ in His names, attributes, qualities, or worship - is the one unforgivable sin if a person dies upon it without repentance.

وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ - and He forgives whatever is besides that for whomever He wills. This statement of mercy immediately follows the statement about shirk. Allah ﷻ may forgive any other sin - no matter how grave - if He wills.

But notice the crucial phrase: لِمَن يَشَآءُ - for whomever He wills. This is not a blank check. Some people misunderstand this ayah, thinking, "Allah forgives everything else, so what's the big deal?" and use it as an excuse for sin. But the ayah doesn't say He will definitely forgive everything else. It says He may forgive, for whomever He wants. His forgiveness is not automatic or guaranteed - it's according to His wisdom and will. This should inspire both hope (that He may forgive) and caution (that He may not).

وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا - and whoever associates partners with Allah has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin. The verb ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ means to invent, to fabricate, to make up something false. Shirk is described as a fabrication - creating false claims about Allah ﷻ - and as إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا, a tremendous sin, the greatest possible wrong.

translation: Have you not seen those who claim themselves to be pure? Rather, Allah purifies whom He wills, and injustice is not done to them, [even] as much as a thread [inside a date seed]
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُزَكُّونَ أَنفُسَهُم ۚ بَلِ ٱللَّهُ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا

After warning against shirk, Allah ﷻ now addresses another subtle form of spiritual corruption: self-righteousness. أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُزَكُّونَ أَنفُسَهُم - haven't you seen those who purify themselves? Or more precisely, those who consider themselves pure.

The verb يُزَكُّونَ comes from the root ز ك ي, which means to purify or to grow. But when used in this reflexive form - purifying oneself - it carries the meaning of self-declared purity, considering oneself purified. This is the essence of self-righteousness.

بَلِ ٱللَّهُ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ - rather, it is Allah who purifies whomever He wills. Purification is not self-declared. You don't get to announce your own sanctity. Allah ﷻ is the one who purifies hearts and souls, and He does so for whom He wills according to His wisdom.

This principle stands against a corruption that has infected religious communities throughout history, including within Islam. People declare sanctity for themselves or others - religious leaders who claim to be beyond sin, spiritual figures elevated to a status where they are considered pure and holy, whose prayers are automatically answered, through whom others must go to reach Allah ﷻ.

We see this in the concept of papal infallibility, in the elevation of priests and religious figures to positions where they cannot be questioned. We see it in the corruption of some Sufi traditions - not the legitimate aspects of Sufism, which include valuable practices of spiritual discipline and mentorship - but the distortion where certain individuals are declared to have reached spiritual stages that place them beyond normal human accountability.

The history of these traditions is complex and shouldn't be oversimplified. There has always been a place in Islam for learning from teachers, for respecting elders and scholars, for finding mentors who inspire us to be better Muslims. The companions (RA) learned manners and ethics from the Prophet ﷺ. Students have always benefited from sitting with righteous scholars and observing their character. This is healthy and necessary.

But there is a line that must not be crossed. We cannot declare anyone infallible. We cannot create a religious hierarchy where certain individuals become intermediaries between people and Allah ﷻ. We cannot elevate anyone to a position where questioning them becomes tantamount to disbelief, where absolute obedience to them is required, where people cannot make basic life decisions without their approval.

True Islamic tradition holds that what gives a person credibility is their character, their knowledge, their righteousness in daily life. Leadership in ethics and learning should be found in every neighborhood through imams, scholars, community elders. People naturally benefit from being around righteous individuals who inspire them to improve. But this is far different from declaring someone the equivalent of a pope, claiming they have reached a spiritual station that makes them beyond sin or grants them special powers.

The balanced approach respects scholarship and righteousness while avoiding sanctification of any human being. We show respect for knowledge and piety without crossing into worship or unquestioning submission to any person other than Allah ﷻ.

وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا - and they will not be wronged even the measure of a fatil. A fatil is a tiny thread that comes from a date seed. Think of the smallest possible fiber, something you could barely see. Allah ﷻ promises that on the Day of Judgment, when accounts are settled, not even this minuscule amount will be lost or wronged.

This is simultaneously reassurance and warning. For those who are righteous, every tiny good deed will be accounted for - nothing will be lost. But for those who consider themselves pure while carrying sins, every tiny wrong will also be accounted for. Divine justice operates with precision beyond human comprehension.

The ayah connects back to the theme of justice that runs throughout the Surah. Don't worry about declaring yourself pure - that's not your job. Focus on doing good and avoiding evil. Allah ﷻ will handle the purification, and His accounting will be perfectly fair down to the smallest measure imaginable.

translation: Look how they invent about Allah untruth, and sufficient is that as a manifest sin
commentary:
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦٓ إِثْمًا مُّبِينًا

Allah ﷻ commands us to observe how certain people fabricate lies against Him. Throughout history, corrupt religious leadership has maintained their status through theatrical displays of supposed divine connection. They would claim direct communication with Allah ﷻ, saying things like "I saw God yesterday and He told me such and such," all to keep people enchanted with their spiritual authority.

The ayah emphasizes وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦٓ إِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (and that in itself is enough of an open sin). This fabrication against Allah ﷻ should serve as clear evidence that such a person is openly sinful, not righteous. The very act of lying about Allah ﷻ disqualifies someone from any claim to righteousness or spiritual authority.

translation: Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture, who believe in superstition and false objects of worship and say about the disbelievers, "These are better guided than the believers as to the way
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْجِبْتِ وَٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَهْدَىٰ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ سَبِيلًا

Allah ﷻ directs our attention to those who were given نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (a portion from the Book). The word نَصِيب (portion) is significant here because it indicates they received an earlier portion of revelation, while the final portion—the Quran—was yet to come. Despite having been given Divine Scripture, these people turned to الْجِبْتِ وَٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ.

The term الْجِبْت encompasses everything worshipped besides Allah ﷻ—idols, false gods, and practices like magic, spell-casting, horoscopes, palm reading, and voodoo. It's remarkable that people who possess Divine Scripture would turn to such practices. Even today, we see this phenomenon among people who have access to the Quran yet seek protection through amulets, talismans with Quranic verses written backwards, or other superstitious practices. They might ask for something to tie around their arm for a particular outcome, or objects to place in someone's house to affect them—all manifestations of jibt.

The word الطَّـٰغُوتِ is particularly emphasized here. While طاغوت normally refers to those who rebel and call others to rebellion, this form الطَّـٰغُوتِ contains additional emphasis through its structure, indicating an especially powerful form of rebellion and misguidance.

What makes this even more troubling is that these people of the Book would say to the disbelievers—those who worship idols—هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَهْدَىٰ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ سَبِيلًا (these are more guided in their path than those who believe). They preferred idol worshipers over Muslims. We see echoes of this today when people say they'd rather deal with followers of any other faith than Muslims, declaring that Muslims worship the devil. They consider those who worship multiple gods or idols as closer to guidance than the followers of pure monotheism.

translation: Those are the ones whom Allah has cursed; and he whom Allah curses - never will you find for him a helper
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يَلْعَنِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ نَصِيرًا

For those who make such statements, Allah ﷻ provides a powerful response: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ (those are the ones that Allah has cursed). This teaches us an important lesson about how to respond to such attacks. We don't need to curse them out or become worked up about their statements—that's already taken care of by Allah ﷻ Himself. When faced with such hostility, we can simply remain composed, knowing that Allah ﷻ has taken these matters into His own hands.

The ayah continues: وَمَن يَلْعَنِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ نَصِيرًا (and whoever Allah curses, you will never find any helper for him whatsoever). Once Allah's curse descends upon someone, no earthly power can protect or help them. This should give believers peace of mind when facing hostility—ultimate justice rests with Allah ﷻ.

translation: Or have they a share of dominion? Then [if that were so], they would not give the people [even as much as] the speck on a date seed
commentary:
أَمْ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ فَإِذًا لَّا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ نَقِيرًا

Allah ﷻ now poses a rhetorical question: أَمْ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ (Do they have any portion in kingdom and dominion?). This addresses the historical reality of the Jewish people at the time of revelation. They had once enjoyed tremendous authority—during the time of Prophet Sulayman (AS), his dominion extended across the entire earth. But by the time of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, they had lost all political power and authority.

Allah ﷻ then reveals their true nature: فَإِذًا لَّا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ نَقِيرًا (if they did have dominion, they wouldn't even give people a naqeera). The word نَقِيرًا refers to the tiny speck that fits inside the groove of a date seed—something utterly insignificant. Allah ﷻ is saying that even if they had power, they would be so miserly and self-serving that they wouldn't give people even this minuscule amount. This very characteristic—their unwillingness to share or serve others—is why they don't have dominion. True leadership requires generosity and service to others, not extreme selfishness.

translation: Or do they envy people for what Allah has given them of His bounty? But we had already given the family of Abraham the Scripture and wisdom and conferred upon them a great kingdom
commentary:
أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ فَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ ءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا

The ayah identifies the root problem: أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ (Are they jealous of other people?). But notice the logic Allah ﷻ presents—they're jealous عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ (based on what Allah has given them out of His own favor). This reveals the absurdity of their jealousy. How can you be jealous over something that was never yours to begin with? Jealousy might make sense if something that was supposed to come to you went to someone else, but this bounty was never theirs—it's entirely Allah's favor to give to whomever He wills.

Allah ﷻ then reminds them: فَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ ءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ (We had given the lineage of Ibrahim the Book and wisdom). Even the legacy they once enjoyed wasn't truly theirs—it was a gift from Allah ﷻ to the descendants of Prophet Ibrahim (AS). The word آل (aal) refers to the family or lineage, and this legacy continues because the final message of Allah ﷻ remains within the Children of Ibrahim (AS) through Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who descends from Prophet Ismail (AS).

وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا (and We had given them a huge dominion)—tremendous rulership and authority. Everything they had was a gift from Allah ﷻ, so their jealousy over His giving prophethood to others is completely unfounded.

translation: And some among them believed in it, and some among them were averse to it. And sufficient is Hell as a blaze
commentary:
فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن صَدَّ عَنْهُ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِجَهَنَّمَ سَعِيرًا

Allah ﷻ acknowledges that even among the People of the Book, there will be variation: فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ (among them are those who believe in it). Some from the Jewish and Christian communities recognized the truth of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and embraced Islam. These individuals had the integrity to follow the truth when it became clear to them.

وَمِنْهُم مَّن صَدَّ عَنْهُ (and among them are those who obstruct from it). Others not only refused to believe themselves but actively worked to prevent others from accepting Islam. They became barriers to guidance, both for themselves and for those around them. Despite knowing in their hearts that this was the truth, they couldn't bring themselves to submit to it.

وَكَفَىٰ بِجَهَنَّمَ سَعِيرًا (and Jahannam is enough as a large flame). The word سَعِيرًا refers to large flames erupting from fire. For those who obstruct the truth despite recognizing it, Jahannam suffices as their recompense. This isn't a small fire—it's characterized by massive, consuming flames.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve in Our verses - We will drive them into a Fire. Every time their skins are roasted through We will replace them with other skins so they may taste the punishment. Indeed, Allah is ever Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا سَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِمْ نَارًا كُلَّمَا نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُم بَدَّلْنَـٰهُمْ جُلُودًا غَيْرَهَا لِيَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا

Allah ﷻ describes the punishment for those who disbelieved in His miraculous signs: سَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِمْ نَارًا (We will throw them into a fire). The verb نُصْلِيهِمْ comes from صلى, which means to cast something forcefully, like throwing something off a canyon or bridge. This isn't a gentle placement—it's a forceful casting into the Fire.

The description that follows is particularly vivid: كُلَّمَا نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُم بَدَّلْنَـٰهُمْ جُلُودًا غَيْرَهَا (every time their skins become uneven, We will replace them with skins other than those). The word نَضِجَتْ comes from a root meaning to be uneven or twisted. It also carries the meaning of being burnt, because when something burns, its shape becomes twisted and mangled.

Medical science has helped us understand the profound nature of this punishment. Once your skin is severely damaged or penetrated, you lose much of the pain sensation—it's the skin that contains the most sensitive pain receptors. Burn victims experience the most excruciating pain in their skin. Allah ﷻ doesn't allow the pain to end through the destruction of the skin. Every time one layer of skin is destroyed, He replaces it with fresh skin, لِيَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ (so they may continue to taste the punishment). The punishment doesn't diminish or fade—it remains constant and intense.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (Certainly Allah has always been the ultimate authority, all-wise). He is عَزِيزًا —the one with ultimate authority and power to carry out this punishment. And He is حَكِيمًا —all-wise, meaning this punishment is not arbitrary or excessive, but perfectly just and measured according to His perfect wisdom.

translation: But those who believe and do righteous deeds - We will admit them to gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide forever. For them therein are purified spouses, and We will admit them to deepening shade
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ لَّهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَنُدْخِلُهُمْ ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا

After describing the punishment of disbelievers, Allah ﷻ immediately presents the contrasting reward: وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (those who had iman and did righteous deeds). Notice it's not just belief, but belief coupled with righteous action.

سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ (We will soon enter them into gardens at the bottoms of which rivers are flowing). The imagery is of elevated gardens with rivers flowing beneath them—a vision of ultimate comfort and beauty. خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (they will remain in them forever)—this isn't temporary, it's eternal residence in Paradise.

لَّهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ (they will have in them purified spouses). The word مُّطَهَّرَةٌ is rich in meaning. On one level, it means clean and pure. But it also implies purified specifically to your taste—completely compatible with you. Allah ﷻ describes this further in Surah Ar-Rahman with the word حِسَان, meaning beautiful in every way you could desire.

This is significant because in this life, no matter how much you love your spouse, there are always imperfections. You love your wife, but there are things that get under your skin. You love your husband, but there are traits you wish were different. This is human nature—we can't possibly be exactly to the liking of someone else in every single way. Your parents aren't exactly the way you want them, and you're not exactly what your parents hoped for. There's always something: "I wish you didn't get angry so much," or "I wish this was different." 

But the spouses of Paradise are مُّطَهَّرَةٌ—purified and cleansed of everything you might not like. They're exactly the way you want them in every single aspect, and this perfection doesn't fade after the first few days like the honeymoon phase of earthly marriage. It's permanent and complete compatibility.

وَنُدْخِلُهُمْ ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا (and We will enter them into shade upon shade). This isn't just الظِّلّ (shade), but ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا —cool, comfortable shade layered upon shade. They'll be in perpetual comfort, never too hot, never uncomfortable, always in perfect, cool shade.

translation: Indeed, Allah commands you to render trusts to whom they are due and when you judge between people to judge with justice. Excellent is that which Allah instructs you. Indeed, Allah is ever Hearing and Seeing
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تُؤَدُّوا۟ ٱلْأَمَـٰنَـٰتِ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا وَإِذَا حَكَمْتُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ أَن تَحْكُمُوا۟ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا

Now the Quran shifts to universal principles that form the foundation of Islamic governance and law. These aren't just abstract ideals—they represent the essence of Sharia, the spirit of Islamic law. When people think of Sharia law today, particularly non-Muslims, they often imagine only the penal aspects. But the true essence of Islamic law begins here, with these fundamental principles of justice and trust.

In any system of governance, there are three branches: executive, legislative and judicial. These ayahs will address all three, starting with the executive branch—who should be given positions of authority and how they should execute their responsibilities.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تُؤَدُّوا۟ ٱلْأَمَـٰنَـٰتِ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا (Certainly Allah commands you that you deliver trusts to those who deserve them). The word أَمَـٰنَـٰت refers to trusts, responsibilities, positions of authority—anything entrusted to someone. Allah ﷻ commands that these be given إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا (to their rightful people)—to those who actually deserve them and can handle them properly.

This principle applies from the smallest organizational level to the highest positions of government. Hire someone who deserves to be hired. Give money to someone who deserves to receive it. Provide an opportunity to someone who has earned that opportunity. Offer education to those who deserve access to it. Elect governors and leaders who actually deserve to be in those positions—not based on family connections, tribal affiliations or wealth, but on merit and qualification.

What characterizes corrupt governments? Often it's nepotism—my uncle becomes the minister of this department, my nephew becomes governor of that province. Family connections determine who gets power, not competence or character. This is why hereditary monarchies can be among the worst forms of government. Everyone in the ruling family lives in luxury while everyone else suffers, because it's not about who deserves the position or can handle the trust—it's simply about bloodline.

Even in so-called democracies, corruption manifests differently but with similar results. People who have money can finance campaigns and make their way into office whether they're rightfully deserving or not. Lobbyists and special interest groups can put candidates forward, run demonizing ads against opponents, and manipulate their way to power. The people who actually deserve leadership positions may never get the opportunity because they lack financial backing.

Consider this: if Prophet Lut (AS) tried to run for election, he would probably lose, while the people of his corrupt city would probably win. But who truly deserves to lead? The principle of أَمَـٰنَـٰتِ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا cuts through all this corruption.

This connects to a question the Israelites once asked about Prophet Talut (AS): "He hasn't been given abundant wealth—how can he run a campaign? We don't put people like that in positions of power." But true public service means exactly that—public service. If someone has accumulated vast wealth, it often means they've been in private service to themselves, focused on personal gain. If they don't have a history of charitable giving and selfless service, why would we expect them to suddenly become selfless leaders?

There's a strange contradiction in modern thinking. Some argue, "This person has been CEO of many companies and made millions—he's the perfect one to run our country." But consider: becoming a CEO requires climbing a corporate ladder, which often demands cutting corners, throwing people under the bus and displaying tremendous personal ambition and greed. These are almost opposite to the qualities needed for genuine public service. Yet society considers ruthless corporate success as qualifying someone to lead a nation out of its problems.

The Islamic principle is clear: from top to bottom in Muslim society, we give positions of governance—whether it's leading prayer, teaching, counseling, or any form of community leadership—to those who actually deserve them based on character, competence and commitment to service.

وَإِذَا حَكَمْتُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ أَن تَحْكُمُوا۟ بِٱلْعَدْلِ (and when you make decisions between people, that you make those decisions with justice). This addresses the judicial branch. But notice the sequence: Allah ﷻ first addresses who should be in positions of authority, then how they should rule. This is intentional—the second can never happen properly if the first isn't implemented. You cannot have just decision-making if the wrong people are making the decisions.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦٓ (How excellent is the advice He is giving to you). The word نِعِمَّا expresses how awesome and beautiful this counsel is. If we truly understood the transformative power of these principles—placing the right people in positions of authority and ensuring they judge with justice—we would realize how they could completely change our societies.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (Certainly Allah has been all-hearing, all-seeing). This is especially relevant when it comes to people holding office. During campaigns and public appearances, they present one face, but in private they show a different character. We see this when politicians get caught on candid camera revealing their true attitudes, and their campaigns collapse. 

Allah ﷻ is telling us that while we should do our best to identify the right people for positions of authority, only He is سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا—fully hearing everything they say in private and fully seeing everything they do behind closed doors. We don't know everything about people, but Allah ﷻ does. To the best of our ability, we must try to discern who truly deserves positions of trust, knowing that ultimate knowledge rests with Allah ﷻ.

translation: O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those in authority among you. And if you disagree over anything, refer it to Allah and the Messenger, if you should believe in Allah and the Last Day. That is the best [way] and best in result
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ ۖ فَإِن تَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا

Now we come to the legislative branch—where does law come from? يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ (O those of you who have iman, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those in authority among you).

There's something remarkable about the grammar here that reveals a crucial principle. Notice that the command "obey" (أَطِيعُوا۟) appears twice, not three times. In typical Arabic, when listing three things, you either say the verb once at the beginning ("Obey Allah, the Messenger and those in authority") or repeat it three times for emphasis ("Obey Allah and obey the Messenger and obey those in authority"). But here, Allah ﷻ uses an unusual structure—He says "obey" twice.

This grammatical choice makes a profound point: the first two sources of obedience are absolute and unconditional—obey Allah and obey the Messenger. The third is relative and conditional. When the verb isn't repeated for أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ (those in authority), it signals that obedience to them is not on the same level as obedience to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

The obedience to those in authority is qualified by the phrase مِنكُمْ (from among you). They must be from among you—they represent you, they're accountable to you. The word مِن also carries the meaning of representation. Leadership cannot simply be imposed from outside; it must emerge from the community and represent its interests.

This establishes the legislative framework: law comes first from Allah ﷻ and the Messenger ﷺ. In most circumstances, these two sources provide clear guidance. When they don't explicitly address a particular situation, then we turn to أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ—those possessing authority from among you—to make decisions within the framework established by Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

These principles—executive, judicial and legislative—form the framework for any successful society, whether explicitly Islamic or not. The values themselves are timeless, though how they materialize in practice will depend on the era and circumstances. Some people call for returning to the exact governmental structure of the time of the Khulafa Ar-Rashideen (the Rightly Guided Caliphs), but we must recognize that the world has changed dramatically. Economics has changed, sociology has evolved, the structure of governments has transformed, and international relations operate differently.

Even among the Rightly Guided Caliphs themselves—Abu Bakr (RA), Umar (RA), Uthman (RA) and Ali (RA)—we see that governmental structures underwent dramatic changes. The way authority was exercised, how decisions were made, even legislative approaches evolved between these leaders. We're now centuries removed from that time. The values and principles don't change, but their practical application must be appropriate to the contemporary context.

Modern Muslim scholars and writers have begun exploring how these timeless principles can take shape in modern governance. Insha'Allah, our next generation of Muslims will include political scientists and thinkers who can combine the latest advances in political science with Islamic principles, giving us a contemporary framework for implementing these teachings.

فَإِن تَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ (And if you fall into a dispute about anything, then return that matter back to Allah and the Messenger). This is like having a constitution. When there's a constitutional disagreement, you take it back to the letter of the law and debate it properly.

Imagine an Islamic Supreme Court where two parties are arguing. One side presents their evidence from the Quran and Sunnah, and the other side presents their evidence from the Quran and Sunnah. Both are claiming to represent the true Islamic position. At that point, if the judge is fair, he will consult his panel of experts, examine whose evidence is stronger and more sound, and pass a verdict.

At the end of the day, even though both sides claimed to represent Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, there's a third entity—أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ (those in authority from among you)—and you'll have to follow one of those verdicts. We can't each maintain our own interpretation indefinitely when it affects collective matters. For issues that impact the community, we must eventually return to some form of judicial authority that can make binding decisions based on the sources of Islam.

إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ (if in fact you are believers in Allah and the Last Day). This frames the entire discussion—if you truly believe in Allah ﷻ and accountability in the Hereafter, then you'll submit to this process of returning disputes to the sources of revelation and accepting authoritative rulings based on them.

ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا (That is better and more beautiful in terms of long-term consequences). The word تَأْوِيلًا doesn't just mean interpretation—it also carries the meaning of ultimate outcome or long-term result. When Allah ﷻ discusses good business practices in the Quran, He sometimes uses this same concept of أَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا—if you don't cheat a customer today, they'll return tomorrow. It's better for you long-term, it's sustainable.

The same principle applies here. If you comply with these laws—placing the right people in authority, ensuring they judge with justice, and returning disputes to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ—it's not only good for you immediately, it's sustainable. It will benefit society in the long run. These aren't just idealistic principles; they're the foundation for building societies that endure and flourish across generations.

translation: Have you not seen those who claim to have believed in what was revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what was revealed before you? They wish to refer legislation to Taghut, while they were commanded to reject it; and Satan wishes to lead them far astray
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَقَدْ أُمِرُوٓا۟ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَيُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُضِلَّهُمْ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا

Have you looked towards those who are very confident in their assumption? The word يَزْعُمُونَ carries this meaning of a confident assumption. These are people who claim with certainty that they believe in what was sent down to the Prophet ﷺ and what was sent down before him. Yet at the same time, they want to resort their decisions to Taghut, to a rebellious entity that opposes Allah's ﷻ authority. They seek someone other than the Messenger ﷺ to make decisions for them, despite having already been commanded to disbelieve in that rebellious entity.

Shaytan wants to mislead them as far away as possible, into ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا - a far-off misguidance. To understand what this phrase truly means, imagine someone traveling on a road who is supposed to stay on course. If someone gives you bad directions and you get lost, but only slightly off course, you might find your way back. But if you're way off course, you end up going around in circles and never finding your path again. This is what Shaytan desires. A slight wrong turn is recoverable, but being way off course means perpetual wandering without direction.

The historical context of this ayah involves a severe incident from the life of the Prophet ﷺ. There was a Muslim, by most accounts considered a hypocrite, who got into an argument with a Jew about property and assets. They went to the Prophet ﷺ for a verdict. The hypocrite assumed the Prophet ﷺ would side with him as a fellow Muslim, but the Prophet ﷺ gave the verdict in favor of the Jew. The hypocrite rejected this, saying he wanted a second opinion.

They went to Umar (RA), and the hypocrite asked him to pass judgment. Umar (RA) asked if they had already gone to the Messenger ﷺ. When they confirmed they had but weren't satisfied with the verdict, Umar (RA) went back, grabbed his sword, and killed the hypocrite, declaring that whoever is not satisfied with the verdict of the Messenger ﷺ deserves this fate.

This created an uproar. The family of the deceased came to the Messenger ﷺ demanding justice, arguing that a Muslim brother had been killed. It was in this context that these ayahs were revealed, explaining that these people wanted to get decisions made by those who rebel against Allah ﷻ. They would keep shopping for verdicts until they found one they liked, which is exactly what Shaytan wants - to mislead them as far as possible from the truth.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Come to what Allah has revealed and to the Messenger," you see the hypocrites turning away from you in aversion
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ رَأَيْتَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنكَ صُدُودًا

When it is said to them, "Come towards what Allah ﷻ has sent down and come to the Messenger ﷺ," you will see the hypocrites obstruct and hesitate at every step. They show extreme reluctance in coming to the Prophet ﷺ for judgment. They ask, "Can we go somewhere else? Can we find another option?" They obstruct themselves and others from accepting the Messenger's ﷺ authority.

This ayah describes what Umar (RA) witnessed. Through this incident, we learn about the special relationship Umar (RA) had with Allah ﷻ. There are multiple instances in the Quran where this special relationship is highlighted, and this is one of them. What's remarkable is that Umar (RA) did not have explicit permission from the Messenger ﷺ to do what he did. He himself didn't know if his action was right or wrong - he had an impulse and acted on it. He was waiting for the final instruction, wondering how the Messenger ﷺ would pass verdict on this situation. Allah ﷻ Himself passed the verdict, and that's how the matter was resolved.

translation: So how [will it be] when disaster strikes them because of what their hands have put forth and then they come to you swearing by Allah, "We intended nothing but good conduct and accommodation
commentary:
فَكَيْفَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ جَآءُوكَ يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْسَـٰنًا وَتَوْفِيقًا

How would it be when a calamity hits them because of what they themselves earned? The word مُّصِيبَةٌۢ refers to something that was bound to hit them as a consequence of their own actions. Whose fault is it? According to this ayah, it's theirs alone.

Then they come to the Prophet ﷺ, swearing by Allah ﷻ, claiming, "We were only looking for the best outcome. We were only looking for some blessing, some good thing to come out of this. That's why we sought another verdict. You can't just execute us for this!" They're complaining loudly to the Messenger ﷺ, but the Messenger ﷺ doesn't know what's truly in their hearts. Who does? Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: Those are the ones of whom Allah knows what is in their hearts, so turn away from them but admonish them and speak to them a far-reaching word
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَعِظْهُمْ وَقُل لَّهُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًۢا بَلِيغًا

Allah ﷻ informs His Messenger ﷺ: those are the people whose hearts I know. They're speaking loudly with you, but let Me tell you what's actually going on inside their hearts. Then Allah ﷻ gives three instructions on how to handle them.

First, فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ - ignore them. Let them complain and cry, but don't engage with their emotional manipulation. 

Second, وَعِظْهُمْ - give them counsel, give them advice. Don't just dismiss them; there's still an opportunity for their guidance.

Third, وَقُل لَّهُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًۢا بَلِيغًا - say to them within their own midst, in their own groups when nobody else is watching, a word that is بَلِيغًا, a word that penetrates deep inside their hearts. Make a special session for them where you give them private advice on how they should respond to the situation.

This approach shows divine wisdom. A family member had been killed, emotions were running high. People in such states don't think clearly and don't want to be around others. So Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to call a special meeting with them and deliver words that truly penetrate their hearts, hoping some good will come from it.

translation: And We did not send any messenger except to be obeyed by permission of Allah. And if, when they wronged themselves, they had come to you, [O Muhammad], and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful
commentary:
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذ ظَّلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ جَآءُوكَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابًا رَّحِيمًا

We never sent any messenger at all except for the purpose that he should be obeyed by Allah's ﷻ permission. This is the fundamental reason for messengership - obedience to the Messenger is obedience to Allah ﷻ.

Had these people, when they wronged themselves - notice that Allah ﷻ emphasizes they didn't wrong the Prophet ﷺ, they wronged themselves - had they come to the Prophet ﷺ in the right way, asked Allah ﷻ for forgiveness, and had the Messenger ﷺ asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah ﷻ to be التَّوَّابَ الرَّحِيمَ - accepting of repentance and always merciful.

In other words, these complainers are now in serious trouble. Had they approached the situation correctly from the beginning, they would have found Allah ﷻ accepting of their repentance and merciful toward them. This ayah teaches us how sensitive Islam is to the idea that the Messenger ﷺ must be obeyed. There are no ifs, ands, or buts about it. Once he passes a verdict, that's the end of the discussion. This is the central concept that Umar (RA) understood so deeply. The next ayah elaborates on this same crucial lesson.

translation: But no, by your Lord, they will not [truly] believe until they make you, [O Muhammad], judge concerning that over which they dispute among themselves and then find within themselves no discomfort from what you have judged and submit in [full, willing] submission
commentary:
فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا

Before we examine the content of this ayah, we must appreciate its form. Throughout the Quran, Allah ﷻ swears by various things - the sun, the moon, time, the fig. These are magnificent creations, and when Allah ﷻ takes an oath by them, we understand the gravity of what follows. But this is one of the rare occasions in the Quran where Allah ﷻ swears by Himself.

First, the taking of an oath itself isn't done in casual situations - it indicates something of tremendous importance. Second, when the object of the oath is Allah ﷻ Himself rather than His creation, we know this subject matter is of ultimate significance. Third, even when swearing by Himself, Allah ﷻ honored His Messenger ﷺ by putting him in the oath: فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ - "No, by your Master!"

When Allah ﷻ swears by something this weighty, whatever follows admits no doubt whatsoever. If there's anything in the Quran about which there can be absolutely no uncertainty, this is it.

What is the statement? لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - they have no Iman. Allah ﷻ chooses to swear by Himself while describing a group of people, declaring they have no faith. This means these are the people furthest from Iman imaginable. That Allah ﷻ would swear they have no faith indicates the most severe distance from true belief.

Who are these unfortunate souls? حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ - until they make you, the Prophet ﷺ, the decision maker. Notice the precision of the Quranic language here. Allah ﷻ didn't say "until they make the Quran the decision maker." He could have said "until they make what We have sent down to you the decision maker," but He didn't. The direct object is clear: you, Muhammad ﷺ, must be made the decision maker.

Some people deny the validity of the Sunnah, claiming the Messenger's ﷺ job was merely to deliver the message and that his personal practices aren't binding. They say, "You like French fries, I like mashed potatoes - it's fine. His preferences aren't binding on us, only the Quran is." But this ayah makes crystal clear that the decision maker isn't just the Quran here - it's the Prophet ﷺ himself. He executes the verdict of the Quran. The word تُحَكِّمُوكَ leaves no room for this interpretation. Allah ﷻ could have said "they must rule by the Book of Allah" - He says this elsewhere in the Quran - but here the entire matter was about bypassing the Prophet ﷺ, and Allah ﷻ makes clear: you cannot do that.

فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ - whatever sprouts between them, whatever new issue suddenly comes up that wasn't there before. Who is qualified to handle it? The Messenger ﷺ of Allah ﷻ. They must make him the decision maker in every issue that arises among them.

But even making him the decision maker isn't enough for true Iman. ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا - then they should not find deep within themselves any discomfort, any tightness in the chest, any reservation whatsoever مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ - regarding whatever verdict you passed. 

Allah ﷻ is saying: I swear by Myself that if they even find themselves uncomfortable with the decision the Messenger ﷺ makes, they don't have Iman. The oath applies to this internal state as well.

Finally, وَيُسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا - they must submit with complete submission. The word تَسْلِيمًا is the emphatic, hyperbolic form of إِسْلَام. It's not just submission - it's absolute, total, unconditional submission. They must give themselves completely and utterly in submission to the verdict of Allah's ﷻ Messenger ﷺ.

This powerful ayah establishes two non-negotiable principles: First, we must have absolute, unconditional obedience to the verdicts of the Messenger ﷺ. Second, we must trust completely that whatever verdict he gives, whatever ruling he passes, is in our best interest.

translation: And if We had decreed upon them, "Kill yourselves" or "Leave your homes," they would not have done it, except for a few of them. But if they had done what they were instructed, it would have been better for them and a firmer position [for them in faith]
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ أَوِ ٱخْرُجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِكُم مَّا فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا۟ مَا يُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتًا

This ayah presents a hypothetical scenario to drive home the principle of complete trust in Allah's ﷻ commands. Imagine if Allah ﷻ had ordained something even more extreme to this group of complainers. What if His verdict had been: "All of you, kill yourselves" or "Leave your homes - they're now confiscated as a result of how you've spoken"?

Think about what's being described here. These are two of the most irrational commands imaginable. On one end of the spectrum of rationality is killing yourself - the ultimate irrational act. On the other end is leaving your home to become homeless despite having a home - highly irrational. Allah ﷻ deliberately chooses these two extremes to make His point.

مَّا فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِّنْهُمْ - they wouldn't have done it except for very few among them. Most people, faced with such seemingly irrational commands, would refuse.

But then comes the powerful statement: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا۟ مَا يُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ - had they actually done what they were advised to do, without questioning, without thinking twice, just done it لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ - it would have been better for them.

That seemingly crazy thing would have been better for them. Why? On what basis can we say this? Because it's coming from Allah ﷻ. Whatever comes from Him can only be better for us, regardless of how it appears to our limited understanding.

وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتًا - and it would have been more firm and solid as a decision. The word تَثْبِيتًا relates to something provable, something well-thought-out, something that makes sense at the deepest level. That seemingly irrational command would actually have been the most valid, most sound thing to do, only because it came from Allah ﷻ.

This teaching isn't merely theoretical. History provides evidence. When Musa (AS) was told to command his people to kill their own for their sin of worshipping the calf, was that not a seemingly irrational command? When they were told to leave Egypt, was that not difficult? Yet in both cases, it was absolutely better for them.

These principles touch on the deeper purposes of Shariah. The first goal of Islamic law is to establish justice - to give people what they rightfully deserve. Whether it's positions of power, shares in inheritance, the rights of a wife or husband, rights and dignity to all people - justice comes first. That's why two-thirds of the Makkan Quran deals with matters of social justice: baby girls being killed unjustly, people being cheated in business, orphans being pushed around.

When these foundational matters of justice are established, Allah ﷻ beautifies society further by revealing laws that prevent falling back into injustice. When we think of Islamic punitive laws, we must understand they are means of protecting a society that's already abiding by justice. 

The problem in much of the Muslim world today is that we have no foundation of justice. When you think of injustice, you think of parts of the Muslim world. When you think of racism, Muslims excel at it - Punjabi versus Sindhi, Gujarati versus Urdu-speaking, village rivalries in Africa. We are deeply divided. Yet all we think about when we hear "Shariah" are the punishments - cutting off hands, stoning. Those punishments exist for a society where the justice and services of Islam are already in place, where all facilities have been provided, yet someone deliberately circumvents them all to commit a crime.

Remember the example of the woman who wasn't culturally acclimated yet and made a mistake. What kind of punishment did she receive? None of the harsh penalties, because she hadn't been properly taught yet. She was in transition. If Islam demanded radical, grotesque changes in society overnight, it wouldn't have lasted. Radical changes come fast and go fast. Sustainable changes are built on principles of justice, implemented gradually and wisely. This is the way of Allah ﷻ, and this is what these ayahs ultimately teach us about trust, obedience and the wisdom behind divine legislation.

translation: And then We would have given them from Us a great reward
commentary:
**وَإِذًا لَّـَٔاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّن لَّدُنَّآ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا**

*And then We would have given them from Us a great reward.*

This ayah begins with وَإِذًا - "and then" - a linguistic connector that ties directly back to ayah 66. To fully appreciate what Allah ﷻ is telling us here, we need to understand the context established in the previous ayah.

In ayah 66, Allah ﷻ presents a powerful hypothetical scenario: "If We had decreed upon them, 'Kill yourselves' or 'Leave your homes,' they would not have done it, except for a few of them. But if they had done what they were instructed, it would have been better for them and a firmer position." 

This hypothetical instruction seems completely irrational at first glance - why would Allah ﷻ command people to kill themselves or leave their homes? Yet there's profound wisdom embedded here about obedience and trust in divine guidance. The message is clear: even if Allah ﷻ were to command something that doesn't immediately make sense to us, compliance would still be better for us. We don't need to fully comprehend the wisdom behind every instruction before we can trust that it will benefit us.

But this isn't merely a theoretical discussion. There's a historical dimension that the early Muslims would have immediately recognized. These exact commands were actually given to Bani Isra'il. When they worshiped the golden calf, they were physically commanded to kill themselves as atonement. Later, they were commanded to leave their homes and fight in Allah's path. In both instances, they were reluctant and skeptical. The Muslims are being reminded: these kinds of difficult instructions have been given before to previous communities, and those who hesitated suffered serious consequences.

The deeper subtext here connects to one of the central themes of Surah An-Nisa: the intertwining of legal rulings and hypocrisy. Throughout this surah, Allah ﷻ alternates between establishing legal principles and addressing hypocrisy - the two main subjects of Medinan revelation. This pattern isn't random. We've already seen this connection explored in depth in Surah Al-Baqarah, where a nation received numerous laws but also displayed tremendous hypocrisy. Bani Isra'il serves as the case study: what happens when a community receives clear divine legislation but treats it casually or with insincerity?

Now it's the Muslim community's turn. The message is: take every instruction seriously. Don't be like those who came before and were reluctant to comply.

The ayah continues with Allah's promise: وَإِذًا لَّـَٔاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّن لَّدُنَّآ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - "And then We would have given them from Us a great reward." The phrase مِّن لَّدُنَّآ - "from Us" - carries special weight. It's not just any reward, but one that comes directly from Allah ﷻ Himself.

This promise of أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - tremendous compensation - echoes the guarantee of victory that Allah ﷻ gave to Bani Isra'il had they simply complied with His commands. Later in the Quran, we'll see this same principle at the Battle of Badr, where Allah ﷻ guaranteed victory regardless of which route the Muslims chose - whether they pursued the trade caravan or faced the well-armed Quraysh army. The guarantee was absolute: comply with My command, and I will deliver on My promise.

But because Bani Isra'il didn't comply, they ended up wandering in the desert for forty years - the diaspora period. That same offer of tremendous reward is now being extended to the Muslims. Will they respond differently?

translation: And We would have guided them to a straight path
commentary:
**وَلَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا**

*And We would have guided them to a straight path.*

This beautiful, concise statement builds directly on the previous promise. وَلَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا - "And truly We would have guided them to and through a straight path."

The word صِرَٰطًا - path - in the Quran doesn't refer merely to spiritual guidance in an abstract sense. It encompasses something much more comprehensive. A path is made up of milestones, of specific steps you must take. You reach this stage, then move to the next stage, then to the next. 

When Allah ﷻ speaks of guiding a community along a straight path, He's offering them a sense of direction as a collective nation. This isn't just about individual spiritual growth - though that's certainly included - but about a community that knows where it's headed, that has clarity about its mission and purpose.

If the Muslims as a collective body start trusting Allah ﷻ and following what He instructs, they would gain both guidance and a clear sense of direction together. This is guidance that shows you not just where to be, but how to get there, step by step.

The connection to Surah Al-Fatihah becomes luminous here. In the Fatihah, we ask Allah ﷻ for guidance: اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ - "Guide us to the straight path." After asking for guidance, we immediately specify: صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ - "The path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor." 

Here in Surah An-Nisa, Allah ﷻ says He would have guided them along the straight path, and then immediately in the next ayah, He describes who travels this path: those He has blessed. The thematic connection is breathtaking - the Quran interpreting itself, showing us how our daily prayer connects to these eternal principles.

translation: And whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger - those will be with the ones upon whom Allah has bestowed favor of the prophets, the steadfast affirmers of truth, the martyrs and the righteous. And excellent are those as companions
commentary:
**وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَٱلصِّدِّيقِينَ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءِ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ ۚ وَحَسُنَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ رَفِيقًا**

*And whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger - those will be with the ones upon whom Allah has bestowed favor from among the prophets, the steadfast affirmers of truth, the martyrs and the righteous. And excellent are those as companions.*

This ayah is considered by virtually every mufassir (Quranic commentator) to be the explanation of the phrase from Surah Al-Fatihah: صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ - "The path of those You have favored." Who are they? This ayah answers that question definitively.

But notice how the ayah begins. Allah ﷻ puts guidance in immediately practical terms: وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ - "Whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger."

The word وَٱلرَّسُولَ - "and the Messenger" - is critical here. The stronger understanding is that the و here serves as clarification (بيان), not addition. In other words, it's not describing two separate acts of obedience - sometimes you obey Allah, other times you obey the Messenger. Rather, it's clarifying what it means to obey Allah. 

Obeying Allah ﷻ does not happen except through the authorization and teaching of the Messenger ﷺ. He is the means by which we discover what obedience to Allah ﷻ actually looks like. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere in the Quran: "Whoever obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah" (4:80). They're one and the same.

This concept is fundamental to our faith. When we ask Allah ﷻ for guidance, He doesn't deliver it directly to each individual. He sends a messenger. He doesn't just deliver a book and leave us to figure it out. He delivers it to a messenger who explains it, demonstrates it, and shows us how to live it. We've already covered the passages in this surah that highlight the messenger's role: يُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ - to clarify for them and purify them.

The concept of obedience (طاعة) deserves careful reflection. Why does Allah ﷻ emphasize obedience so strongly throughout the Quran? We're constantly told: hear and obey. But there's tremendous internal struggle that can prevent obedience. Part of that struggle involves accepting a higher authority - accepting that someone has the right to tell us what to do.

Some people say, "I believe in God. I don't need any messengers or intermediaries. I can have a direct relationship with God myself." But this reasoning is fundamentally flawed. What it really means is: "I don't want to answer to any human being. I don't want to accept anyone's authority over me." It's a problem with authority itself. Because if you claim to answer to God directly, then ultimately you get to determine what God wants - and where do you get that understanding from? From the messenger. There's no escaping it.

The relationship we have with the Messenger ﷺ is, in practical terms, the most authoritative relationship in existence. Consider: it's even more comprehensive than the relationship between a master and slave. A slave must obey his master, but he's not obligated to love him. He doesn't have to go out of his way when the master isn't watching. 

But with the Messenger ﷺ? He's our role model in everything we do. The minimum he requires of us, and the best we can possibly achieve, is to emulate him more and more in every aspect of our lives. And we're obligated to love him - love him more than we love ourselves. This is an incredible relationship of complete submission to the Sunnah of the Messenger ﷺ.

That's why in ayah 65, Allah ﷻ says: فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ - "But no, by your Lord, they will not believe until they make you the judge in all disputes that arise between them." Then the ayah continues: ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيمًا - "Then they find no discomfort within themselves over what you have decided and submit in complete submission."

Notice that last part - complete submission to everything the Messenger ﷺ decides - wasn't even explicitly mentioned in the requirement. It's assumed. Our submission to the instructions of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is inherent in what it means to accept guidance. This doesn't mean we worship the messenger. But we understand that following the messenger is, to us practically, the following of Allah ﷻ.

فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم - "Those are the ones with those Allah has favored."

The word مَعَ - "with" - is beautiful here. It doesn't just mean "counted among" in some abstract registry. The word مَعَ in Arabic implies companionship, camaraderie, a sense of oneness. When we say "we're with you in spirit" or "we stand with our brothers and sisters who are suffering" - that's the meaning of ma'a. 

Allah ﷻ is saying: these people who obey Allah and His Messenger, they share a bond, they have a oneness with all those who came before them - all those ancient historical figures. You're connected to them. You belong to this group now.

Then Allah ﷻ presents a hierarchy of those He has favored: مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ - "from among the prophets."

At the top are the prophets (النَّبِيِّينَ). The opportunity to be a prophet is obviously closed - prophethood ended with Muhammad ﷺ.

وَٱلصِّدِّيقِينَ - "And the Siddiqin."

The word صِدِّيق is an intensive form - it describes someone who is perpetually, consistently, extremely truthful. These are people who remain committed to the truth no matter what circumstances they face. 

These are specifically the people who accepted the call of messengers when there was no other reinforcement whatsoever. Think about it: sometimes we attend a program or a khutbah where there are many Muslims present. You feel energy in the crowd, you're motivated by the collective spirit. When someone takes shahada in that environment, there's this wave of positive reinforcement.

But when a messenger first gives his call to Islam, especially in the early days, there's zero reinforcement. None. You have no encouragement from anywhere. Actually, all you face is deterrence - social pressure, threats, persecution. Despite all of that, you still cling to the truth. These are the Siddiqin.

The opportunity to be a prophet may be gone, but the opportunity to be a Siddiq - to accept and uphold the truth despite all pressure against it - remains available to every generation.

وَٱلشُّهَدَآءِ - "And the Shuhada."

The word شُهَدَاء means both "witnesses" and "martyrs." These are people who bear witness to the truth with their entire being. Their life itself becomes a testimony that they were committed to the truth. In the ultimate sense, they bear witness by sacrificing their very lives.

وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ - "And the righteous."

At the bottom of this list - last but certainly not least - are the صَّـٰلِحِينَ, the righteous. These are the broad category of people who simply strive to do what's right, to live uprightly before Allah ﷻ.

Notice that these are presented as categories one should aspire to join. But all of them share one essential starting point: they all strived to obey Allah ﷻ and therefore obey the Messenger ﷺ. That's where the ayah began, and we can't lose sight of that foundation.

We can't look for what it means to be righteous, to be a witness to truth, to be steadfast - we can't look for any of these qualities anywhere outside the framework of obedience to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. That's the starting point. How it progresses into these wonderful stations - from Islam to Iman to Ihsan - that comes later. But the foundation is obedience.

وَحَسُنَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ رَفِيقًا - "And excellent are those as companions."

The verb حَسُنَ is used to express amazement and admiration. How excellent, how wonderful they are!

The word رَفِيقًا - commonly translated as "friend" or "companion" - requires deeper understanding. Arabic has many words for friend: صديق , حميم , رفيق. Each carries distinct nuances.

رَفِيق comes from the same root as رِفَادَة - a pillow, something soft you can recline on, a source of comfort. A rafiq is a friend you can rely on, someone you can literally lean on. When you're around them, you become relaxed. You calm down. They're a source of tranquility in your life. That's the companion called a rafiq.

Allah ﷻ is saying: all these people - the prophets, the truthful ones, the martyrs, the righteous - all of them throughout history will be your rafiq. You can turn to them, draw comfort from them, find peace in their company. This is the companionship you get to enjoy in the eternal abode.

May Allah ﷻ grant us all that blessed company.

translation: That is the bounty from Allah, and sufficient is Allah as Knower
commentary:
**ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَضْلُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ عَلِيمًا**

*That is the bounty from Allah, and sufficient is Allah as Knower.*

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَضْلُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ - "That is the ultimate favor from Allah."

The word فَضْل means favor, bounty, grace - something given freely that wasn't earned or deserved. After describing this incredible promise - that those who obey will be in the company of prophets, the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous - Allah ﷻ reminds us: this entire arrangement, this whole system of guidance and reward, this is fadl. This is pure grace from Allah ﷻ.

We don't earn our way into this companionship through our deeds alone. Our deeds matter, yes - obedience matters - but ultimately it's Allah's overwhelming generosity that places us in such exalted company.

وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ عَلِيمًا - "And sufficient is Allah as Knower."

The phrase كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ - "sufficient is Allah" - is powerful. Allah ﷻ is absolutely sufficient, completely adequate in His knowledge.

Knowledge of what? The ayah leaves it open, which makes it comprehensive. Allah ﷻ knows where you stand. He knows what's in your heart. Now that you've heard this call to obedience, now that you understand what's at stake and what's promised, how has that impacted you? What are you going to do with this knowledge?

Allah ﷻ knows. He knows whether you'll be among those who comply or those who hesitate. He knows whether this message will transform your relationship with His commands or whether you'll continue treating divine guidance casually.

The ayah ends with this profound reminder: you can't hide your response from Allah ﷻ. He is عَلِيمًا - the All-Knowing. Your internal state, your level of commitment, your sincerity in obedience - all of it is completely transparent before Him.

And that knowledge isn't threatening - it's actually liberating. Because the same Allah ﷻ who knows your struggles also knows your sincere efforts. The same One who knows when you fall short also knows when you're genuinely trying. His knowledge is sufficient. You don't need to prove yourself to anyone else. Just focus on sincere obedience to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, and trust that Allah's knowledge of your true state is enough.

translation: O you who have believed, take your precaution and [either] go forth in companies or go forth all together
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ خُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ فَٱنفِرُوا۟ ثُبَاتٍ أَوِ ٱنفِرُوا۟ جَمِيعًا

This ayah marks an immediate transition to military instructions for the Muslim community. We're now in the fourth year after Hijrah, a period when both major battles and smaller skirmishes were occurring regularly. The Muslims found themselves in a constant state of conflict—not just the large confrontations like Badr and Uhud, but ongoing smaller engagements that kept the community perpetually on guard.

The connection to the previous passage about obedience to the Messenger ﷺ becomes clear when we understand that battlefield loyalty represents the ultimate test of obedience. The leading hypocrites of Madinah would often stand in the first row during prayer—prayer wasn't where their hypocrisy showed most clearly. But the battlefield? That's where true loyalty revealed itself. That's where the question became stark: Are you willing to give up your life?

Allah ﷻ begins: "O you who believe, hold onto your caution." The Arabic خُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ means to stay on high alert—code red, all the time. A society in a state of war operates fundamentally differently from one at peace. Even when not actively fighting, precautions must remain constant. Citizens must be vigilant, ready, prepared for what might come at any moment.

After establishing this defensive posture—this state of constant readiness—Allah ﷻ moves to offensive strategy: فَٱنفِرُوا۟ ثُبَاتٍ أَوِ ٱنفِرُوا۟ جَمِيعًا "march forward in small bands, or march forward altogether." The word نَفَر means to come forward to meet an enemy, to advance with purpose. The term ثُبَات refers to small, elite groups of soldiers—perhaps fewer than ten—highly skilled fighters who never miss their mark. Think of specialized mission forces sent on covert operations. This represents one military strategy.

The alternative? جَمِيعًا —everyone coming out together in full force, the entire army engaging in all-out war. Both approaches have their place in military strategy. What matters is that you're prepared not just to defend, but to take action, to advance, to meet the challenge head-on.
