translation: And indeed, there is among you he who lingers behind; and if disaster strikes you, he says, "Allah has favored me in that I was not present with them
commentary:
وَإِنَّ مِنكُمْ لَمَن لَّيُبَطِّئَنَّ فَإِنْ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالَ قَدْ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىَّ إِذْ لَمْ أَكُن مَّعَهُمْ شَهِيدًا

Now Allah ﷻ exposes a dangerous element within the community: وَإِنَّ مِنكُمْ لَمَن لَّيُبَطِّئَنَّ "There is certainly among you someone who will slow things down." The word يُبَطِّئ emphasizes intensive effort to delay and obstruct. This person will try his absolute best to slow everyone down, to create hesitation and doubt.

Picture the scene: The army prepares to depart at a specific time. Soldiers pack their gear, ready themselves mentally and physically. But this individual? He's suddenly searching for his toothbrush. He can't find his favorite pillow. "Just give me ten more minutes—I'm coming with you, I promise!" But those ten minutes stretch longer. The army can't wait. Does the entire force delay its departure for one person making excuses? No. They leave, and he stays behind. And the moment they're gone: relief floods through him.

In warfare, every second matters. Every decision can mean the difference between life and death. Someone constantly trying to delay and obstruct isn't just annoying—they're dangerous. Though Allah ﷻ doesn't explicitly name them here, we understand these are the hypocrites, the munafiqun, those who outwardly claim Islam but inwardly resist its demands.

Then comes the moment of revelation: فَإِنْ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ "when a calamity strikes you"—when the army faces difficulty, when soldiers are injured or killed—this person who stayed behind says: قَدْ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىَّ "Allah ﷻ has truly blessed me!" 

Blessed him how? إِذْ لَمْ أَكُن مَّعَهُمْ شَهِيدًا "Because I wasn't with them present." The word شَهِيد here means "present as a witness"—he's thanking God that he didn't have to witness the horrors of battle, that his manufactured delay saved his life. "Thank God my toothbrush strategy worked! I can't believe how blessed I am!"

translation: But if bounty comes to you from Allah, he will surely say, as if there had never been between you and him any affection. "Oh, I wish I had been with them so I could have attained a great attainment
commentary:
وَلَئِنْ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ فَضْلٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لَيَقُولَنَّ كَأَن لَّمْ تَكُنۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُۥ مَوَدَّةٌ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ مَعَهُمْ فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا

But now consider the opposite scenario: وَلَئِنْ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ فَضْلٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ "If favor from Allah ﷻ strikes you instead"—if you achieve unprecedented success, if you annihilate the enemy with minimal losses, if huge spoils of war come your way, if it's an amazing, resounding victory.

The army returns triumphant. This should be a moment of joy, of brothers embracing brothers, of congratulations and celebration among fellow soldiers. But look at this individual's response. Allah ﷻ inserts a striking observation: كَأَن لَّمْ تَكُنۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُۥ مَوَدَّةٌ "as though there was no love between you and him."

When your brothers return victorious from risking their lives, that's the moment to show love, to express gratitude that they survived, to celebrate their courage. But this person displays no such warmth, no affection, no genuine care for his fellow believers. Why does Allah ﷻ highlight this absence of love? Because of what comes out of his mouth:

يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ مَعَهُمْ "Oh, if only I had been with them!" Not "I'm so relieved you're safe" or "I'm so proud of what you accomplished" or "Thank God you survived." No—his focus is entirely different: فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا "then I would have achieved a tremendous success!"

He's looking at the spoils of war loaded on the camels and horses. He's calculating what his share would have been. "Oh man, they struck it big! I should have gone. I knew it. That toothbrush excuse—I should have saved it for next time!" He has no love for his brothers. He has love for wealth, for reputation. His imagined "success" isn't about contributing to the cause or defending the community—it's about material gain and being counted among the war heroes, which would have further concealed his hypocrisy and earned him undeserved praise.

translation: So let those fight in the cause of Allah who sell the life of this world for the Hereafter. And he who fights in the cause of Allah and is killed or achieves victory - We will bestow upon him a great reward
commentary:
۞ فَلْيُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَمَن يُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيُقْتَلْ أَوْ يَغْلِبْ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

After exposing the hypocrite's hollow opportunism, Allah ﷻ presents the opposite mindset: فَلْيُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ "So let those fight in the path of Allah ﷻ..." Who are these people? ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ "those who sell the life of this world for the Hereafter."

They've made an exchange, a transaction. They've let go of attachment to worldly accumulation. They've contented themselves with the knowledge that real success lies in the akhirah. This is especially critical in a state of war. When you march out to battle, you must be mentally prepared for the possibility that you won't return, that your home might be destroyed, that everything you've built might be gone. But you know one thing with certainty: there's a home waiting in the Hereafter. That conviction, that complete trust in Allah's promise, is what enables a person to stand firm alongside the Messenger ﷺ on the battlefield.

The next part is crucial: وَمَن يُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيُقْتَلْ أَوْ يَغْلِبْ "And whoever fights in the path of Allah ﷻ and is killed or achieves victory..." Notice the structure here. Either outcome—martyrdom or triumph—leads to the same conclusion: فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا "We will give him a tremendous reward."

This is encouragement, not commandment. That distinction matters profoundly. Many militaries throughout history have used drafts—mandatory conscription where all able-bodied citizens of a certain age must serve whether they want to or not. Sometimes this becomes necessary when a nation faces existential threat and doesn't have sufficient reserves. Everyone must contribute or everyone perishes.

But in early Islam, up until the expedition to Tabuk, Muslim armies were volunteer forces. That's remarkable. When 300 people walked away from the battlefield at Uhud, what were the consequences? Harsh words in the Quran describing their desertion, yes, but no execution, no court-martial, no legal punishment. They received stern rebuke—that's it.

This changed at Tabuk, when an open draft was instituted. That's mentioned in Surah At-Tawbah, where participation became obligatory. If you couldn't go, you had to come to the Messenger ﷺ personally and explain yourself. You couldn't just stay home with excuses. But we're not there yet in the passage we're studying. Here, the massive reward promised for either outcome—martyrdom or victory—serves as motivation, as encouragement for those who would voluntarily dedicate themselves to defending the nascent Muslim community.

translation: And what is [the matter] with you that you fight not in the cause of Allah and [for] the oppressed among men, women, and children who say, "Our Lord, take us out of this city of oppressive people and appoint for us from Yourself a protector and appoint for us from Yourself a helper
commentary:
وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلظَّالِمِ أَهْلُهَا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَى نَصِيرًا

Now Allah ﷻ poses a direct question: وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ "What is wrong with you that you don't fight in the path of Allah ﷻ?" But the question doesn't end there. Fighting in Allah's path here has a specific, concrete objective: وَٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ "and for those who have been made weak—from among the men, the women, and the children."

Who are these المُسْتَضْعَفِين, these oppressed, weakened ones? They are Muslims still trapped in Makkah—believers who couldn't migrate for various reasons. Perhaps they lacked financial means. Perhaps their families prevented them. Perhaps they were elderly, sick, or enslaved. They belong to that category mentioned earlier in the surah: those unable to devise a strategy to escape, unable to find a way out. They're stuck in a city whose inhabitants wrong them constantly.

Listen to their prayer: ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلظَّالِمِ أَهْلُهَا  "Those who say: Our Master, deliver us from this town whose people are wrongdoers." They're crying out to Allah ﷻ for rescue from Makkah itself, from the city that torments them.

This provides essential context for understanding "fighting in the path of Allah ﷻ" at this historical moment. The Muslims now have somewhere to bring their trapped brothers and sisters—they can potentially rescue them and bring them to Madinah, to a place of peace, to somewhere Islam is already established and protected. 

Islam has transitioned from being just a group to becoming a state. Madinah now has governance, constitutional laws, a judiciary that passes verdicts between people. We've already seen examples of judicial matters in previous ayahs. There's a structure now, the framework of statehood, even if not yet fully formed. And this emerging state has a responsibility to defend and fight for its citizens.

It's still a voluntary call rather than a draft because the state remains in its formative stages. But the foundation exists, and with it comes responsibility. The believers trapped in Makkah continue their desperate prayer: وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ نَصِيرًا "And appoint for us, especially from Yourself, a protective ally, and appoint for us, especially from Yourself, a constant helper."

They're asking for ولي —a guardian, a protector, someone who will shield them from harm. And they're asking for نصير —someone who will give them ongoing aid and support. The repetition of مِن لَّدُنكَ "from Yourself, from Your own resources" emphasizes that they recognize only Allah ﷻ can provide what they need, but they're asking Him to send that help through human means—through their brothers and sisters who now have the capability to reach them.

Understanding سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ, the path of Allah ﷻ, requires recognizing its ultimate purpose. This path leads people to belong to one of four groups that Allah ﷻ mentions in this surah. The first group—those who stubbornly disbelieve—clearly cannot be part of Allah's objective for humanity. But the other three groups represent what Allah ﷻ wants for people, how He wants to bless them and guide them.

The fighting that occurred during the Prophet's ﷺ lifetime was a necessary event for fulfilling that greater goal. It wasn't the goal itself—it was a painful but essential step toward achieving something far more important. Some scholars compare it to a sick patient requiring surgery, perhaps even amputation. It's painful, but is it good for him? Yes. It's necessary pain for greater healing. That's the nature of military conflict in the Prophet's ﷺ mission—a difficult medicine for a greater cure.

This raises a critical question: What work had the Messenger ﷺ already done before this stage was reached? What came before fighting in understanding the complete picture of struggling in Allah's path? There was enormous effort, years of calling people with wisdom and beautiful preaching, building a community, establishing hearts on faith—all of this preceded military engagement. And after the fighting, there would be continued struggle in Allah's path as well.

Fighting represents one phase in a continuum, but we must not oversimplify something deep and complex. We need Muslim scholars who are also political scientists, sociologists, experts in international affairs—people who understand both the revealed texts and the world those texts must be applied to. We've made a grave error by separating نص (the revealed text) from واقع (the reality), by telling our traditional scholars to avoid studying worldly sciences and current affairs. 

The Quran itself will teach us a word—يَسْتَنبِط —describing the ability to deeply analyze situations, to hear news and fully understand and evaluate it. Muslim scholars must be like skilled journalists and researchers, people who grasp situations thoroughly. Without that comprehensive understanding, conclusions become shallow and disconnected from reality. When we reach the relevant passages, we'll explore this theme of fighting in Allah's path within the complete context of the Prophet's ﷺ mission and the responsibilities that come with emerging statehood.

translation: Those who believe fight in the cause of Allah, and those who disbelieve fight in the cause of Taghut. So fight against the allies of Satan. Indeed, the plot of Satan has ever been weak
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ كَانَ ضَعِيفًا

Those who truly have iman, they fight in the path of Allah ﷻ. This ayah makes fighting in Allah's ﷻ cause a test of faith itself. In contrast, those who have disbelieved fight in the path of الطَّـٰغُوت - the rebellious one, the rebellion. The core idea behind taghut is the desire to be free from any kind of obedience to Allah ﷻ. This rebellion takes different names and manifests in different forms throughout history, but at the end of the day, it remains the same entity working against the believers.

The ayah commands: fight these close, intimate friends of the shaytan. Then Allah ﷻ passes His verdict on the entire plotting and scheming of the shaytan: إِنَّ كَيْدَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ كَانَ ضَعِيفًا - indeed, the plot of the shaytan has always been weak. No doubt about it. The shaytan has never had anything substantial. The believers don't need to worry about his schemes because they are inherently feeble.

translation: Have you not seen those who were told, "Restrain your hands [from fighting] and establish prayer and give zakah"? But then when fighting was ordained for them, at once a party of them feared men as they fear Allah or with [even] greater fear. They said, "Our Lord, why have You decreed upon us fighting? If only You had postponed [it for] us for a short time." Say, The enjoyment of this world is little, and the Hereafter is better for he who fears Allah. And injustice will not be done to you, [even] as much as a thread [inside a date seed]
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ ٱلنَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْقِتَالَ لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ ۗ قُلْ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلدُّنْيَا قَلِيلٌ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا

This ayah presents an incredibly powerful idea that requires careful analysis. The phrase أَلَمْ تَرَ - "didn't you look" or "haven't you seen" - should be understood in contemporary language as: didn't you analyze this? Haven't you thought about it deeply? Haven't you come to certain conclusions about this situation?

### The Command to Hold Back

Allah ﷻ asks: didn't you look towards those who were told كُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَكُمْ - hold your hands back? The word كَفّ in Arabic means to stop an attack with your hand, with the palm of your hand. Picture someone about to strike you with a baton or sword, and you put your hand out defensively - this is kaff. The expression كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ means to hold your hands back entirely. Someone is attacking you, and the only thing you're doing is defense - no offense whatsoever. You're warding off attacks with your hands, not even with a shield, just literally your hands. You're not responding or retaliating at all.

This is the Arabic expression for taking a beating. When someone كَفَّهُ - held him back - it means he took a beating without responding. If you imagine someone getting attacked, all he's doing is putting his hands up protectively. This phrase كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ is the Quranic expression that describes the entire Makkan period - the command that characterized those difficult years.

### Understanding the Makkan Period

The ayah before this one spoke about fighting, and now this ayah speaks about an instruction that came from Allah ﷻ not to fight. There are two kinds of jihad - one is armed struggle, but there was also a time when that form of jihad was prohibited. That was the Makkan period of non-violent resistance. The Muslims couldn't fight back even though they desperately wanted to. Their families were being attacked, their friends were being attacked, their neighbors were being attacked, they themselves were being attacked. It's a natural human sentiment to defend yourself and your family, to fight back against aggression. But Allah ﷻ had given clear instructions through His Messenger ﷺ: don't fight back.

The passive construction in the ayah - قِيلَ لَهُمْ (it was said to them) - indicates that someone other than Allah ﷻ delivered this instruction to them, which means the Messenger ﷺ commanded them. But this instruction from the Prophet ﷺ was itself rooted in divine revelation, making it a verdict from Allah ﷻ Himself.

You know the stories of Muslims not fighting back in Makkah, don't you? Multiple incidents of Muslims not responding, not retaliating despite severe provocation and persecution. This is a hidden gem in the sirah of the Prophet ﷺ. The modern political term for this strategy is called nonviolent resistance or nonviolent opposition - nonviolent protest. When scholars study movements of nonviolence in contemporary world history, which examples do they cite? They quote Gandhi, perhaps Tiananmen Square (which eventually turned violent). These are presented as the heroes of nonviolence, those who preached peaceful resistance.

But the first nonviolent movement of change in the history of the world was the Makkan sirah of the Prophet ﷺ. This was unprecedented - nothing like it had ever happened before. It was revealed by Allah ﷻ as a divine strategy, and it's something that must not only be respected but studied, analyzed and deeply appreciated. What were its benefits? Why was this the commanded approach? Why not proceed straight to fighting in the path of Allah ﷻ? Why didn't Allah ﷻ reveal the permission to fight early on when the Messenger ﷺ first made his call?

### The Foundation of Islamic Thought

Understanding this period is crucial for developing Islamic thought - how we're supposed to think about our deen and its strategy, how Islam came to operate in the world. Islam didn't come merely as a set of ideas that people could take or leave. It came to become a fixture on the earth, to root itself in the earth like a tree, to become an institution. How did it become that institution?

When we study history, most people examine the visible outcomes. They study the sirah and go straight to the battles: the Messenger ﷺ migrated, then the action began. He built a masjid, then Badr happened, then Uhud, then Khandaq, then the conquest of Makkah. We love listing out the battles. But that's like looking at a magnificent tree and being amazed by it without ever asking about the seed. What about what was happening behind the scenes?

For ten to twelve years, you don't see any visible progress - no physical progress at all. Just a handful of people. If anything, things are getting worse and worse by the day. But that period represents the seed. That's the foundation. If there's no Makkan period, there's no hijrah, and there's no Badr, and there's no subsequent victories. Everything that Islam accomplished is based on that seed.

Consider it another way: two-thirds of the Quran was revealed during the Makkan period. Two-thirds of what Allah ﷻ revealed to humanity as permanent guidance came down in these twelve years. This demonstrates just how important those years were.

### What Made Someone a Muslim in Makkah?

Back in the Makkan era, what were the expectations from Muslims? Did they have to have a beard or wear a hijab? Did they have to pray five times a day? That came much later in Madinah. Did they have to pay 2.5% in zakat? No. Did inheritance laws apply to them? No. Did they have to fast in the month of Ramadan? No. Did they have to go to Hajj once in their lifetime? None of that was revealed in Makkah.

Today when you think of a Muslim, you think of these practices, right? But these early believers just became Muslim, and for twelve years, you have to ask yourself: what actually made them Muslim? What defined their identity? They didn't look any different from other Arabs. No special dress code had been revealed to them. Even the prayers in their official form were a later development in Madinah.

All that distinguished them was their iman - their faith and character. Even in the Quran revealed during this period, what was the fundamental message? If you were teaching a Sunday school class to children, you could take half a page and summarize the entire Makkan Quran - two-thirds of the Quran summarized on half a piece of paper. What would it say?

You should believe in Allah ﷻ, that He has a messenger, and that there is an afterlife. You should learn from the mistakes of previous nations. There are some really terrible things you must avoid - like killing people, oppressing others, being unfair. You should be fair in your life. These are general principles of fairness, not intricate, minute details of jurisprudence. These are principles of fairness that anyone would read and say, "Yes, that makes sense." Don't kill baby girls - any humanitarian group today would agree with that. Don't push orphans around - that makes complete sense. You don't have to be Muslim to agree with these things.

What was revealed in the Makkan Quran wasn't something Muslim-specific. These were universal values of justice. Any human being with an ounce of decency would say these make complete sense.

### The Escalation of Opposition

The believers were calling for absolute justice and these wonderful, universally acceptable ideas. As a result, they were first made fun of and mocked. They didn't retaliate - they continued their call. Then some young people started joining them, and the elders got worried. "Our youth are foolish. They join cults. Maybe they're joining this Muslim cult. We should warn them. We should warn them against their leader, this cult leader."

So they started a media campaign against the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. It was completely logical that they would do this. "He's insane. He's a liar. He's a poet." They tried every angle of defamation.

When that didn't work, something else happened. Members of the working class started joining - some of the slaves began accepting Islam. This became problematic for Makkan society: "We need slaves to do our work. Now they're becoming rebellious. They're not following our way. This is hurting the economy. Our labor market is suffering."

Then it went further. Some wealthy individuals - the entrepreneurs and businessmen of that society, the equivalent of successful modern executives - started becoming Muslim. Abu Bakr (RA) became Muslim, Uthman (RA) became Muslim. This was a serious problem. "If these influential people with wealth start becoming Muslim, we've got a real crisis on our hands. This minuscule movement led by this orphan, who we thought had no real influence, is now gaining more and more momentum."

"We need to do something about this. We need to spread a campaign that these people are a threat to our security. We need to attack them first. We need to raise fear among the population that these Muslims are dangerous. Once we do that, then certain random acts of violence against them will be justifiable. Maybe we won't come out against them all together as official policy, but here and there we'll attack somebody, beat somebody up."

Did that start happening? Yes. Random acts of violence against the Muslim community because they were perceived as a threat to Makkan society. They were told Islam would separate them from their parents, make them question their loyalties: are you Makkan or are you Muslim? This rhetoric should sound familiar.

When that didn't work and only gave the Muslims more resolve - they kept going, they kept preaching - what came next? "Wherever you see them, it's now open policy. These people are a threat. In the name of securing our national interests, it's acceptable for us to torture them. We're going to pass certain constitutional amendments that allow us to torture these citizens. It's justified."

### The Breaking of Sacred Norms

The Makkans did not attack their own. Caring for your own tribe was the highest constitutional law in pre-Islamic Arabia. You never harm people of your own tribe. That was seen as a sign of weakness by outsiders. Every society has certain lines it never crosses. The line Makkans would never cross was attacking their own people.

There was only one time they were willing to cross that line: when it came to dealing with Muslims. Similarly, Firaun would never attack his own. Musa (AS) was perceived as one of Firaun's own because he was raised in the palace. The only time Firaun made an exception to attack one of his own was when it came to opposing the message of Allah ﷻ.

So when we express shock - "But the constitution guarantees rights! There should be equal opportunity before the law, a fair justice system!" - and we wonder why Muslims aren't treated with fairness, this isn't news. This is ancient history repeating itself.

### The Wisdom of Nonviolence

In the face of all that persecution, what was the response commanded for the Muslims? What did Allah ﷻ instruct them to do? Don't fight back. Take the beating. Take the abuse.

The question naturally arises: why? Why not fight back? They had every reason to. The Makkans were being completely unfair. They were justifying crimes of violence against innocent people. Why couldn't the Muslims fight back?

The issue is this: the Makkans were saying the Muslims were a threat to society, weren't they? They were painting them as dangerous. If the Muslims fought back even once, the opposition would capture it, publicize it widely and say, "See? We told you! We told you they're crazy. We told you they're dangerous. Now it's totally justified to crack down on them." One act of stepping out of line, one failure to hold back, would nullify the entire movement. It would destroy everything they were working toward.

Is this pattern happening today? We can proclaim "Islam is peace" all we want. You can hold a conference about how Islam is peaceful. All they need to do is show something in the news - one group, one foolish young person making a mistake, one incident on a plane - and that's it. Everything we've said gets dismissed: "Oh, this is all deception. They have a hidden agenda. They want us to believe they're peaceful, but something else is really going on."

This was the strategic wisdom of the early Makkan command to hold your hands back - nonviolent protest, nonviolent resistance. The only thing that should come out of your mouth is the Quran and the fundamental message: we're simply saying Allah ﷻ is One, and His commands are just. We're saying people should be given what they rightfully deserve. We're saying baby girls shouldn't be killed.

### Fighting for Universal Justice

What's remarkable is that they weren't even fighting for their own rights. They weren't organizing Muslim civil rights campaigns in Makkan society. They were advocating for baby girls' civil rights. They were establishing consumer protection when people were cheated at the marketplace. They were fighting for people being killed and oppressed - not even fighting for themselves.

If you observed them during this high-profile period when they were being persecuted, all you would hear from them is a message of accepting One God - a message that makes complete rational sense - and a message of justice. That's all. And not even justice for themselves. They weren't saying, "Stop beating us up. We protest against you persecuting us." There's no such campaign. They were willing to take these hits for the sake of the weaker members of that society.

Even when fighting is eventually mentioned in the Quran, what motive is given? Fighting is prescribed to help those who have been weakened, those who are tortured, those who are beaten up - go and fight for them. It comes back to justice. That would be a justifiable cause to engage in war in any country. You don't have to be Muslim to accept this principle. Any nation would say that if innocent civilians are being hurt, if our citizens are being harmed, if our people are being held hostage, we're going to rescue them. There's no controversy in international law about defending the oppressed.

### The Power of Moral Consistency

Imagine if the Muslim world today - and this is something scholars need to discuss in depth - took a policy of nonviolent resistance everywhere. If every man, woman and child in Palestine came out unarmed, every single one of them on the streets, nonviolent, saying, "Kill us all you want, go ahead" - how long could that last?

Think about what happened in Iran. When the Shah was overthrown, men, women and children simply came out on the streets. The Shah had an army, he had tanks positioned throughout the streets. You had women wearing niqab with babies in their arms, standing in front of tanks. How many could he run over before his own soldiers said, "These are our people. I can't do this"? The military turned on the Shah. The Iranian Revolution succeeded through this principle.

More recently, we saw the Arab Spring. Frustrated youth realized they could bring change to society through peaceful means. For so long, the rationale had been that militant groups were necessary, that violence was the only option against tyrant rulers. That entire rationale was challenged by the Arab Spring. Changes that seemed impossible in the Arab world came about through what? Nonviolent protests. We're not certain what everything will turn into, but the remarkable changes came primarily through nonviolent resistance. The only violence that sparked was initiated by the other side, but so long as the people held on and said, "No, we're not going to fight back, we're not going to turn violent," amazing transformation became possible.

All of this was inspired originally by - the root of it goes back to - what happened in the sirah of the Prophet ﷺ.

### The Moral Depletion of the Oppressor

There's one last profound benefit to this strategy of nonviolent resistance: it morally depletes the society that's on the attack. When you're constantly attacking people who haven't done anything wrong, persistently persecuting people who are innocent, eventually your will to fight them diminishes. Your will to attack them goes down. People start thinking, "Why are we doing this to them? How can this be right?" They begin questioning the foundations of their own society: "How can we do this to our own people?"

Because the Quraysh didn't have moral strength, moral conviction or willpower built on justice, when they came out to fight at Badr, they weren't nearly as driven and motivated as the Muslims were. Why? Because they knew they were the ones who had done wrong. They kicked the Muslims out of their homes. They were the ones who beat them up. The Muslims hadn't done anything to deserve persecution. The Quraysh killed innocent people, tortured believers, placed boulders on their chests, burnt their backs. They knew exactly what they had done.

They didn't have moral strength when they lost at Badr. But then, after that defeat, did they have something to be fired up about? Yes - "They beat us at Badr!" So they came back much stronger at Uhud. Now they had grounds for their enthusiasm. Now they had something to build their motivation upon. But that initial moral weakness came from years of being the unjust aggressors.

### The Contrast: Two Groups Who Didn't Fight

The ayah says: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ - establish the prayer and give zakat. This was the command during the Makkan era - the holding back period is now over. The Madinah period begins.

فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ - when fighting was prescribed upon them, when it was mandated, إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ - suddenly a group among them began يَخْشَوْنَ ٱلنَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ - fearing the people as though they should be fearing Allah ﷻ, أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً - or with even more intense fear.

This creates a profound contrast. In Makkah, there was a group that desperately wanted to fight, but Allah ﷻ told them no. Now in Madinah, Allah ﷻ is telling them it's time for war, and a group says, "No thanks. We're afraid of the people."

Allah ﷻ presents two situations of nonviolence - two groups who didn't fight. One group refrained from fighting in obedience to Allah ﷻ, and by not fighting, they pleased Him. The other group refused to fight in disobedience to Allah ﷻ, and by not fighting, they displeased Him. There's a dramatic contrast between these two responses.

### Questioning Divine Wisdom

وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْقِتَالُ - they said, "Our Master, why did You prescribe fighting upon us? Why did You mandate fighting? What is this? I thought this was a religion of peace!"

لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ - "Why couldn't You just give us a little bit more time? Let us gain a little more strength, and then You could have prescribed fighting on us. The timing seems wrong."

### The Divine Response

Allah ﷻ responds: قُلْ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلدُّنْيَا قَلِيلٌ - Tell them the enjoyment of worldly life, the utility of this temporary existence, is very little. It's limited and fleeting.

وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ - and the afterlife is better for the one who truly has taqwa, who is genuinely fearful and conscious of Allah ﷻ.

وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا - and you will not be wronged even the amount of a fateel. A fateel is the tiny thread or wick in a candle that burns. In another meaning, if you examine a date seed carefully, you'll notice little tiny fibers emerging from it. One of those microscopic fibers is called a fateel. Allah ﷻ is saying you won't be wronged even that infinitesimal amount. Nothing unfair will happen to you, not even in the least bit.

So instead of complaining, "It's so unfair, why do we have to go fight?" - understand that nothing unfair will happen to you. Not even the smallest injustice. What are you truly worried about?

translation: Wherever you may be, death will overtake you, even if you should be within towers of lofty construction. But if good comes to them, they say, "This is from Allah "; and if evil befalls them, they say, "This is from you." Say, "All [things] are from Allah." So what is [the matter] with those people that they can hardly understand any statement
commentary:
أَيْنَمَا تَكُونُوا۟ يُدْرِككُّمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ وَلَوْ كُنتُمْ فِى بُرُوجٍ مُّشَيَّدَةٍ ۗ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِكَ ۚ قُلْ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَالِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا

**Wherever you may be, death will overtake you, even if you should be within towers of lofty construction. And if good befalls them, they say, "This is from Allah," but if evil befalls them, they say, "This is from you." Say, "All things are from Allah." So what is wrong with those people that they can hardly understand any statement?**

This ayah continues the discussion from the previous passages about those who questioned the mandate for fighting. The opening addresses a fundamental reality: أَيْنَمَا تَكُونُوا۟ يُدْرِككُّمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ (Wherever you may be, death will overtake you). The word يُدْرِك comes from دَرَكَ, which means to descend steps gradually. The verb form يُدْرِك carries the meaning of something reaching you stage by stage, step by step. This linguistic choice paints death not as a sudden ambush but as a journey that began the moment you were born—a companion traveling toward you with each passing moment, getting closer with every breath.

The ayah continues: وَلَوْ كُنتُمْ فِى بُرُوجٍ مُّشَيَّدَةٍ (even if you should be within towers of lofty construction). The word بُرُوج refers to tall, fortified towers. The descriptor مُّشَيَّدَةٍ comes from شيد, referring to the plastering and reinforcement of walls. In ancient construction, brick walls had indentations that could be scaled, but when walls were plastered smooth with reinforcements, they became impossible to climb—impenetrable fortresses. Allah ﷻ is saying that no matter how secure your defenses, no matter how impenetrable your fortress, death will reach you. This directly addresses those who feared fighting because they feared death, as if staying behind would somehow protect them from their inevitable end.

The ayah then shifts to expose the hypocritical mindset of the munafiqun. وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ (And if good befalls them, they say, "This is from Allah"). When victories came or blessings arrived, the hypocrites would quickly attribute them to Allah ﷻ—not out of genuine faith, but to diminish the role of the Messenger ﷺ and dampen the enthusiasm of the believers. They wanted to ensure that no credit went to the Prophet's ﷺ leadership or guidance.

But وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِكَ (if evil befalls them, they say, "This is from you"). When difficulties arose—losses in battle, hardships, setbacks—they immediately blamed the Messenger ﷺ personally. This is the psychology of the hypocrite: deflecting responsibility, using even the name of Allah ﷻ to make snide remarks, and never maintaining a consistent position. They invoke Allah ﷻ when it serves their criticism but show no genuine submission to Him.

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: قُلْ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ (Say, "All things are from Allah"). Both the good and the bad, the victories and the trials—all are from Allah ﷻ. This is the foundational belief that the hypocrites refused to internalize.

Then comes the piercing question: فَمَالِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا (So what is wrong with those people that they can hardly understand any statement?). Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't call them a "nation" (ummah) but rather هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ—"this group of people"—as if separating them from the believing community. The phrase لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا suggests they're almost incapable of understanding even a single piece of guidance or advice. It's as though no matter what is said to them, no matter how clear the message, they refuse to comprehend.

translation: What comes to you of good is from Allah, but what comes to you of evil, [O man], is from yourself. And We have sent you, [O Muhammad], to the people as a messenger, and sufficient is Allah as Witness
commentary:
مَّآ أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ ۚ وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولًا ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا

**What comes to you of good is from Allah, but what comes to you of evil is from yourself. And We have sent you, as a messenger to all people. And sufficient is Allah as Witness.**

This ayah has been interpreted in two primary ways, both of which carry profound meaning. The first interpretation takes the ayah as direct instruction to the Messenger ﷺ himself: whatever good comes to you is absolutely from Allah ﷻ, and whatever difficulty comes to you does have something to do with you—meaning it comes as a test from Allah ﷻ based on your own actions or as a means of elevation. This understanding aligns with other places in the Quran where Allah ﷻ says that whatever good comes is from Him, and whatever difficulty comes is from ourselves as a consequence or test.

However, the more contextually compelling interpretation considers what was just said in the previous ayah. The hypocrites were saying, "Good things are from Allah, bad things are from you (the Prophet ﷺ)." This ayah can be understood as Allah ﷻ quoting their statement in a tone of incredulity: "Oh, so you think that when good comes it's from Allah, but when bad comes it's from you?" The statement مَّآ أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ echoes their twisted logic, and then Allah ﷻ immediately follows with the reality: وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولًا (And We have sent you as a messenger to all people).

In other words, "You claim bad things happen because of him? We sent him as a messenger to humanity. How can you blame Our messenger for what We have decreed?" The ayah concludes with وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (And sufficient is Allah as Witness)—Allah ﷻ Himself bears witness to the truth of the Messenger's ﷺ mission and the absurdity of their accusations.

translation: He who obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah; but those who turn away - We have not sent you over them as a guardian
commentary:
مَّن يُطِعِ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطَاعَ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا

**He who obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah. But those who turn away—We have not sent you over them as a guardian.**

This ayah establishes one of the most fundamental principles in Islam: obedience to the Messenger ﷺ is obedience to Allah ﷻ. There is no separation between following the Prophet's ﷺ guidance and following Allah's ﷻ commands. مَّن يُطِعِ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطَاعَ ٱللَّهَ leaves no room for the notion that one can claim faith in Allah ﷻ while disregarding the Messenger ﷺ.

The second part addresses those who turn away: وَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا (But those who turn away—We have not sent you over them as a guardian). The word حَفِيظًا means a guardian, one who watches over and is responsible for others. This is remarkable because even at the point when Islam had become a state and the Prophet ﷺ held judicial and executive authority, Allah ﷻ reminds him that he cannot force anyone to believe or obey. His responsibility is to convey the message, to guide, to advise—but not to compel hearts or control actions. All he can do is fulfill his role as a messenger. The rest is between them and Allah ﷻ.

translation: And they say, "[We pledge] obedience." But when they leave you, a group of them spend the night determining to do other than what you say. But Allah records what they plan by night. So leave them alone and rely upon Allah. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs
commentary:
وَيَقُولُونَ طَاعَةٌ فَإِذَا بَرَزُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ بَيَّتَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى تَقُولُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَكْتُبُ مَا يُبَيِّتُونَ ۖ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا

**And they say, "We hear and we obey." But when they leave your presence, a group of them spend the night determining to do other than what you say. But Allah records what they plan by night. So turn away from them and rely upon Allah. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.**

This ayah exposes the duplicitous behavior of the hypocrites. وَيَقُولُونَ طَاعَةٌ (And they say, "We hear and we obey")—to the Prophet's ﷺ face, they affirm obedience. They attend meetings, they sit in prayer, they nod in agreement. But فَإِذَا بَرَزُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ (when they leave your presence)—and the word بَرَزُوا۟ means to come out openly, to emerge—they don't just quietly leave. They make it obvious they've left, creating the appearance of departure.

Then comes the real treachery: بَيَّتَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى تَقُولُ (a group of them spend the night determining to do other than what you say). The word بَيَّتَ comes from بَاتَ, meaning to spend the night, but بَيَّتَ specifically means to spend the night plotting or planning. After the public meeting with the Messenger ﷺ ends, a faction among them reconvenes privately. They have a secret meeting to undo what was just discussed. In the Quran, this is called نَجْوَىٰ in other places—secret counsel. 

This practice remains unfortunately common: a meeting happens, decisions are made, then a subgroup gets together afterward—perhaps over coffee or a meal—and begins to undermine what was agreed upon. They wrap their criticism in seemingly pious or thoughtful language, but the essence is the same: plotting against what was instructed.

Allah ﷻ says: وَٱللَّهُ يَكْتُبُ مَا يُبَيِّتُونَ (But Allah records what they plan by night). He is taking minutes of their secret meetings. Nothing they plot in darkness is hidden from Him. Every word, every scheme, every betrayal is documented.

Then comes the instruction to the Prophet ﷺ: فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ (So turn away from them). Don't confront them right now. Don't call them out. Don't get worked up about their schemes. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying, "I'm letting you know what they're doing, but your response should be to ignore them for now. You have bigger concerns. Let Me deal with them."

وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ (And rely upon Allah)—place your complete trust in Allah ﷻ alone. وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs). He is enough to handle your matters. He is enough to take care of these plotters. You need no other guardian or manager of your affairs.

translation: Then do they not reflect upon the Qur'an? If it had been from [any] other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction
commentary:
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۚ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا۟ فِيهِ ٱخْتِلَـٰفًا كَثِيرًا

**Then do they not reflect upon the Quran? If it had been from any other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction.**

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the root of the hypocrites' problem. After describing their plotting and scheming, He asks: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ (Then do they not reflect upon the Quran?). The word تَدَبُّر means deep, careful reflection—to think about something from all angles, to ponder its meanings and implications.

This question might seem more appropriate for those being introduced to Islam, for those questioning whether the Quran is divine revelation. Why direct it to the hypocrites? Because at their core, the munafiqun and the disbelievers share the same fundamental problem: lack of conviction. The hypocrites stood in prayer behind the Messenger ﷺ, listening to the Quran being recited. These ayahs were being revealed in real time, often directly addressing the situations they were living through. When the Prophet ﷺ would recite in prayer, they would hear verses about people who come to prayer lazily, about those who plot at night, about those who make excuses—and they knew these ayahs were about them.

Yet after the prayer ended and the Messenger ﷺ would give them instructions, they would mock those instructions and scheme against them. The underlying attitude was: "Here he goes again—another convenient revelation against us." They thought the Prophet ﷺ was making it up. So Allah ﷻ takes them back to the foundation: Have you actually thought about this Quran?

وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا۟ فِيهِ ٱخْتِلَـٰفًا كَثِيرًا (If it had been from any other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction). This is one of the most profound proofs of the Quran's divine origin. If this book were the product of a human mind—even the most brilliant human mind—it would inevitably contain contradictions. Humans change their minds, forget what they said previously, contradict themselves under different circumstances, and make errors in logic and fact. But the Quran, revealed over 23 years in vastly different circumstances—times of peace and war, weakness and strength, poverty and prosperity—maintains perfect internal consistency. Not a single genuine contradiction exists within it.

This challenge stands for all time: if you truly believe this is from other than Allah ﷻ, then find the contradictions. But they won't, because there are none.

translation: And when there comes to them information about [public] security or fear, they spread it around. But if they had referred it back to the Messenger or to those of authority among them, then the ones who [can] draw correct conclusions from it would have known about it. And if not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy, you would have followed Satan, except for a few
commentary:
وَإِذَا جَآءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْنِ أَوِ ٱلْخَوْفِ أَذَاعُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ وَإِلَىٰٓ أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ لَعَلِمَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَنۢبِطُونَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَٱتَّبَعْتُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

**And when there comes to them information about security or fear, they spread it around. But if they had referred it back to the Messenger or to those of authority among them, then the ones who can draw correct conclusions from it would have known about it. And if not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy, you would have followed Satan, except for a few.**

This ayah addresses another manifestation of hypocrisy: the spreading of unverified information during critical times. وَإِذَا جَآءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْنِ أَوِ ٱلْخَوْفِ (And when there comes to them information about security or fear)—when any news arrived that concerned either peace or danger. Perhaps news of a tribe wanting to make a peace treaty, expanding the influence of the Muslims. Or dangerous intelligence that an enemy tribe was mobilizing, or spies had been discovered. Any sensitive information related to the security of the community.

أَذَاعُوا۟ بِهِۦ (they spread it around). The word أَذَاعُوا۟ is the same root used in modern Arabic for broadcasting. In classical Arabic, إذاعة described someone who cannot hold a secret—they broadcast everything everywhere, especially information that shouldn't be made public. This is particularly dangerous in times of war when information warfare and psychological operations are crucial. Spreading rumors can instill false fear or create false security, leaving people unprepared for an attack. This could be an enemy strategy: spread misinformation to make people let their guard down, then strike.

The proper course of action is clear: وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ وَإِلَىٰٓ أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ (But if they had referred it back to the Messenger or to those of authority among them). The word رَدُّوهُ suggests an attitude of rejection—"We're not going to spread this. The first thing we'll do is take this information to the Messenger ﷺ himself and to the commanders, those in positions of military and strategic authority."

لَعَلِمَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَنۢبِطُونَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ (then the ones who can draw correct conclusions from it would have known about it). The word يَسْتَنۢبِطُونَ is remarkable. The root نَبَطَ refers to digging deep into a dry well until you finally reach water. اِسْتِنْبَاط means to extract something through deep investigation and analysis—digging through information from every angle until you reach the truth, like finally reaching water after extensive digging. Those qualified to analyze intelligence would have processed it thoroughly, investigated it completely, and only then would action be taken.

The ayah concludes with a sobering reminder: وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَٱتَّبَعْتُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (And if not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy, you would have followed Satan, except for a few). Following rumors—hearing something and immediately passing it on without verification—is equated with following Shaytan. Had Allah ﷻ not favored us with guidance and His mercy, all of us except a few would have fallen into this trap of Shaytan, spreading misinformation and causing harm to the community.

translation: So fight, [O Muhammad], in the cause of Allah; you are not held responsible except for yourself. And encourage the believers [to join you] that perhaps Allah will restrain the [military] might of those who disbelieve. And Allah is greater in might and stronger in [exemplary] punishment
commentary:
فَقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ ۚ وَحَرِّضِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَكُفَّ بَأْسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْسًا وَأَشَدُّ تَنكِيلًا

**So fight in the cause of Allah; you are not held responsible except for yourself. And encourage the believers. Perhaps Allah will restrain the military might of those who disbelieve. And Allah is greater in might and stronger in exemplary punishment.**

After describing all the obstacles—the hypocrites' plotting, their spreading of rumors, their lack of conviction—Allah ﷻ directly addresses the Messenger ﷺ in the singular: فَقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (So fight in the cause of Allah). This is a command specifically to him.

Then comes a crucial clarification: لَا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ (you are not held responsible except for yourself). No one is being compelled by Allah ﷻ to fight. This isn't a draft. The believers are being motivated, promised enormous rewards, having their iman challenged and elevated—but they're not being forced. Allah ﷻ is emphasizing to the Prophet ﷺ that he cannot compel anyone to join him. He is only responsible for himself.

وَحَرِّضِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (And encourage the believers). There are two words in Arabic for motivation: تَرْغِيب and تَحْرِيض. Both relate to encouraging action, but حَرَّضَ specifically means to instill excitement and passion—to fire people up. Think of a powerful, eloquent speaker at a rally who gets people jumping, yelling, excited to take action. That's تَحْرِيض. Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ that his role now is to inspire and motivate the believers, to kindle that fire within them that moves them to action.

عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَكُفَّ بَأْسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (Perhaps Allah will restrain the military might of those who disbelieve). The word بَأْس means might, strength, or war-making capability. كَفَّ means to hold back or tie the hands. The word كَفّ itself means "palm of the hand," and يَكُفَّ means to restrain or prevent action—like holding someone's hands so they cannot strike. Allah ﷻ is saying that He may well put the enemy on the defensive, restraining their ability to wage war against you.

The word عَسَى (perhaps) indicates strong likelihood—and historically, this is exactly what happened after the Treaty of Hudaybiyah. Though the treaty seemed at face value to contain disadvantages for the Muslims, it proved to be the strategic turning point. Allah ﷻ called it a clear victory (فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا) in Surah Al-Fath, even though no battle occurred. He didn't use those words for Badr or Uhud, but He used them for Hudaybiyah—the treaty that appeared disadvantageous but led to all the subsequent strategic advantages.

There are three major instances in the Prophetic biography where violence was expected but didn't occur: the entire Makkan period despite persecution, the Treaty of Hudaybiyah which could have turned bloody instantly, and the conquest of Makkah itself—bloodless despite expectations. Each represents a different form of Allah's ﷻ promise to restrain the might of the disbelievers.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: وَٱللَّهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْسًا وَأَشَدُّ تَنكِيلًا (And Allah is greater in might and stronger in exemplary punishment). Even if you go by yourself, you're not alone—Allah ﷻ is with you, and He is far more powerful in warfare than any enemy.

The word تَنكِيلًا is particularly striking. It comes from نَكَالٌ, which means to make an example of someone—to give them such a punishment that others look at them as a cautionary tale. Allah ﷻ uses this word in the ayah about cutting the hand of the thief: نَكَالًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ (as a deterrent from Allah) (5:38). When people see the consequence, they're deterred from that crime. Here, أَشَدُّ تَنكِيلًا means Allah ﷻ is most powerful in making such an example—in delivering the kind of punishment that people will speak about as a warning for generations. It's the kind of punishment where people say, "Did you see what happened to them? Do you want that to happen to you?"

This is the nature of Allah's ﷻ power—absolute in might, and absolute in His ability to make clear examples for those who would take heed.

translation: Whoever intercedes for a good cause will have a reward therefrom; and whoever intercedes for an evil cause will have a burden therefrom. And ever is Allah, over all things, a Keeper
commentary:
مَّن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ نَصِيبٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً سَيِّئَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ كِفْلٌ مِّنْهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقِيتًا

After discussing matters of warfare and community defense, the Quran shifts to examine social laws that govern daily Muslim life. This ayah addresses the powerful social phenomenon of شَفَاعَة - intercession or recommendation.

The word shafa'ah encompasses several meanings in Arabic. It refers to putting in a good word for someone, whether for employment, marriage consideration, business opportunities, or resolving disputes between people. When someone asks about a potential marriage partner and you provide honest testimony about their character, that is shafa'ah. When you recommend someone for a job based on their actual qualifications and track record, that is shafa'ah. When you intervene in a conflict to make peace between friends or family members by clarifying misunderstandings based on truth, that is shafa'ah hasanah - a good intercession.

Allah ﷻ says: "Whoever intercedes with a good intercession will have a share from it." This principle connects to the hadith teaching that whoever points to good is like the one who does it. When you help facilitate something beneficial, you receive a portion of the reward. The word نَصِيب means "portion" or "share," indicating that the person who makes the intercession participates in the blessing of the outcome.

However, the ayah immediately warns against شَفَاعَةً سَيِّئَةً - evil intercession. This takes several forms. It includes speaking against someone without basis, perhaps warning others away from a marriage prospect because of personal bias rather than legitimate concern. It includes falsely recommending someone you know is unqualified or has a poor track record simply because of family connections, tribal affiliations, or the desire to see "your people" advance regardless of merit.

This evil intercession appears when business competitors spread false rumors about each other's halal status or integrity. It manifests when someone refuses to recommend a qualified person simply because they cannot stand to see others succeed. The ayah addresses the destructive tribalism and nepotism that plagued pre-Islamic Arabian society and unfortunately continues in various forms today.

The consequence is severe: "And whoever intercedes with an evil intercession will have كِفْل from it." While naseeb and kifl both mean "portion," the word kifl carries additional weight. Some scholars note that kifl can mean "double," suggesting the person receives punishment both for lying and for whatever harm resulted from their false testimony. They bear responsibility for all the negative consequences that flow from their deceit.

The ayah concludes with a profound Divine attribute: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقِيتًا - "And Allah has always been, over all things, a Keeper of careful account." The word مُقِيت derives from a root containing three meanings: to have power, to have guardianship, and to have the ability to hold something firmly without letting it go.

This ending serves as both comfort and warning. Your false testimony may put someone in a position they don't deserve, or it may cause an innocent person to lose an opportunity or even face punishment. But ultimately, all things and all people rest under Allah's protection. False testimony cannot ultimately save someone Allah ﷻ has decreed should face consequences, nor can it permanently harm someone Allah ﷻ protects. Every act of intercession, good or bad, is recorded and guarded by Allah ﷻ, and justice will prevail.

translation: And when you are greeted with a greeting, greet [in return] with one better than it or [at least] return it [in a like manner]. Indeed, Allah is ever, over all things, an Accountant
commentary:
وَإِذَا حُيِّيتُم بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا۟ بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْهَآ أَوْ رُدُّوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَسِيبًا

This ayah establishes the etiquette of greetings in Muslim society, using the word تَحِيَّة, which derives from the root meaning "life." The Arabic greeting حَيَّاكَ ٱللَّٰهُ literally means "may Allah give you life" - a wish for someone to live well and long.

The beauty of this legislation is its universality. Allah ﷻ says وَإِذَا حُيِّيتُم بِتَحِيَّةٍ - "When you are greeted with a greeting" - using the general term tahiyyah rather than restricting this to the Islamic greeting of salam alone. This means the principle applies to any respectful greeting: "Good morning," "Have a nice day," "Hope you're well" - these all fall under the category of greetings that deserve a response.

The command has two levels. First, فَحَيُّوا۟ بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْهَآ - "then greet with something better than it." Allah ﷻ prefers that we respond with something superior to what we received. If someone says "Have a nice day," we should respond with "I hope you have an even better day." This is why when someone says "Assalamu alaikum," we don't simply respond with "Wa alaikum" but add "wa rahmatullahi wa barakatuh" - we enhance the greeting.

There's wisdom in this preference for the one who responds to add more. The one who initiates the salam receives greater reward, so the respondent compensates by adding to their return greeting, creating a beautiful competition in goodness.

The second level provides a minimum requirement: أَوْ رُدُّوهَآ - "or at least return it equally." If you cannot better the greeting, you must at minimum match it. This is basic courtesy and respect. To ignore a greeting or respond dismissively violates this Divine command.

The most excellent greeting in Islam is السَّلَام - the greeting of peace. When we say "Assalamu alaikum," we're not just making pleasant small talk. We're invoking peace upon that person in this life and the hereafter. We're declaring that there is no animosity between us, that we wish them well in both worlds.

This transforms the greeting into something sacred and binding. When spouses are arguing and one leaves saying "Assalamu alaikum," that greeting should signal the end of the dispute. The Prophet ﷺ taught that believers should not abandon speaking to each other for more than three days, and the act of giving salam is the means of reconciliation. It's a peace treaty embedded in daily interaction.

Unfortunately, we've made a mockery of this greeting. We mumble it carelessly: "salam" without proper pronunciation or attention. We text it as a formality. Board members exchange salam in meetings while harboring grudges and planning legal action against each other. Husband and wife say it while anger still burns between them. The words are empty shells.

The ayah ends with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَسِيبًا - "Indeed, Allah is ever, over all things, an Accountant." The word حَسِيبًا emphasizes meticulous accounting. Even these small interactions - these greetings and responses - are being recorded. Nothing is too minor for Allah's attention. Every salutation, every response, every sincere or insincere greeting is noted.

translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him. He will surely assemble you for [account on] the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt. And who is more truthful than Allah in statement
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۗ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حَدِيثًا

After discussing social interactions and greetings of peace, Allah ﷻ reminds us when the true meaning and value of these greetings will become fully apparent.

The ayah begins with the fundamental declaration: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - "Allah - there is no deity except Him." This is the foundation of Islamic belief, stated with absolute clarity.

Then comes the certainty: لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - "He will surely gather you for the Day of Resurrection." The emphatic lam at the beginning and the emphasis in the verb structure leave no room for doubt. This gathering is inevitable, certain, decreed. The phrase إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ uses "towards" the Day of Standing, indicating movement toward that appointed time when every human will stand for accountability.

When we stand on that Day, what will we hope for? Safety. Peace. Security. All those greetings of salam we exchanged in this life - were they sincere? Did we truly wish peace upon our brothers and sisters? Did we make peace when we said "Assalamu alaikum"? Those accumulated blessings, those sincere prayers for each other's wellbeing, will matter immensely on that Day when we desperately need peace.

The ayah emphasizes: لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ - "about which there is no doubt." The Day is absolutely certain. It's not a possibility or probability. It's a guaranteed reality.

Finally, Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question: وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حَدِيثًا - "And who is more truthful than Allah in statement?" The word حَدِيثًا means "speech" or "word" or "promise." When Allah ﷻ gives His word about something, it is the ultimate truth. No statement can be more reliable, more certain, more trustworthy than the word of Allah ﷻ. He has promised this gathering, this accounting, this Day of Standing. It will happen exactly as He has declared.

translation: What is [the matter] with you [that you are] two groups concerning the hypocrites, while Allah has made them fall back [into error and disbelief] for what they earned. Do you wish to guide those whom Allah has sent astray? And he whom Allah sends astray - never will you find for him a way [of guidance]
commentary:
۞ فَمَا لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ وَٱللَّهُ أَرْكَسَهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوٓا۟ ۚ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَهْدُوا۟ مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا

The Quran now returns to address the complex issue of the hypocrites, showing how social laws and matters of warfare are interwoven in Surah An-Nisa.

When the Prophet ﷺ migrated from Makkah to Madinah, there were different categories of people left behind in Makkah. Some believers were genuinely unable to migrate - they were oppressed, weak, or prevented from leaving. Allah ﷻ addressed their situation in earlier passages. However, another group had the full capability to migrate but chose not to. They could have left but deliberately decided to stay, unwilling to make that life change or sacrifice. These people claimed Islam but refused the hijrah (migration) that was obligatory upon them at that time.

Allah ﷻ essentially declared this second group to be munafiqeen - hypocrites. They said they believed but their actions contradicted their claim. This created confusion among the Muslims in Madinah: How should we regard these people? If we encounter them in battle, are they Muslims or enemies?

Allah ﷻ responds: فَمَا لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ - "What is [the matter] with you [that you are] two groups concerning the hypocrites?" Why are you divided into two factions about them? Some Muslims wanted to give them the benefit of the doubt, while others recognized them as threats.

Allah ﷻ clarifies the matter decisively: وَٱللَّهُ أَرْكَسَهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوٓا۟ - "while Allah has reversed them for what they have earned." The verb أَرْكَسَ (arkasa) means to turn something upside down, like when cloth is hung upside down and dipped in dye. Allah ﷻ has completely reversed their status. Whatever good deeds they claimed, whatever commitments they made, whatever words they spoke - all of it has been turned upside down and nullified because of their actions. Their refusal to migrate when able demonstrated that their faith was not genuine.

Then comes a powerful rhetorical question: أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَهْدُوا۟ مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ - "Do you wish to guide those whom Allah has sent astray?" Are you hoping to guide people whom Allah ﷻ Himself has determined don't deserve guidance at this point? Allah ﷻ has already passed judgment on their state based on their choices and actions.

The ayah concludes with a general principle: وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا - "And whoever Allah sends astray - never will you find for him a way." When Allah ﷻ decrees someone has gone astray as a consequence of their own choices, you will not find a path to guide them.

This raises the question many people ask: Why would Allah ﷻ misguide someone? The answer lies in understanding that Allah's misguidance is always a response to human choice. When people persistently reject truth, make choices that distance them from guidance, and demonstrate through their actions that they don't want to be guided, Allah ﷻ may seal that state as a consequence of their own earning. The phrase بِمَا كَسَبُوٓا۟ - "for what they earned" - makes this clear. Their misguidance is the result of what they themselves did.

translation: They wish you would disbelieve as they disbelieved so you would be alike. So do not take from among them allies until they emigrate for the cause of Allah. But if they turn away, then seize them and kill them wherever you find them and take not from among them any ally or helper
commentary:
وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَمَا كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَكُونُونَ سَوَآءً ۖ فَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا

Allah ﷻ now reveals the true intentions of these hypocrites who remained in Makkah. The ayah exposes what lies in their hearts: وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَمَا كَفَرُوا۟ - "They wish you would disbelieve as they disbelieved." Despite their claims of faith, these people want the Muslims to abandon Islam just as they have essentially abandoned it.

The phrase كَمَا كَفَرُوا۟ - "as they disbelieved" - refers back to what was mentioned in the previous ayah. Allah ﷻ said He "reversed them" (arkasahum), meaning they have effectively left the religion, though they're keeping their options open. They haven't publicly announced their disbelief because if the Muslims win, their stored claim to Islam might come in handy. They're hedging their bets.

Their wish is: فَتَكُونُونَ سَوَآءً - "so you would be equal." They want everyone to be the same, all equally distant from true faith and commitment. This reflects a psychological reality: people often want others to sink to their level rather than rising themselves.

Based on this reality, Allah ﷻ commands: فَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ - "So do not take from among them allies." The word أَوْلِيَآءَ means protective friends, people you rely upon, people you trust with important matters. These hypocrites cannot be trusted as reliable allies or friends.

However, Allah ﷻ provides a path back: حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "until they emigrate in the way of Allah." If they make hijrah, demonstrating through this action their genuine commitment to Islam, then the situation changes. Migration in that context was the proof of sincerity, the test that separated genuine believers from those merely claiming faith.

But if they refuse: فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ - "But if they turn away, then seize them and kill them wherever you find them." This command must be understood in its proper context: a state of war existed between the Muslims and the Makkan disbelievers. These individuals claimed to be Muslim but refused to migrate and align themselves with the Muslim community. The risk was real that they were spies, gathering intelligence from Muslims who trusted them as fellow believers, then passing that information to the enemy.

When such people "turn away" after being given the chance to prove their sincerity through migration, and they're found in contexts suggesting espionage or collaboration with the enemy, the Muslims were commanded to treat them as the enemy combatants they had proven themselves to be.

The ayah concludes by repeating the prohibition: وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا - "and do not take from among them a protector or a helper." Don't rely on them for friendship, protection, or assistance. They have demonstrated they cannot be trusted.

translation: Except for those who take refuge with a people between yourselves and whom is a treaty or those who come to you, their hearts strained at [the prospect of] fighting you or fighting their own people. And if Allah had willed, He could have given them power over you, and they would have fought you. So if they remove themselves from you and do not fight you and offer you peace, then Allah has not made for you a cause [for fighting] against them
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ أَوْ جَآءُوكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ أَن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ أَوْ يُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَسَلَّطَهُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ فَلَقَـٰتَلُوكُمْ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ فَمَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلًا

After establishing the harsh ruling regarding hypocrites who refuse to migrate and may be working with the enemy, Allah ﷻ immediately provides critical exceptions. Islamic law is precise and just, never painting situations with a broad brush.

The first exception: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ - "Except for those who take refuge with a people between yourselves and whom is a treaty." This addresses a specific scenario: someone was labeled as a hypocrite because they refused to migrate from Makkah. They realized the pressure on them was increasing, so they left Makkah - but they didn't come to Madinah. Instead, they went to another tribe, and that tribe happened to have a peace treaty with the Muslims.

This person is no longer in enemy territory (Makkah), and they're not in Madinah, but they're in a place where Muslims have diplomatic relations and agreements. The ruling: you cannot pursue them there. They've removed themselves from the battlefield, and they're under the protection of a treaty. Muslims must honor their treaties.

The second exception describes a different situation: أَوْ جَآءُوكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ أَن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ أَوْ يُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ - "Or those who come to you, their hearts strained at [the prospect of] fighting you or fighting their own people." The phrase حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ beautifully captures a state of inner exhaustion and constriction. Their chests have become tight; they're mentally and emotionally drained.

These are people who say: "I can't handle this war situation anymore. The Makkans want me to fight with them against you. You want me to prove myself by fighting them. I don't want to fight anyone. I want nothing to do with any of this. Just leave me alone. I'm not necessarily a committed Muslim, I'm not a loyal ally, I just want to live. Keep me out of it."

They come to the Muslims in this state of surrender and exhaustion, seeking only to be left alone. They're not claiming faith or seeking privileges. They're simply asking for neutrality.

Allah ﷻ reveals what could have been: وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَسَلَّطَهُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ فَلَقَـٰتَلُوكُمْ - "And if Allah had willed, He could have given them power over you, and they would have fought you." Had Allah ﷻ wished, these same people who now come exhausted and defeated could have gained power and influence. When they saw the Makkans winning, they might have turned against the Muslims and fought with full force. But Allah ﷻ didn't allow that scenario to unfold.

The ruling for such people: فَإِنِ ٱعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ - "So if they remove themselves from you and do not fight you and offer you peace." Three conditions are mentioned: they stay away from you (ٱعْتَزَلُوكُمْ - they isolate themselves, they don't engage), they don't fight you, and they extend peace to you, saying "We're at peace with you."

When all three conditions are met: فَمَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلًا - "then Allah has not made for you a cause [for aggression] against them." The phrase مَا جَعَلَ - "has not made" or "has not allowed" - is emphatic. Allah ﷻ has not given you any path, any right, any justification to pursue action against them.

You cannot make a case against them. You cannot pursue any course of action against them. They've removed themselves from the conflict, they haven't fought you, and they've offered peace. Islamic law recognizes their neutrality and commands Muslims to respect it.

These exceptions demonstrate the precision and mercy in Islamic legislation. Even in the midst of war, even when dealing with hypocrites and potential traitors, the law distinguishes between different situations and different levels of threat. Those who genuinely seek peace or take refuge in protected territories are not to be harmed. Justice, precision, and the honoring of treaties remain paramount, even during conflict.

translation: You will find others who wish to obtain security from you and [to] obtain security from their people. Every time they are returned to [the influence of] disbelief, they fall back into it. So if they do not withdraw from you or offer you peace or restrain their hands, then seize them and kill them wherever you overtake them. And those - We have made for you against them a clear authorization
commentary:
سَتَجِدُونَ ءَاخَرِينَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَأْمَنُوكُمْ وَيَأْمَنُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ كُلَّ مَا رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ أُرْكِسُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَعْتَزِلُوكُمْ وَيُلْقُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ وَيَكُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ جَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا

This ayah addresses a different category of people from those mentioned in the previous passage. The earlier group had withdrawn from conflict entirely, maintaining neutrality between the Muslims and their own people. They made their position clear: they would not fight the believers and asked only to be left alone.

However, Allah ﷻ now describes another group entirely: يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَأْمَنُوكُمْ وَيَأْمَنُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ - "they want to make sure that they're safe with you, and they want to make sure they're safe with their nation." At first glance, this sounds similar to the previous group.
