The crucial difference lies in what comes next: كُلَّ مَا رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ أُرْكِسُوا۟ فِيهَا - "every time they are turned back to a situation of fitnah, they are turned upside down in it."

The word أُرْكِسُوا۟ carries the imagery of being flipped upside down, of tumbling headfirst into something. These are opportunists whose declarations of peace are conditional and temporary. Whenever their sentiments of peace from both sides are tested, whenever they see an advantage on one side, they betray their word. When the market shifts in favor of the disbelievers, they calculate that they can profit more by siding with them again. Their loyalties are perpetually for sale to the highest bidder.

Allah ﷻ then establishes clear guidelines for dealing with such duplicitous groups: فَإِن لَّمْ يَعْتَزِلُوكُمْ وَيُلْقُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ وَيَكُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ - "If they do not withdraw from you, and extend peace to you, and restrain their hands." Notice the grammatical precision here. The ayah says يُلْقُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ rather than يُسَلِّمُوا۟. This choice of wording emphasizes the continuation of their earlier claim. They had extended peace initially, but when opportunity arose, they attacked anyway.

The command is then decisive: فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ - "then seize them and kill them wherever you find them." These are traitors, and they represent too dangerous an entity to be left unchecked in a state of war. Their betrayal makes them active combatants despite their protestations of peace.

The ayah concludes: وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ جَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا - "And those are the ones - We have made for you against them a clear authority." The word سُلْطَـٰنًا means an open, crystal-clear authority. When you take military action against such traitors, you are totally and absolutely justified. There should be no guilt, no hesitation, no second-guessing. Allah ﷻ Himself has granted you this clear authorization.

translation: And never is it for a believer to kill a believer except by mistake. And whoever kills a believer by mistake - then the freeing of a believing slave and a compensation payment presented to the deceased's family [is required] unless they give [up their right as] charity. But if the deceased was from a people at war with you and he was a believer - then [only] the freeing of a believing slave; and if he was from a people with whom you have a treaty - then a compensation payment presented to his family and the freeing of a believing slave. And whoever does not find [one or cannot afford to buy one] - then [instead], a fast for two months consecutively, [seeking] acceptance of repentance from Allah. And Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَن يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِنًا إِلَّا خَطَـًٔا ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَ مُؤْمِنًا خَطَـًٔا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ وَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَصَّدَّقُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَّكُمْ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ فَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ وَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ تَوْبَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

After discussing battlefield strategies and the rules of engagement with various groups, this ayah shifts to address what we might call "friendly fire" - the tragic situation where one believer accidentally kills another. The ayah begins with a powerful statement: وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَن يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِنًا إِلَّا خَطَـًٔا - "It is not becoming for any believer to kill another believer except by mistake."

The phrase مَا كَانَ expresses something that is fundamentally inappropriate, something that should never characterize a believer. The only exception is خَطَـًٔا - unintentional error, genuine mistake. This could occur in various scenarios: a spear or arrow shot in battle that strikes the wrong person, an explosion where someone else dies, or in our contemporary context, situations like accidentally pushing someone in circumstances that lead to their death.

Allah ﷻ then establishes the recompense for involuntary manslaughter: وَمَن قَتَلَ مُؤْمِنًا خَطَـًٔا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ وَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦٓ - "Whoever kills a believer by mistake, then the freeing of a believing slave and blood money delivered to his family." There are two distinct obligations here. The first is تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ - freeing a believing slave. This is your compensation to Allah ﷻ, your way of bringing yourself back to Him after this terrible mistake.

The second obligation is دِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ - blood money submitted to the family. The word مُّسَلَّمَةٌ is significant. It implies that the amount is exact, complete, and given without reluctance or delay. It's not partial payment, not installments, not something given begrudgingly. It is submitted absolutely and completely to the family of the deceased.

The ayah then adds إِلَّآ أَن يَصَّدَّقُوا۟ - "unless they give it away in charity." If the family chooses to forgive and not accept the blood money, that is their right. However, and this is crucial, even if they forgive their portion, you still owe Allah ﷻ His portion. You must still free the slave. The order in which these are mentioned teaches us something profound: Allah's right is mentioned first because even if people forgive you, you still need to seek forgiveness from Allah ﷻ.

The ayah then addresses three specific scenarios based on the victim's tribal and religious affiliations:

First scenario: فَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَّكُمْ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ - "If he was from a nation that is your enemy, but he himself was a believer, then free a believing slave." In this case, the person you killed accidentally was Muslim, but his entire family and tribe are non-Muslim enemies at war with the Muslims. You still owe Allah ﷻ compensation through freeing a slave, but you do not give blood money to the family. Why? Because if you help them financially, those resources will be used to further the war effort against the Muslims. That makes no strategic or moral sense. But you still owe Allah ﷻ His due.

Second scenario: وَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ فَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ وَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ - "If he was from a people with whom you have a treaty, then blood money delivered to his family and the freeing of a believing slave." Here the victim's tribe may not be Muslim, but you have a peace treaty with them. In this case, both obligations apply fully. You give them the blood money because you have peace with them, so those funds cannot be used against the Muslims. And you still free a slave to fulfill your obligation to Allah ﷻ.

Finally, the ayah addresses practical limitations: فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ - "Whoever cannot find [a slave to free], then fasting two consecutive months." This accommodation recognizes that freeing a slave might not always be possible. The alternative is fasting for two months consecutively, without breaking the sequence. This extended act of worship becomes the means of seeking Allah's forgiveness.

The ayah concludes by framing this as تَوْبَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ - "repentance from Allah" - meaning this is how Allah ﷻ has opened the door of repentance for this grave mistake. And as always, وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا - "Allah has always been All-Knowing, All-Wise." He knows the circumstances of every case, and His legislation reflects perfect wisdom in balancing justice, mercy and societal wellbeing.

translation: But whoever kills a believer intentionally - his recompense is Hell, wherein he will abide eternally, and Allah has become angry with him and has cursed him and has prepared for him a great punishment
commentary:
وَمَن يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِنًا مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآؤُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَلَعَنَهُۥ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُۥ عَذَابًا عَظِيمًا

The transition from the previous ayah to this one contains a subtle but profound grammatical shift. The earlier ayah used the past tense: وَمَن قَتَلَ - "whoever killed." Past tense suggests something that happens once, a singular occurrence. This makes perfect sense for accidental killing - a mistake happens once. If the same "mistake" keeps happening every weekend, it's clearly not accidental anymore.

But notice how this ayah begins: وَمَن يَقْتُلْ - using the present tense, which in Arabic carries implications of continuity and repetition. This is someone who kills deliberately, and deliberate killing carries with it the potential to kill again. The choice of present tense subtly indicates an ongoing criminal disposition rather than a one-time error.

The ayah describes the one who مُّتَعَمِّدًا - deliberately, intentionally, on purpose - kills a believer. The punishment outlined is staggering in its severity, with multiple layers: فَجَزَآؤُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ - "his recompense is Jahannam." This alone would be devastating enough, but Allah ﷻ adds خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا - "remaining in it eternally." 

Then comes the second layer: وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ - "Allah is angry with him." The anger of the Creator, the One who gave life, directed at someone who deliberately took the life of a believer.

Third layer: وَلَعَنَهُۥ - "and He has cursed him." To be removed from Allah's mercy, to be distanced from His grace.

Fourth layer: وَأَعَدَّ لَهُۥ عَذَابًا عَظِيمًا - "and He has prepared for him a tremendous punishment." Even beyond Jahannam itself, there is additional tremendous punishment prepared.

Look at how these punishments accumulate: eternal Hellfire, plus Allah's anger, plus His curse, plus tremendous additional punishment. All of this for what single crime? The deliberate murder of a believer. This shows us how serious, how grave, how monumentally catastrophic the crime of murder is in Islam.

This stacking of punishments forces us to confront a painful reality about the Muslim world today. How cheap Muslim blood has become. How easy it is for people to kill each other. The news brings daily reports: twenty died here, ten died in a car explosion there, a shooting inside a masjid, someone opening fire in a marketplace. It's constant, relentless. There seems to be no value left for human life.

What does this signify? It reveals that there is no real Iman in Allah ﷻ left in the hearts of those who commit such acts. Someone who believes in Allah ﷻ, who believes even the slightest bit in standing before Him on the Day of Judgment, cannot possibly take this crime lightly. The weight of this ayah, the layers of punishment described, should make any believer's hand freeze before committing such an act. When murder becomes casual, it's a symptom that faith has been casualties as well.

translation: O you who have believed, when you go forth [to fight] in the cause of Allah, investigate; and do not say to one who gives you [a greeting of] peace "You are not a believer," aspiring for the goods of worldly life; for with Allah are many acquisitions. You [yourselves] were like that before; then Allah conferred His favor upon you, so investigate. Indeed Allah is ever, with what you do, Acquainted
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا۟ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَغَانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا

This ayah begins with a direct address: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ - "O you who have believed." What follows is crucial guidance about judgment and assumptions, particularly in uncertain situations.

إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "When you travel in the path of Allah." The word ضَرَبْتُمْ comes from the root meaning to strike or beat. It conveys the image of traveling so far that your footsteps beat heavily against the earth. You've gone way out there into unknown territory, exhausted, each step a heavy impact on the ground. You're in unfamiliar land, far from home.

The command is immediate and emphatic: فَتَبَيَّنُوا۟ - "then clarify, then verify, then get clear information." Gather intelligence. Understand who these people are. Know where you're going and who you're dealing with. Don't make assumptions.

Then comes the heart of the instruction: وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا - "Do not say to the one who extends salam to you, 'You are not a believer.'" The word أَلْقَىٰٓ means "throws" or "extends." Someone throws salam your way - "As-salamu alaykum" - and this is their opening greeting to you.

Consider the context here. In Arabian tradition, Muslims and non-Muslims dressed similarly. There was no distinct "Islamic uniform." When someone approached you and said salam, it wasn't because they saw you wearing Islamic garb and identified you as Muslim. They couldn't tell by looking. They took a risk in extending that salam because they didn't know if you were Muslim either. By saying it first, they made their identity known. They took the initiative to identify themselves.

But here's the remarkable thing: appearance is not the judge of Islam. Appearance is not what determines someone's faith. When somebody extends salam to you, you have no right to judge beyond that point. This is the divine standard.

Now understand the situational context of this revelation. This is a state of war. There's legitimate reason to fear that someone approaching could be a spy. This could literally be a matter of life and death. The person saying salam to you in enemy territory might actually be trying to kill you. You would be completely justified in being skeptical, in questioning their motives, in being cautious about whether they're really Muslim.

Yet even in that scenario - even when your life could be at stake, even when skepticism seems not just reasonable but necessary for survival - Allah ﷻ still says: don't you dare say they're not a believer. Notice something else profound: Allah ﷻ doesn't even say "don't say they're not Muslim." He says لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا - "you are not a mu'min, not a true believer." Don't question their level of faith.

Let this sink in. In a situation where you are absolutely justified in being skeptical, where your very life might depend on accurate assessment, where every instinct tells you to be cautious, Allah ﷻ still removes from you the right to judge someone's Iman once they've extended salam.

Now apply this principle to our communities, our Muslim societies, our interactions with each other. We're not in a state of war where someone greeting us might be about to kill us. We're not in life-and-death situations. Yet how casually we judge each other's faith! How easily we throw around accusations of deviation, misguidance, even kufr.

There are entire online campaigns dedicated to warning people: "Don't listen to that shaykh, he'll take you to Hellfire. Don't listen to this group or that group. Don't pray behind them." We've created a rhetoric across the Muslim world where we question not just the Islam but the Iman of people we've never even met. And if we met them, they'd probably say salam to us.

The tragedy deepens when you realize this happens in masjids. Brothers have said salam to others who knowingly, deliberately, look right at them and refuse to respond. Young people have approached others saying, "Brother, you're calling people to the Hellfire." The labels fly: deviant, kafir, spy, covert agent, Zionist, Orientalist. People receive these accusations constantly.

What has happened to us? There is one thing that comes out of a person's mouth that should end all speculation, all judgment about their faith. What is that one word? As-salamu alaykum. After that, according to Allah ﷻ, this conversation is over. You have no license to judge their Iman. You can disagree with someone's ideas, critique their arguments, say "with all due respect, I disagree with this position." But you have no authority to judge their faith.

Allah ﷻ identifies the real motivation: تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - "You are seeking the temporary goods of worldly life." What does this mean in a battlefield context? The person walks up, says salam, has a nice bag with them. You think, "Are you sure you're Muslim? That's a nice bag you have there." You want their possessions. You want their goods. You're looking for an excuse to take their wealth.

How does this manifest in our time? It's tragic what's become of the religious segment of the Muslim community, even in the West. From a pure observation standpoint, it resembles a fight over market share. Let's be generous and say 95% of Muslims don't even know where the masjid is. The vast majority of Muslims living in the West are far removed from religious practice. They're disconnected from Islam in their daily lives.

The small percentage who are trying to hold onto some sense of community, who attend classes and conferences, who are engaged with religious learning - this tiny minority is being fought over by competing ideologies. "Come take our class, don't take their class. Go to our masjid, not theirs. Listen to this imam, not that one. They will call you to the Hellfire. No, those other ones will call you to Hellfire. These guys are this way, those guys are that way."

Meanwhile, the vast 95% majority looks at this infighting and concludes: "These religious people are idiots. That's why I'm not religious - because all they do is bicker among themselves. They have so much time for this petty stuff." Youth come asking about terms they've heard in the masjid - labels and classifications they don't understand. They ask, "Does not knowing these terms make me an ignorant Muslim?" The answer should be: "Not knowing those labels makes you a protected Muslim, brother. You don't need to know what all these divisive names mean."

We've turned Islam into a turf war. We're taking basic principles of our religion - like the one that says once someone extends salam, you shouldn't judge past that - and we're ignoring them. What's most tragic is that our scholars, those who are supposedly knowledgeable, are willing to participate in this. They put these labels forward. They should be on a crusade against labeling and judging other Muslims, but instead some perpetuate it.

"But brother, we have to speak the truth! That person is calling to shirk, to kufr, to deviation. We have to stand up for truth!" Really? Let's examine our priorities. What truths do we need to stand up for first? Before you make combating other Muslims your crusade, what are the actual priorities that our religion wants from us?

We have no sense of priority. What are the priorities for an individual? What are the priorities for a community? There are fundamental, pressing issues we haven't even begun to address. What does it mean to be a father in Islam? What does it mean to be a mother, a husband, a wife? What does it mean to be an ethical Muslim in the workplace? These are basic foundational questions we haven't come to terms with yet.

But we're ready to discuss how someone quotes from a book written in the seventh century that was influenced by this scholar who was influenced by that one, and therefore they're calling people to Hellfire? Most of those arguments don't benefit a single person. If they have any benefit at all, it's in closed academic circles studying specialized topics. They have zero benefit for the masses of Muslims.

The Messenger of Allah ﷺ didn't prioritize abstract theological debates. Someone asked him about the timing of the Hour, and his response? He asked about their preparation for it, not philosophical speculation about when it would occur. Someone asked complex questions about Allah's attributes, and the Sahabah (RA) learned to focus on who Allah ﷻ is - the Merciful, the Forgiving, the Creator, the Sustainer - rather than abstract philosophical puzzles.

We've made questions central to our religion that were never meant to be central. We use those questions to label each other. This isn't about siding with one group over another - these questions themselves are often irrelevant for the average Muslim. They're subjects for people deeply immersed in specialized study, not matters that should divide the Ummah.

Allah ﷻ reminds us: فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَغَانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ - "With Allah are abundant spoils, abundant benefits." Don't look for benefits in discounting other Muslims, in tearing each other down. True benefit comes from Allah ﷻ.

Then comes a powerful reminder: كَذَٰلِكَ كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ - "This is how you were before, then Allah conferred favor upon you." You used to be judgmental, dismissive, looking for reasons to attack others. That was your jahiliyyah, your ignorance. Allah ﷻ gave you Islam as a huge favor to elevate you beyond that ignorance. Why would you want to return to it?

The command is repeated for emphasis: فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ - "So verify, clarify." The question of clarification is simple: Did the person say salam? That should be enough for you. That's your verification.

The ayah closes with a reminder that should make us all pause: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا - "Indeed Allah has full knowledge, full awareness of whatever you have been doing." Allah ﷻ knows the reality behind every judgment we make, every label we throw, every time we question someone's faith. He has complete account of all our actions. And on the Day we stand before Him, we'll answer for how we treated His servants, how we judged them, and whether we followed His guidance about extending the benefit of the doubt when someone says salam.

translation: Not equal are those believers remaining [at home] - other than the disabled - and the mujahideen, [who strive and fight] in the cause of Allah with their wealth and their lives. Allah has preferred the mujahideen through their wealth and their lives over those who remain [behind], by degrees. And to both Allah has promised the best [reward]. But Allah has preferred the mujahideen over those who remain [behind] with a great reward
commentary:
لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْقَـٰعِدُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ غَيْرُ أُو۟لِى ٱلضَّرَرِ وَٱلْمُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَفَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

This ayah establishes a crucial distinction between those believers who stayed behind and those who went out to struggle in Allah's ﷻ path. The word الْقَـٰعِدُونَ refers to those who sat behind or remained at home. Allah ﷻ is not necessarily condemning all those who stayed behind, but rather clarifying that those who showed initiative and went out to fight hold a higher rank.

The ayah immediately makes an exception: غَيْرُ أُو۟لِى ٱلضَّرَرِ - "except those with disabilities or harm." This refers to believers who were afflicted with some kind of harm, disability or genuine incapacity. They are not included in this comparison because their circumstances prevented them from participating. However, for those who were not disabled, who were not hurt, and who still didn't come out to fight in the path of Allah ﷻ - they cannot be equated with وَٱلْمُجَـٰهِدُونَ, those who actually went out and struggled in Allah's ﷻ path with their wealth and their very selves.

Allah ﷻ then declares: فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً - "Allah has given preference and a higher rank to those who struggle with their wealth and themselves over those who sit behind, by a degree." This is a clear statement of spiritual ranking. Those who sacrificed and struggled have been elevated.

Yet the ayah continues with remarkable mercy: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ - "And to all, Allah has promised the best." This means that even those who stayed behind - so long as they were sincere Muslims who had some valid reason for not participating, even if they were capable - they are still promised the best reward. This is not the same severe verdict given to those who refused to make hijrah from Makkah. Perhaps someone didn't go out in a particular battle like Badr or Uhud, but they remained good, faithful Muslims. A sweeping condemnation is not made against them.

Nevertheless, Allah ﷻ concludes by reiterating: وَفَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - "And Allah has given preference to the mujahideen over those who sit behind with a tremendous reward."

translation: Degrees [of high position] from Him and forgiveness and mercy. And Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
دَرَجَـٰتٍ مِّنْهُ وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

This ayah elaborates on the tremendous reward mentioned at the end of the previous ayah. These are دَرَجَـٰتٍ مِّنْهُ - various ranks and elevations that come directly from Allah ﷻ Himself. These are spiritual stations that no human being can assign to another. Only Allah ﷻ knows who truly deserves which rank.

In addition to these elevated stations, Allah ﷻ promises وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً - forgiveness and mercy. These ranks are not something we can label each other with or compete over in a worldly sense. They are divine recognitions of sincere sacrifice and struggle.

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا - "And Allah has always been extremely forgiving, always merciful." This closing is significant. Even though the word الْقَـٰعِدُونَ (those who sat behind) is not necessarily a compliment in Arabic, and even though Allah ﷻ has stated they'll receive الْحُسْنَىٰ (the best), there's something between the lines. Allah ﷻ is telling them: you should have come out too. And by ending with His forgiveness and mercy, He's saying the door is still open. It's not too late to change.

translation: Indeed, those whom the angels take [in death] while wronging themselves - [the angels] will say, "In what [condition] were you?" They will say, "We were oppressed in the land." The angels will say, "Was not the earth of Allah spacious [enough] for you to emigrate therein?" For those, their refuge is Hell - and evil it is as a destination
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ فِيمَ كُنتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ ٱللَّهِ وَٰسِعَةً فَتُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا

Now the ayah presents a stark contrast. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ - "Indeed, those whom the angels take in death..." The scene shifts to the moment of death, when the angels come to claim someone's soul. These are people who died ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ - while they were in a state of wronging themselves.

This refers to those people in Makkah who didn't make hijrah when they could have, who were mixing their iman with kufr. It may also include the hypocrites who were in Madinah but didn't go out to battle, who lacked sincerity. These people are not under the protection of the previous ayah's promise. They're not among those to whom Allah ﷻ promised الْحُسْنَىٰ.

The angels will confront them: قَالُوا۟ فِيمَ كُنتُمْ - "They will say: In what state were you?" The Arabic فِيمَ كُنتُمْ literally means "in what were you?" implying: what was your situation? What state were you in? Why didn't you do something about your circumstances?

The deceased will offer an excuse: قَالُوا۟ كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "They will say: We were oppressed and weakened in the land." They're claiming they had no power, no choice, no ability to change their situation.

But the angels won't accept this excuse: قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ ٱللَّهِ وَٰسِعَةً فَتُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِيهَا - "Was not the earth of Allah spacious enough for you to migrate within it?" Notice that Allah ﷻ didn't explicitly say these people failed to migrate. He said they were ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ - wronging themselves. They were engaged in wrongdoing, and then they blamed their environment. "We were weak, what could we do?" But Allah's ﷻ earth is vast. There's always somewhere to go if you're sincere about protecting your faith.

The verdict is severe: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا - "Those are the ones whose refuge is Hell, and what a horrible destination." The word سَآءَتْ appears here - an expression of how terrible something is to return to.

translation: Except for the oppressed among men, women and children who cannot devise a plan nor are they directed to a way
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا

But Allah ﷻ immediately provides an exception: إِلَّا ٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ - "Except the truly weakened ones." These are people upon whom weakness was genuinely imposed, مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ - from among the men, the women and the children.

Their situation is described: لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً - "They are not able to devise any strategy." The word حِيلَةً refers to a kind of trickery or clever strategy used to escape from something. Interestingly, this same word was later used in Islamic history when people tried to evade Islamic financial obligations - like giving all their assets to their wife as a "gift" right before zakat was due, then having her give it back after the audit. That's حِيلَةً - a clever scheme to get around something.

But these truly oppressed people لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً - they can't devise any trick or strategy to escape. وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا - "And they are not able to find their way to a path." This isn't about religious guidance here. It means they literally cannot find a way out. There's no escape route for them. They're completely stuck, truly trapped with no options.

translation: For those it is expected that Allah will pardon them, and Allah is ever Pardoning and Forgiving
commentary:
فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا

For those truly oppressed people described in the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ says: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ - "Those are the ones for whom there is hope that Allah will pardon them." The word عَسَى indicates strong possibility and hope - Allah ﷻ is likely to pardon them.

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا - "And Allah has always been extremely pardoning, tremendously forgiving." The combination of عَفُوًّا (extremely pardoning) and غَفُورًا (tremendously forgiving) emphasizes Allah's ﷻ willingness to overlook the shortcomings of those who were genuinely trapped in impossible circumstances.

translation: And whoever emigrates for the cause of Allah will find on the earth many [alternative] locations and abundance. And whoever leaves his home as an emigrant to Allah and His Messenger and then death overtakes him - his reward has already become incumbent upon Allah. And Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
۞ وَمَن يُهَاجِرْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يَجِدْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُرَٰغَمًا كَثِيرًا وَسَعَةً ۚ وَمَن يَخْرُجْ مِنۢ بَيْتِهِۦ مُهَاجِرًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ ٱلْمَوْتُ فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

This ayah provides tremendous encouragement for those who choose to migrate for Allah's ﷻ sake: وَمَن يُهَاجِرْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "And whoever migrates in the path of Allah..."

The promise is: يَجِدْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُرَٰغَمًا كَثِيرًا - "He will find in the earth abundant muragham." Let's examine this fascinating word. مُرَٰغَمًا (muragham) comes from the root راغم which originally means dirt. You might say someone's nose was filled with dirt during a sandstorm. But the word evolved to mean land that is acquired after a quarrel or conflict.

Picture two people fighting out in a field, dust rising around them, and at the end of the struggle, some patch of land is seized. That land is called muragham - it came as a result of rising dust, as a result of conflict, something that was taken from someone else. So Allah ﷻ is saying: whoever migrates in His path will find opportunities to seize land, to take territory, to establish themselves somewhere. This was fulfilled for the Muslims who migrated to Madinah - they were given a patch of land that was literally taken from those who opposed them, like the influence of Abdullah ibn Ubayy and others who no longer had control.

And not just a patch of land, but وَسَعَةً - expansion. Allah ﷻ will expand that territory even further. What starts as a small foothold becomes something much greater.

Then comes one of the most moving promises in the Quran: وَمَن يَخْرُجْ مِنۢ بَيْتِهِۦ مُهَاجِرًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ ٱلْمَوْتُ - "And whoever leaves his house as a migrant toward Allah and His Messenger, and then death overtakes him..." Notice the addition of وَرَسُولِهِۦ (and His Messenger) - this indicates he was migrating specifically to where the Messenger ﷺ was, which in this historical context means Madinah. To migrate to Allah ﷻ meant to migrate to the Messenger ﷺ.

For this person who dies on the journey: فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ - "Then his reward has become an obligation upon Allah." This is one of the most powerful expressions of reward in the entire Quran. Allah ﷻ has taken personal responsibility to ensure this person receives their compensation. 

We often think the most powerful descriptions of Paradise are the gardens, rivers and trees. But actually, the most powerful descriptions are the ones where Allah ﷻ says: "It's so magnificent, all I can tell you is that I Myself will take care of them." When the reward is beyond words, Allah ﷻ simply says, "I've got this. I'm taking care of them." These are the most powerful expressions of reward - when Allah ﷻ Himself guarantees it.

On a side note, this is one reason why I personally believe it's actually a mercy from Allah ﷻ that He didn't explicitly detail all the rewards for women in Paradise. The shared rewards are mentioned - gardens, rivers, all of that is obviously for everyone, not just men. And yes, there are some gender-specific rewards mentioned for men, like spouses. But the equivalent for women is left open, and in Arabic, leaving something open is not a restriction - it's an expansion.

When people ask, "How come it's not mentioned?" they're looking at it backwards. The fact that it's not detailed means it's so tremendous that it can't be put into words. Just like this ayah about the person who dies while migrating - their reward isn't listed because listing it wouldn't do it justice. When Allah ﷻ says someone's compensation is directly with Him, that's the highest honor. If He listed gardens, rivers, mountains and trees, that would actually limit it. By leaving it open, He's saying: "What they'll receive is far beyond what words can describe."

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا - "And Allah has always been extremely forgiving, always merciful." This conclusion indicates several things. Even this person who made hijrah for Allah's ﷻ sake - they had sins. Everyone makes mistakes. But because this person made such a significant life decision, because they changed their entire life and migrated toward Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, all those sins are forgiven. Allah's ﷻ reward is so immense that their previous mistakes won't get in the way. He pardons everything and enters them into His mercy.

translation: And when you travel throughout the land, there is no blame upon you for shortening the prayer, [especially] if you fear that those who disbelieve may disrupt [or attack] you. Indeed, the disbelievers are ever to you a clear enemy
commentary:
وَإِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَقْصُرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَن يَفْتِنَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ كَانُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا

This ayah shifts to practical guidance for believers traveling in dangerous circumstances: وَإِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "And when you travel out in the land..."

The permission granted is: فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَقْصُرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - "Then there is no harm upon you that you shorten the prayer." The Arabic تَقْصُرُوا۟ مِنَ literally means "that you take away from" or "diminish from" the prayers.

The condition is specified: إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَن يَفْتِنَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ - "If you fear that those who disbelieve will attack you." The word يَفْتِنَكُمُ (yaftinikum) from the root فتن can mean to test or trial, but here it means to attack or assault. So the permission to shorten prayers is given when there's a genuine fear of attack from the disbelievers.

The ayah concludes with a reminder: إِنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ كَانُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا - "Indeed, the disbelievers have always been a clear and open enemy to you." This isn't paranoia - it's reality. The hostility is real and open, so taking precautions during prayer is not just allowed but necessary.

translation: And when you are among them and lead them in prayer, let a group of them stand [in prayer] with you and let them carry their arms. And when they have prostrated, let them be [in position] behind you and have the other group come forward which has not [yet] prayed and let them pray with you, taking precaution and carrying their arms. Those who disbelieve wish that you would neglect your weapons and your baggage so they could come down upon you in one [single] attack. But there is no blame upon you, if you are troubled by rain or are ill, for putting down your arms, but take precaution. Indeed, Allah has prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment
commentary:
وَإِذَا كُنتَ فِيهِمْ فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَلْتَقُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُم مَّعَكَ وَلْيَأْخُذُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَهُمْ فَإِذَا سَجَدُوا۟ فَلْيَكُونُوا۟ مِن وَرَآئِكُمْ وَلْتَأْتِ طَآئِفَةٌ أُخْرَىٰ لَمْ يُصَلُّوا۟ فَلْيُصَلُّوا۟ مَعَكَ وَلْيَأْخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ ۗ وَدَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ تَغْفُلُونَ عَنْ أَسْلِحَتِكُمْ وَأَمْتِعَتِكُمْ فَيَمِيلُونَ عَلَيْكُم مَّيْلَةً وَٰحِدَةً ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن كَانَ بِكُمْ أَذًى مِّن مَّطَرٍ أَوْ كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَن تَضَعُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَكُمْ ۖ وَخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا

This remarkable ayah provides detailed instructions for congregational prayer during battle. It's addressed to the Prophet ﷺ: وَإِذَا كُنتَ فِيهِمْ - "And when you are among them..." This is when the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is traveling with the Muslim army and they're in a state of war.

فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ - "And you establish prayer for them" - you're leading them as their imam. The instruction begins: فَلْتَقُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُم مَّعَكَ - "Then let a group from among them stand with you."

Not everyone should join the prayer at once, because they're on the battlefield. If everyone prays together, they become vulnerable. So one group prays while another stands guard. But this raises a concern - what about the group that's fighting? They might think, "We have to miss praying behind the Messenger ﷺ as our imam? That's not fair! They get to have the Prophet ﷺ as imam and we don't?"

Allah ﷻ provides an elegant solution. The group that prays first: وَلْيَأْخُذُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَهُمْ - "And let them take their weapons with them." Even while praying, they hold their weapons. فَإِذَا سَجَدُوا۟ فَلْيَكُونُوا۟ مِن وَرَآئِكُمْ - "And when they go into prostration, let the other group be standing right behind them." Sujood is obviously the most vulnerable position, so the group not praying stands guard while the first group is in prostration.

Here's how it worked practically: The Prophet ﷺ would lead a shortened prayer (two rak'ahs since they're traveling). He would make the first rak'ah with the first group joining him. When that first rak'ah is complete, normally you'd stand up for the second rak'ah. But the Prophet ﷺ would remain sitting. The first group would then stand up, complete their second rak'ah on their own, say salam and leave to stand guard.

The Prophet ﷺ, having completed only one rak'ah, remains sitting. وَلْتَأْتِ طَآئِفَةٌ أُخْرَىٰ لَمْ يُصَلُّوا۟ - "And let another group come that has not prayed." They say "Allahu Akbar" and sit down. Then the Prophet ﷺ stands up for what is his second rak'ah but their first rak'ah, and they pray together. At the end, the Prophet ﷺ says salam (he's completed his two rak'ahs), but this second group stands up and completes their own second rak'ah before saying salam.

The result? Both groups got to pray congregation behind the Prophet ﷺ, and both groups maintained security. It's a remarkable display of discipline and military organization. Just imagine the level of coordination, the focus, the trust that would be required to execute this properly in the midst of a battlefield situation.

The instruction continues: وَلْيَأْخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ - "And let them take their caution and their weapons." Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't just say "take your weapons," He says حِذْرَهُمْ (their caution) first, then weapons. Why both? Because the transition time when one group finishes and another comes is particularly vulnerable. That's when alertness must be at its peak.

وَدَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ تَغْفُلُونَ عَنْ أَسْلِحَتِكُمْ وَأَمْتِعَتِكُمْ - "The disbelievers would love it if you became heedless of your weapons and your supplies." They're watching for any moment of carelessness, any time you set your sword down, any time your military supplies are left unguarded. فَيَمِيلُونَ عَلَيْكُم مَّيْلَةً وَٰحِدَةً - "Then they would lean upon you with one decisive assault."

The word مَيْلَةً means a leaning or tilting. Imagine the gates of a city that have been broken and are barely standing. One side is trying to hold the gate up, the other is pushing to knock it over. If they see you weakening, they'll make one final crushing push to knock you down completely.

Then comes a concession: وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن كَانَ بِكُمْ أَذًى مِّن مَّطَرٍ أَوْ كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَن تَضَعُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَكُمْ - "And there is no harm upon you if there is discomfort upon you from rain, or you are sick, that you lay down your weapons."

This is Allah's ﷻ mercy. Someone might have gotten a serious infection from being out in the rain during battle, or they're so sick they can barely stand, yet when they hear this command from the Quran to hold their weapons, they're struggling to comply even though they can't physically manage it. Allah ﷻ says: No, you can relax. Put it down. You're excused. وَخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ - But still take your caution. Stay alert even if you can't physically hold your weapon.

The ayah concludes with a stern warning: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا - "Indeed, Allah has specially prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment." This isn't just any punishment - it's one designed to humiliate, to completely disgrace those who opposed Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

translation: And when you have completed the prayer, remember Allah standing, sitting, or [lying] on your sides. But when you become secure, re-establish [regular] prayer. Indeed, prayer has been decreed upon the believers a decree of specified times
commentary:
فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِكُمْ ۚ فَإِذَا ٱطْمَأْنَنتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَـٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا

This ayah provides guidance for what happens after the prayer in these battle conditions: فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ - "Then when you have completed the prayer..."

The instruction is remarkable: فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِكُمْ - "Then remember Allah standing, sitting and on your sides." Think about what armies typically do during their downtime. When soldiers are in battle and they get a break - nighttime comes, they go back to camp - what happens? Historically, this is time to get drunk, tell stories, engage in debauchery. "We might die tomorrow, so tonight is all we have left."

What does the believing army do? They remember Allah ﷻ - standing, sitting, lying on their sides. Picture the contrast between a Muslim army and a non-Muslim army in their free time. It's incredible. While others are losing themselves in temporary pleasures, the believers are engaged in constant remembrance of their Lord.

فَإِذَا ٱطْمَأْنَنتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ - "Then when you reach complete tranquility" - when the state of war is over, when there's no danger anymore, when you're in a state of peace - فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ "then establish the prayer." Meaning, perform it properly as it's supposed to be done, with all its requirements and at its proper times.

Then comes one of the most profound statements about prayer in the Quran: إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَـٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا - "Indeed, the prayer has always been prescribed upon the believers at specific fixed times."

It's amazing that this statement about كِتَـٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا (prescribed at appointed times) appears in an ayah discussing prayer during emergency situations. The message is clear: if you're establishing salah even in these dire circumstances, why wouldn't you be disciplined about it in times of peace? Why wouldn't you establish it at its exact, precise time?

This ayah teaches us something fundamental about our daily priorities. Every day, all of us have things we need to accomplish. As students, you have assignments due. As employees, you have projects to complete. As business owners, you have operations to run. As homemakers, you have households to manage. Everyone who's driven has a task list, goals they want to achieve. We discipline ourselves to get things done.

But a day is a failure if we didn't pray on time. Everything else we accomplish is secondary. The primary objective of the morning is Fajr. The primary objective of the afternoon is Dhuhr. The primary objective of late afternoon is Asr. Everything else in between is filler. The real objective is salah. Those milestones must be achieved because that's the bare minimum expected from us on a daily basis. If we don't accomplish that, we're failures that day. That day is a failure.

What used to characterize Muslim society was that all our schedules, all our commitments and agreements were wrapped around salah. "I'll see you after Maghrib." "I'll meet you before Asr." That's how our appointments were structured. Now our commitments are: we have to go to school, we have to go to work, and we try to force salah somewhere into that schedule.

Even when we do pray, what happens? A teenager is in the middle of an epic scene in a video game. The adhan goes off. "Ugh." He pauses the game, rushes through wudu, spreads the prayer mat and starts praying. But while he's in salah, his little brother walks near the PlayStation controller. Without breaking his prayer, his eyes are locked on that controller: "Don't touch that!" "Astaghfirullah..." His mind is somewhere else. He's worried about something else. Salah is just an obstacle in his day - get it over with and go back to what you really need to do.

Or consider a programmer typing code all day. Prayer time comes. He spreads his prayer mat right there in his cubicle. He's physically praying, but he's still mentally typing code. "Allahu Akbar" but his mind is going: function, slash, colon, semicolon... Business people can't let go of their deals and negotiations even during salah.

I'm not saying it's easy. If I'm immersed in studying something, reading an article, discussing a program we're planning to launch, and prayer time comes - "Oh no, the flyers! Oh no, the website update!" - it becomes: other stuff is priority and this is just something to get over with so we can get back to what we really need to be doing.

But Allah ﷻ is painting a different picture. Everything else is secondary. This is what your life is supposed to be about - connecting to Allah ﷻ. The word صَلَوٰة literally means connection. When that connection is not taken care of, any conversation about wanting to get closer to Allah ﷻ is artificial. It's fake.

There is no genuine path to closeness with Allah ﷻ if someone hasn't taken time to fix their prayer. You don't have a right to say "I want to get closer to Allah" if your salah isn't established, because literally, salah is that connection. Look at how Allah ﷻ articulates this expectation. What excuse do you have? You're not in a state of war anymore. Now you can establish it properly. This is the ultimate divine expectation from every believer.

translation: And do not weaken in pursuit of the enemy. If you should be suffering - so are they suffering as you are suffering, but you expect from Allah that which they expect not. And Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ فِى ٱبْتِغَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ۖ إِن تَكُونُوا۟ تَأْلَمُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ يَأْلَمُونَ كَمَا تَأْلَمُونَ ۖ وَتَرْجُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَرْجُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

Don't become weakened in the pursuit of the enemy, in your pursuit of that nation. Allah ﷻ is speaking to the Muslims who have been engaged in conflict with the coalition for a long time. This struggle had been ongoing for about fourteen years, and in conflicts that last this long, something happens to people—they lose the energy and fuel to keep going.

Allah ﷻ addresses this natural human weakness: "If you're in pain, if you're hurting because you've been going at it for so long, then know that they're hurting just like you are. They're hurting too." This is a remarkable statement. The enemy also needs perseverance. They give each other pep talks too. They struggle with the same exhaustion you do.

But here's the difference, the critical advantage: وَتَرْجُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَرْجُونَ—you have hope from Allah ﷻ that they don't have. You have expectations from Allah ﷻ that they'll never possess. This hope should be your fuel. You should never run out of steam because you have something they can never have.

What Allah ﷻ teaches us here is profound. Hope from Allah ﷻ is a great pain reliever. Expectations from Allah ﷻ toughen you up and make you capable of doing things that would otherwise be impossible. Without hope from Allah ﷻ, you'd simply say, "I'm done. I can't do anymore." But with that hope, you keep going, you keep pushing forward.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا—and certainly, Allah ﷻ has always been All-Knowing, All-Wise.

translation: Indeed, We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], the Book in truth so you may judge between the people by that which Allah has shown you. And do not be for the deceitful an advocate
commentary:
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِمَآ أَرَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَا تَكُن لِّلْخَآئِنِينَ خَصِيمًا

There's actually an interesting incident that connects to this ayah. The mixture in this surah between principles of war and social laws continues, going back and forth. Whenever we return to the ethics of war, the hypocrites are constantly being addressed. But this ayah relates to a particular incident.

There was a Muslim—let's just say he wasn't exactly a good Muslim—who stole a bag of flour from a Jewish neighbor. The bag had a hole in it, so when he ran from the Jewish man's house to his own, it left a trail that he didn't notice. When he got home, he had a feeling that maybe he'd get caught. Not yet aware of the trail, he went to another neighbor's house—another Jewish man—and said, "Can you hold onto this for me? Just for a couple of days." The neighbor said, "Sure, you're my friend, my neighbor. Why wouldn't I?"

The Muslims traced the trail to the thief's house and checked it—nothing. Then they traced another trail going to the other Jewish man's house. When they asked him, "Do you have this bag of flour?" he said, "Yes, but this Muslim gave it to me." They said, "What are you talking about?" The trail made it clear there was first one location, then another.

But then the entire tribe of the Muslim man came forward. "How can you go against a Muslim when there's a Jew involved? Are you going to believe them over us?" They started making a commotion around the masjid. "We know this is a good man," they argued about Tu'mah ibn Ubayruq and his tribe Banu Zafar. "He would never steal a bag of flour. Look at the other guy—he looks like the real crook." They went all out making their case, and a huge crowd gathered.

The hypocrites tried to emotionally manipulate the Messenger ﷺ. This is especially dangerous when dealing with a compassionate leader during a state of war, when maintaining the morale of your people is crucial. "What kind of leader are you?" they implied. "You can't even give us justice?" Emotional manipulation of leaders is particularly effective against those who are soft-hearted, and the Messenger ﷺ was extremely compassionate with the believers. These hypocrites tried to take advantage of that quality.

In this context, Allah ﷻ says: إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ—We have sent the Book to you with truth and purpose. لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ—so that you may make decisions between the people. Then comes a beautiful addition: بِمَآ أَرَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ—based on what Allah ﷻ has shown you.

Notice the word أَرَىٰكَ. There's the law, the explicit text of the Quran, but then there's also the insight, the wisdom, the perception of the judge. The Messenger ﷺ also possessed emotional intelligence and psychological intelligence. He could tell when people were trying to manipulate him or take advantage of the situation. Allah ﷻ had shown him this kind of insight. So in making decisions, he needed to use both the Book and the divine insight Allah ﷻ had granted him.

وَلَا تَكُن لِّلْخَآئِنِينَ خَصِيمًا—and don't you dare become someone who argues on behalf of these cheaters. The one man cheated and stole, but the entire tribe that came to his defense—all of them—Allah ﷻ labeled as cheaters because of this incident.

translation: And seek forgiveness of Allah. Indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَٱسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

وَٱسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ—and you should seek forgiveness from Allah ﷻ. This is fascinating. The Quran repeats this concept several times when it comes to passing judgment. Similar passages describe Dawud (AS), when he had to make judgment between the man who had ninety-nine sheep and the other who had only one. There too, istighfar is mentioned.

The meaning is clear: if you even had a thought of siding with them, even slightly, as a judge, then you should make istighfar. The judge must hold himself to an extremely high standard. A judge should constantly be thinking, "Is Allah ﷻ judging me right now?" That's how a judge should think. There's profound importance of taqwa in the Islamic judiciary.

Consider this example: a judge goes through a difficult divorce, and his wife takes everything. If he's a domestic affairs judge, a divorce court judge, what happens the next week when he has to make a judgment between a man and a woman? Who is he going to let his frustrations out on? If she's a judge who went through a divorce, who is she going to let her anger out on? The next person who walks in with a divorce case might be finished before they even start, because the judge is letting personal stress influence the judgment.

There's a human element to this, but Allah ﷻ constantly maintains these standards, even for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. He hadn't done anything wrong—he hadn't—but we're learning the principles, the standards a judge has to hold himself to when passing decisions.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا—but don't worry, Allah ﷻ has always been Extremely Forgiving, Always Merciful. Hope is offered at the same time as the warning.

translation: And do not argue on behalf of those who deceive themselves. Indeed, Allah loves not one who is a habitually sinful deceiver
commentary:
وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلْ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ خَوَّانًا أَثِيمًا

وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلْ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ—and don't you even debate, don't make a case on behalf of those who have cheated themselves. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ—Allah ﷻ does not love مَن كَانَ خَوَّانًا أَثِيمًا—anyone who has been a repeated offender, a repeated con artist, constantly engaged in sin.

The word أَثِيمًا is particularly significant here. People who con others—financial scams, stealing and then investing ill-gotten gains—they don't just commit one act. The scams they commit can literally ruin a person's life. People who cheat others in financial investments, people like Bernie Madoff, for example—every single day their victim suffers harm. Every single bit of harm the victim faces is a constant addition to the perpetrator's sin. So it's not just one act; it's the ongoing consequences of that act that are constantly adding to their record. That's why Allah ﷻ uses خَوَّانًا أَثِيمًا—a repeated cheater and constantly engaged in sin.

translation: They conceal [their evil intentions and deeds] from the people, but they cannot conceal [them] from Allah, and He is with them [in His knowledge] when they spend the night in such as He does not accept of speech. And ever is Allah, of what they do, encompassing
commentary:
يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ مَا لَا يَرْضَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطًا

يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ—they hide their faces, they hide away from people out of embarrassment. You could be a con artist operating as a corporate executive, and people might not know. But once you're exposed, once you're hauled away in handcuffs, that's humiliating. Before this, you wore a suit and tie, sat in an executive office, and people looked at you with respect. But when the authorities take you away, journalists try to get pictures and you're hiding your face.

Allah ﷻ says: وَلَا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ—but they don't try to hide from Allah ﷻ. They're not embarrassed before Him. وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ مَا لَا يَرْضَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ—and He is with them when they spend the entire night plotting, saying things He does not approve of. They're scheming and speaking words that Allah ﷻ is nowhere near approving.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطًا—and Allah ﷻ had always completely encircled everything they had been doing. All the plots they made were completely within a boundary that Allah ﷻ had set for them. They weren't getting away with anything.

translation: Here you are - those who argue on their behalf in [this] worldly life - but who will argue with Allah for them on the Day of Resurrection, or who will [then] be their representative
commentary:
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ جَـٰدَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَمَن يُجَـٰدِلُ ٱللَّهَ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَم مَّن يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا

هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ—here you are! Some of the Muslims got caught up in the emotional rhetoric and started arguing on behalf of the wrongdoers. جَـٰدَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا—all of you are making a case for them, for these people and their tribe, in this worldly life.

فَمَن يُجَـٰدِلُ ٱللَّهَ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ—but who's going to debate Allah ﷻ on their behalf on the Day of Judgment? You think you're debating with the Messenger ﷺ, but you haven't realized that means you'll be trying to debate this case with Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day too. أَم مَّن يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا—or who will come forward to take care of their affairs?

This ayah contains an extremely valuable and relevant contemporary lesson for us. Muslims do wrong things. And Muslims should be able to stand by our brothers when they're right, but we should also stand against our brothers when they're wrong. We can't just blindly stand by Muslims no matter what they do. If Muslims are doing something wrong, we have to say it's wrong. It's not right, and we don't have to add "but" every time.

"Well, Muslims are doing something wrong, but you know..." What does that mean? It means you're making it sound justifiable. If something is wrong in our deen, it's wrong. That's the end of it. What the enemy is doing, what somebody else is doing—that's a separate issue. These two things shouldn't be confused with each other. When you put them together, it's as though between the lines you're justifying what Muslims may be doing. That's not acceptable.

We have to be able to speak on behalf of justice, even when it means standing against Muslims who are in the wrong. We do this for our own sake, because we have to stand by justice—كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ. That's probably the central idea of this surah: standing up for justice, even if it means standing against your own interests.

translation: And whoever does a wrong or wrongs himself but then seeks forgiveness of Allah will find Allah Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ يَجِدِ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

وَمَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُۥ—whoever does any evil or wrongs himself. Notice the distinction Allah ﷻ makes between two kinds of wrongdoing: doing something evil (سُوٓءًا) and wronging yourself (يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُۥ).

Let's understand what scholars have said about these two categories. The word سُوٓء comes from a root that refers to something hideous, like an animal corpse lying on the side of the road—something nasty that you don't even want to make eye contact with. It's a symbol of ugliness. Some deeds are inherently ugly through and through. They have no allure, no attraction, no temptation to them. Backbiting is ugly in every way—there's nothing attractive about it. Lying in and of itself is an ugly thing. It doesn't have any appeal.

Then there are other sins that do have an allure to them. Stealing might have an appeal—you might get something beautiful out of it. Something valuable attracted your attention and you tried to steal it. Shamelessness has an allure to it. These are the sins where you wrong yourself in pursuit of some worldly benefit, some gain, to fulfill a temptation or lust.

The first category are deeds that don't even bring you pleasure—no material benefit, no physical pleasure—but you do them anyway. The second category are deeds you commit in pursuit of some kind of worldly gain.

ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ—then afterward, he seeks forgiveness from Allah ﷻ. يَجِدِ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا—he will find Allah ﷻ Extremely Forgiving, Always Merciful.

translation: And whoever commits a sin only earns it against himself. And Allah is ever Knowing and Wise
commentary:
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ إِثْمًا فَإِنَّمَا يَكْسِبُهُۥ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

وَمَن يَكْسِبْ إِثْمًا—whoever earns a sin. فَإِنَّمَا يَكْسِبُهُۥ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ—he has only earned that sin against his own self. He thinks he's earned something valuable. He thinks he's gained a bag of flour, but he's only earned something against his own soul.

This recalls an earlier ayah: إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا—they're only consuming into their bellies nothing but fire.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا—Allah ﷻ has always been All-Knowing, All-Wise.

This was a judicial matter where an entire tribe came and spoke on behalf of a wrongdoer. Allah ﷻ shifts it from being a case of "this group versus that group" and makes it a matter of individual, personal accountability. When a person loses their individual sense of accountability, they hide behind their tribe, their people, their community. They think their group will shield them from justice.

translation: But whoever earns an offense or a sin and then blames it on an innocent [person] has taken upon himself a slander and manifest sin
commentary:
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ خَطِيٓـَٔةً أَوْ إِثْمًا ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِۦ بَرِيٓـًٔا فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلَ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا

Allah ﷻ presents another scenario: وَمَن يَكْسِبْ خَطِيٓـَٔةً—whoever earns a mistake. خَطِيٓـَٔة is a significant error, but crucially, it's not done on purpose. أَوْ إِثْمًا—or a sin, which is committed intentionally.

ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِۦ بَرِيٓـًٔا—then he throws that sin onto an innocent person, accusing someone who had nothing to do with it. فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلَ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا—then he has carried upon himself a massive false accusation and an open, clear, obvious, undoubted sin.

In other words, on Judgment Day this will be brought against him as a serious charge: that he accused an innocent person, adding بُهْتَـٰنًا (slander) to his original إِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (clear sin).

translation: And if it was not for the favor of Allah upon you, [O Muhammad], and His mercy, a group of them would have determined to mislead you. But they do not mislead except themselves, and they will not harm you at all. And Allah has revealed to you the Book and wisdom and has taught you that which you did not know. And ever has the favor of Allah upon you been great
commentary:
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَهَمَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُضِلُّوكَ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُن تَعْلَمُ ۚ وَكَانَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا

وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ—had it not been for the special favor of Allah ﷻ upon you, the Messenger ﷺ, and His mercy on you. لَهَمَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُضِلُّوكَ—a group among them really wanted desperately to mislead you. They were going to do everything they could to persuade you to side with them.

In other words, the favor Allah ﷻ is referring to is the insight He gave the Messenger ﷺ to see through these manipulations and to make the judgment in the right way. That was Allah's favor upon him. Had he not possessed that kind of insight and character, these people would have swayed him. They would have made him make the wrong decision.

وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ—but the fact of the matter is, they're not misleading anyone except their own selves. وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِن شَىْءٍ—and they're not going to be able to harm you in any way, shape or form.

وَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ—and while it is the case that Allah ﷻ sent upon you the Book and the Wisdom. وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُن تَعْلَمُ—and He taught you what you couldn't possibly have known yourself.

Notice the grammatical structure: لَمْ تَكُن تَعْلَمُ. The past tense of كَانَ combined with the present tense of تَعْلَمُ creates a meaning of impossibility—there was absolutely no way for you to have known what Allah ﷻ taught you.

وَكَانَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا—and the favor of Allah ﷻ, the blessing of Allah ﷻ upon you is incredible. It's immense.

translation: No good is there in much of their private conversation, except for those who enjoin charity or that which is right or conciliation between people. And whoever does that seeking means to the approval of Allah - then We are going to give him a great reward
commentary:
لَّا خَيْرَ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّن نَّجْوَىٰهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلَـٰحٍۭ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

After addressing those who displayed hypocrisy by being unhappy with the Prophet's verdict ﷺ, Allah ﷻ now turns to the broader issue of secret meetings and private conversations. The word نَجْوَىٰ refers to secret councils and private discussions. The fact that Allah ﷻ mentions these secret meetings means they have been exposed—He knows what transpires behind closed doors.

Allah ﷻ declares: "There is no good in much of their private conversations." This is a powerful indictment of how we use our time together. Consider this common scenario: a meeting takes place about building two extra classrooms for a school. Everyone agrees during the meeting, but afterwards, a few people gather at a coffee shop and discuss how stupid the idea was and how irresponsible the leadership is. All these supposedly valid opinions and intelligent ideas were withheld when the time was right to voice them, but now they surface as najwa—unproductive, destructive gossip.

However, Allah ﷻ makes three critical exceptions: "Except for one who commands charity, or good, or reconciliation between people." These are the only private conversations that carry genuine value.

The first exception is commanding charity (صَدَقَة). Imagine the same meeting about building classrooms, but afterwards two people meet privately and decide to fund the entire project themselves before the fundraising even begins. This is najwa for a good cause.

The second exception is commanding good (مَعْرُوف). Perhaps a community is planning to take another Muslim group to court. After the meeting, someone suggests privately, "Why don't we just go talk to them? Let's take them out to dinner and try to resolve this." They work behind the scenes to prevent the lawsuit from proceeding. This is constructive private discussion.

The third exception is reconciliation between people (إِصْلَـٰحٍۭ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ). Sometimes making peace requires discrete, private conversations. When two people have a serious conflict and each has gathered their supporters, you cannot call everyone together for a meeting—that would likely turn into a brawl. Instead, the peacemaker must go to one friend's house and say, "You misunderstood what he said. That's not what he meant at all." Then visit the other person and explain, "He misunderstood you completely." You go back and forth, working privately to restore understanding and peace.

This is entirely different from someone approaching you to say, "Our masjid board is the source of all evil. Let me tell you about this one particular person..." Stop right there. That is not good najwa. That is drawing unrelated parties into conflict and spreading discord.

Allah ﷻ then emphasizes the proper intention: "And whoever does that seeking the pleasure of Allah, then We will grant him an enormous reward." The key phrase is ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ —doing this solely to make Allah ﷻ happy. When Muslims are fighting, you want to restore the remembrance of Allah ﷻ between them. You want love to exist between believers because that pleases Him.

The enormous reward (أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا) is well-deserved because making peace between Muslims requires extraordinary patience and thick skin. Have you seen the kind of things Muslims say about each other? Those who try to reconcile fighting parties take hits from all sides. Both parties may question your intentions and say harsh things about you. Family feuds especially test the resolve of peacemakers. Many people simply avoid the issue: "Every time I bring up Uncle Joshua, all hell breaks loose in the house, so I'm not even going to mention it." But the peacemaker persists, taking the verbal abuse, enduring the accusations, all because they are not trying to prove anything to anyone—they simply want Allah ﷻ to be pleased with them.

translation: And whoever opposes the Messenger after guidance has become clear to him and follows other than the way of the believers - We will give him what he has taken and drive him into Hell, and evil it is as a destination
commentary:
وَمَن يُشَاقِقِ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِۦ مَا تَوَلَّىٰ وَنُصْلِهِۦ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا

This ayah continues the theme of opposing the Messenger ﷺ and introduces the critical concept of following the path of the believers. Several incidents in Surah An-Nisa have addressed those who were unhappy with the Prophet's verdicts ﷺ, and this subject continues here with even graver consequences.

"Whoever opposes the Messenger after guidance has become clear to him, and follows other than the way of the believers..." The phrase يُشَاقِقِ ٱلرَّسُولَ means to oppose or create a rift with the Messenger ﷺ. This opposition occurs مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ (after what has become clear to him of guidance)—meaning the person understood the truth but chose to reject it anyway.

The second part of the ayah introduces an essential principle: وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and follows other than the path of the believers). Imam Ash-Shafi'i (may Allah have mercy on him) famously stated that he read the Quran 300 times looking for proof that ijma' (consensus of the Muslim community) is a valid source of Islamic legislation. He could not find clear evidence until the 300th reading, when he stopped at this ayah and the phrase "the path of the believers." This demonstrates that when the believing community has consensus on a matter, that itself represents the right way.

However, we must be careful with the concept of ijma'. It has become a buzzword that can be manipulated: "There's ijma' that those guys are deviant." Really? You're going to throw the word ijma' around like a tennis ball? This is serious. Even saying there is ijma' among the Sahaba (RA) on something requires extensive research. There can be twenty different opinions on an issue, but ijma' emerges when, for example, a caliph like Umar (RA) passes a verdict and even those with dissenting opinions agree to follow it because he is the Amir of the believers. There is discussion, there may be difference of opinion, but at the end, there is consensus to follow a particular verdict. That becomes organized ijma'.

In our time, claiming there is ijma' on something—especially on difficult, nuanced issues—is very challenging. This makes the principle important but also one that requires scholarly precision and cannot be casually invoked.

The consequence for opposing the Messenger ﷺ and the believers is severe: نُوَلِّهِۦ مَا تَوَلَّىٰ (We will turn him away in the direction he wanted to turn). Allah ﷻ will let him follow the path he chose. وَنُصْلِهِۦ جَهَنَّمَ (And We will cast him into Hell). وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (And what a horrible final destination).

translation: Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills. And he who associates others with Allah has certainly gone far astray
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا

The thematic progression in the Quran is remarkable. The previous ayah addressed opposing the Messenger ﷺ, and now Allah ﷻ speaks about shirk (associating partners with Him). These connections are rhetorically significant. Why does shirk come up after discussing opposition to the Messenger ﷺ?

Because refusing to accept the Messenger ﷺ as the final authority is, in essence, refusing to accept Allah ﷻ as the final authority. The Messenger ﷺ is the vessel through which Allah's authority is delivered to us. When people during the Prophet's lifetime ﷺ would look at him and say, "I'm not sure about his ability to pass verdicts. I'm not convinced he made the best decision," they were effectively suggesting that he makes things up on his own, that he is not guided by revelation. This is why some scholars defined hypocrisy (nifaq) as a hidden form of shirk—it is shirk of intentions, harboring secret opposition to Allah's guidance while appearing to accept it outwardly.

Allah ﷻ states unequivocally: "Indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners be associated with Him." The phrase لَا يَغْفِرُ is emphatic—He absolutely does not forgive shirk. This is the one unforgivable sin if a person dies upon it.

However, Allah ﷻ immediately provides hope: وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ (But He forgives whatever is besides that for whoever He wills). This is not an open license to commit other sins, but it establishes that there is hope. Other sins, no matter how grave, carry the possibility of divine forgiveness if Allah ﷻ wills it. This should fill the believer's heart with both fear of shirk and hope in Allah's mercy for other shortcomings.

The ayah concludes: وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (And whoever associates partners with Allah has certainly gone far astray). The word ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا indicates being far, far away from the truth—not just off the path, but wandering in complete darkness at an immense distance from guidance.

translation: They call upon instead of Him none but female [deities], and they [actually] call upon none but a rebellious Satan
commentary:
إِن يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ إِنَـٰثًا وَإِن يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطَـٰنًا مَّرِيدًا

Now that shirk has been mentioned in principle, Allah ﷻ exposes the reality of the idolatry practiced by the polytheists of Makkah. This is how Quranic discourse works—concepts trigger related ideas that naturally follow one another. Hidden shirk (hypocrisy) was addressed, so now open shirk is examined.

"They do not call upon other than Him except female deities." The word إِنَـٰثًا means females or feminine deities. The pagan Arabs worshipped goddesses like Al-Lat, Al-'Uzza and Manat. The name Al-Lat is particularly telling—it is the feminine form of Allah. Just as 'Aziz (the Mighty) has the feminine form 'Uzza, these idol names were feminine derivations of Allah's names. The polytheists took the names and attributes of Allah ﷻ and created female versions, fashioning deities they could control and manipulate according to their desires.

But Allah ﷻ reveals the reality: وَإِن يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطَـٰنًا مَّرِيدًا (They are not calling upon anyone except an extremely rebellious devil). The word مَّرِيدًا describes a devil who is exceptionally rebellious—one who goes beyond the normal boundaries of rebellion. Imagine a gang of criminals, and one of them says, "Let me show you something nobody else has ever done," and goes completely beyond what even the other criminals would do. That is marida—someone who rebels beyond all limits. When people worship these idols, they think they are calling upon deities, but in reality, they are submitting to Shaytan.

translation: Whom Allah has cursed. For he had said, "I will surely take from among Your servants a specific portion
commentary:
لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۘ وَقَالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبَادِكَ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا

Allah ﷻ now quotes the very words of Shaytan, giving us insight into his strategy and his vow. لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ (Allah has cursed him). The word لَعَنَ can mean both "may Allah curse him" and "Allah has already cursed him"—both meanings apply to Iblis.

Then we hear Shaytan's declaration: وَقَالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبَادِكَ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا (And he said, "I will surely take from Your servants a specific portion"). The word نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا means a set, mandated portion—a guaranteed percentage. Shaytan is saying, "I'm going to take a bunch of them with me."
