Stick with the Muslims for their growing power, keep ties with the disbelievers for their established authority, and ensure you always have نَصِير - someone to aid you.

But the one Helper they should have sought was Allah ﷻ. Because they failed to make Him their protector and instead sought protection in created beings, on the Day of Judgment they will find themselves in the worst possible situation with no helpers whatsoever. The very thing they spent their lives pursuing - security through alliances - will be completely absent when they need it most.

### A Door Still Open

Despite this severe warning, Allah ﷻ immediately offers an exception in the following ayah: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا - "Except those who repent." Even for those heading down the path of hypocrisy, the door of repentance remains open. Those who are rattled by these warnings, who recognize there's still some good left in them, who feel shaken by the prospect of ending up in the lowest pit - for them, return is possible.

### The Stages of Hypocrisy

Hypocrisy doesn't happen overnight. It progresses through stages, and understanding these stages helps us recognize the warning signs in ourselves. The Quran, particularly in Surah Al-Munafiqun and Surah At-Tawbah, provides a complete psychological analysis of how hypocrisy develops.

It begins with weakness of faith. At this early stage, you might still feel remorse. A tear comes to your eye when you hear a reminder. You think, "I really should change. I messed up." That's not yet hypocrisy - that's weakness struggling with itself.

But then the tear dries up. You hear the strongest reminder and nothing moves inside you. Nothing budges. It becomes artificial, a superficial exercise. The concern about being hypocritical itself disappears. You've heard it all before, and it no longer affects you.

When you stop worrying about your own spiritual state, you begin worrying about others instead. "That guy over there, I don't know about him." When you find yourself constantly commenting on others' faith and practice, that's already an indication you've stopped examining yourself seriously. And when you stop examining yourself, it's a sign the heart has begun to harden.

Then paranoia sets in. You think everyone is out to criticize you specifically. Someone gives a lecture about hypocrisy and you're convinced they're staring directly at you, that every word is aimed at you personally. This isn't the healthy response of applying a general reminder to yourself; it's a sickness that sees persecution where none exists.

The next phase brings hatred for those you imagine are judging you. "I don't want to be around them. I hate those people. They're idiots, fools, extremists." And then comes the final inversion: you reinterpret the religion itself to make yourself appear as its champion while casting those who remind you as the real problem. "You're the backwards literalists. I'm the one who truly understands. I'm trying to save Islam from people like you."

When people are told "Don't make corruption in the earth," what do they say? لَا إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ - "We are only reformers." They turn the tables completely. The best defense becomes offense.

### Staying Vigilant Against Hypocrisy

These are stages we must watch for in ourselves. Hypocrisy is perhaps the psychological and spiritual problem addressed most extensively in the Quran. Two major chapters - Surah An-Nisa (which we're studying now) and Surah At-Tawbah - deal extensively with it, and an entire surah, Surah Al-Munafiqun, is dedicated to diagnosing this condition.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from falling into hypocrisy. May He keep our hearts sincere, our faith strong, and our commitment to Him and His Messenger ﷺ unwavering. And may He help us recognize the signs of spiritual disease in ourselves before they progress to stages from which return becomes increasingly difficult.

translation: Except for those who repent, correct themselves, hold fast to Allah, and are sincere in their religion for Allah, for those will be with the believers. And Allah is going to give the believers a great reward
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

"Except for those who repent, correct themselves, hold firmly to Allah, and devote their religion to Allah alone. Those will be with the believers, and Allah will grant the believers a great reward."

This ayah presents a powerful path of return for those who have strayed. Allah ﷻ outlines four essential conditions for genuine repentance from hypocrisy. First comes تَابُوا۟ - they turn back to Allah ﷻ in sincere repentance. Second is أَصْلَحُوا۟ - they reconcile and correct their behavior, making tangible changes in how they conduct themselves.

The third condition reveals something profound: وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ - they hold firmly to Allah ﷻ. The previous ayahs described the hypocrites as barely remembering Allah ﷻ, so this represents a complete transformation. They now cling to Allah ﷻ with determination. But what does it mean to truly hold onto Allah ﷻ?

The Quran itself provides the answer. Earlier in Surah Al-Imran, Allah ﷻ describes His Book as الْحَبْلِ - the rope we should grasp. To hold onto Allah ﷻ is to hold onto the Quran. This requires developing a personal, private relationship with Allah's words - not just studying them academically, but sitting alone with the Quran and asking, "What is Allah ﷻ saying to me? What is He guiding me toward in this ayah?"

When we establish this kind of relationship with the Quran, something remarkable happens. The Quran becomes a mirror. We begin to see ourselves reflected in its ayahs, and often what we see isn't flattering. The Quran forces us to confront our shortcomings with brutal honesty.

Consider Surah Al-Mu'minun, which opens with a description of successful believers: those who have khushu' in their prayers, who avoid useless talk, who constantly work to purify their character, who guard their chastity, who fulfill their trusts and promises, who maintain their prayers. When you make an honest checklist comparing yourself to these qualities, the exercise can be humbling. You might find yourself unable to check off a single box with confidence.

This isn't meant to discourage us - it's meant to awaken us. This self-awareness doesn't come primarily from lectures or teachers. It comes from private engagement with the Quran, from time spent alone with Allah's words when nobody else is watching.

The fourth condition is وَأَخْلَصُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ - they make their religion purely and sincerely for Allah ﷻ. Through holding onto the Quran, they come to understand what it truly means to have a relationship with Allah ﷻ for His sake alone.

Allah ﷻ has given us structured opportunities for this sincerity. In Salah, even when standing in congregation with thousands of people, we're meant to feel as though we're alone with our Creator. The additional voluntary prayers we perform at home reinforce this individual connection. The night prayer (Qiyam al-Layl) offers perhaps the purest opportunity - the household is asleep, the world is quiet, no distractions exist. Everything is still, giving your heart the chance to come alive in the presence of Allah ﷻ.

The reward for meeting these conditions is profound: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - "those will be with the believers." They rejoin the community of faith, fully embraced.

Then comes a beautiful statement: وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - "and Allah will grant the believers a great reward." The word أَجْرًا specifically denotes compensation for labor, wages for work performed. Allah ﷻ doesn't mention particular good deeds here - He describes the very effort of becoming sincere as labor deserving of reward. The struggle to purify our intentions and hold firmly to Allah ﷻ is itself valuable work in His sight.

translation: What would Allah do with your punishment if you are grateful and believe? And ever is Allah Appreciative and Knowing
commentary:
مَّا يَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابِكُمْ إِن شَكَرْتُمْ وَءَامَنتُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ شَاكِرًا عَلِيمًا

"What would Allah do with your punishment if you are grateful and believe? And Allah is ever Appreciative and Knowing."

After warning about the severe punishment awaiting the hypocrites, Allah ﷻ pivots with stunning gentleness. The tone shifts dramatically: مَّا يَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابِكُمْ - "What would Allah do with your punishment?" 

This rhetorical question conveys profound mercy. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying, "Why would I want to punish you? What benefit would I gain from it? I have no desire to punish you - I get nothing out of it." This is Allah ﷻ expressing that punishment isn't His goal or preference. He's not eager to inflict pain upon His creation.

The condition for avoiding punishment is beautifully simple: إِن شَكَرْتُمْ وَءَامَنتُمْ - "if you are grateful and believe." Just two things: gratitude and faith. That's all Allah ﷻ asks.

Then comes a remarkable statement: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ شَاكِرًا عَلِيمًا - "And Allah is ever Appreciative and Knowing."

The word شَاكِرًا is extraordinary here. In Arabic, there's a distinction between شَاكِر - one who is grateful - and مَشْكُور - one who is thanked or appreciated. Allah ﷻ could have called Himself مَشْكُور, the One deserving of gratitude. Instead, He describes Himself as شَاكِر - the One who shows gratitude.

This is Allah ﷻ elevating the dignity of the believer in the most humbling way. He's saying, "When you show gratitude to Me, I will be grateful to you. I will appreciate your appreciation."

Imagine a teacher pleading with students: "If you would just come to class on time, you'd be doing me such a favor. I would feel so honored." This would be embarrassing for the teacher - it inverts the natural order. Or a mother saying to her child, "If you would just greet me with Salaam when you come home, I would be so honored." The child should feel ashamed that the parent is reduced to making such a plea.

This is the tone Allah ﷻ adopts with us. After all the warnings about punishment, He essentially says, "If you could just be a little grateful and have some faith, I would really appreciate it." How low have we fallen that our Creator speaks to us this way?

Yet this is also a promise: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ شَاكِرًا عَلِيمًا - Allah ﷻ is Appreciative and All-Knowing. He won't forget even the smallest good we do. He knows every tiny act of gratitude, every moment of faith, and He appreciates it all.

translation: Allah does not like the public mention of evil except by one who has been wronged. And ever is Allah Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
۞ لَّا يُحِبُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْجَهْرَ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَن ظُلِمَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًا عَلِيمًا

"Allah does not like the public mention of evil except by one who has been wronged. And Allah is ever Hearing and Knowing."

The discourse now shifts to a universal social principle that appears repeatedly throughout this surah. Allah ﷻ states: لَّا يُحِبُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْجَهْرَ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ - "Allah does not like the public mention of evil."

This addresses the problem of broadcasting evil deeds, of making scandals public, of announcing wrongdoing openly. In our communities, this often happens under the guise of "raising awareness" - parents need to know what the youth are doing, the community needs to know what's happening, we must confront these problems openly.

Sometimes speakers will use graphic descriptions or shocking revelations for what amounts to entertainment value, as though they're giving people "the real talk" about what's happening. But Allah ﷻ says He doesn't like this. He doesn't want nasty behavior, dirty deeds, and evil actions becoming common discourse.

Why? Because when something becomes easy to talk about, it becomes normalized. The more you discuss something, the more you think about it. The more you think about it, the more it influences you. Conversation has psychological effects. Public discourse shapes perception and attitude.

The exception is إِلَّا مَن ظُلِمَ - "except by one who has been wronged." The victim of injustice has the right to cry out, to seek justice publicly, to go to court or even the media to expose the wrong done to them. Allah ﷻ permits this because otherwise the wronged person has no recourse.

But for everyone else, especially regarding widespread moral failings in society, the solution isn't public scandal-mongering. Consider the Quranic approach to even serious sins like fornication. How many witnesses are required to establish the crime? أَرْبَعَة - four witnesses who actually saw the act. How realistic is that standard? It's deliberately high.

Moreover, you cannot simply go around testifying against people. If you make accusations without meeting this standard, you yourself become punishable for قَذْف - false accusation. The only context where testimony is permitted is under oath in a formal trial. These regulations exist precisely to prevent sins from becoming widespread public knowledge and casual conversation.

The proper Islamic approach is private counsel, bringing people in discreetly, discussing issues individually without exposure. This was the methodology of the Prophet ﷺ and the righteous scholars.

Remember, this is actually a characteristic of the hypocrites: whenever they hear news of fear or safety, they broadcast it immediately. The Quran criticizes them for not taking information to those qualified to analyze it properly before spreading it around.

The ayah closes with وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًا عَلِيمًا - "And Allah is ever Hearing and Knowing." He hears what's said publicly and privately, and He knows the full context and truth of every situation.

translation: If [instead] you show [some] good or conceal it or pardon an offense - indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and Competent
commentary:
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ خَيْرًا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا۟ عَن سُوٓءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيرًا

"Whether you reveal a good deed or conceal it or pardon an offense - indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and Competent."

This ayah presents the opposite side of the previous principle. While Allah ﷻ dislikes the public announcement of evil, He's perfectly fine with good being either public or private: إِن تُبْدُوا۟ خَيْرًا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ - "Whether you reveal a good deed or conceal it."

Good deeds can be performed openly or in secret - both are acceptable. There's no restriction on spreading news of positive actions and righteous behavior.

Then comes a powerful addition: أَوْ تَعْفُوا۟ عَن سُوٓءٍ - "or pardon an offense." Remember, the previous ayah gave the wronged person permission to speak out publicly. But here Allah ﷻ presents an even better option: forgiveness. Even the victim who has every right to expose the wrong done to them - if that person chooses to pardon instead, it's praiseworthy.

Why is this so important? Because making someone's mistake public can destroy their life entirely. Many professions, especially within the Muslim community, depend entirely on reputation - trust, respect, dignity. Start a scandal about an imam, and he'll never find work again. Expose a teacher's misconduct, and their career is finished.

Sometimes people do make mistakes. Sometimes they genuinely learn from those mistakes and change. But if you make their error public, you eliminate any possibility of rehabilitation. You've sentenced them to permanent social death.

The ayah concludes: فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيرًا - "indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and Competent."

Two attributes paired together: عَفُوًّا - Pardoning, and قَدِيرًا - Competent, Capable, Powerful. Why this combination? Allah ﷻ is powerful enough to punish every wrong, yet He chooses to pardon. He's also powerful enough to wipe away the evil effects from a community, to reconcile difficult situations, to bring good out of what seemed irredeemable.

When we pardon others, Allah ﷻ will pardon us. When we give people the chance to reform privately without destroying their reputation, Allah ﷻ can work to transform that community in ways we couldn't imagine.

## Ayahs 150-151

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يُفَرِّقُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ حَقًّا ۚ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا

"Indeed, those who disbelieve in Allah and His messengers and wish to discriminate between Allah and His messengers and say, 'We believe in some and disbelieve in others,' and wish to adopt a way in between - Those are the disbelievers, truly. And We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment."

After focusing on social principles, the discourse returns to a central theme that has woven through the entire surah: the relationship between Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, and by extension, the role of all prophets.

Throughout Surah An-Nisa, we've repeatedly encountered the problem of people not complying with the Messenger ﷺ, questioning his instructions, distinguishing between what Allah ﷻ says and what the Prophet ﷺ teaches. This persistent theme now reaches a crucial conclusion.

Allah ﷻ identifies a specific category of disbelief: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ - "Indeed, those who disbelieve in Allah and His messengers."

But He doesn't stop there. He exposes their methodology: وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يُفَ - "and wish to discriminate between Allah and His messengers."

This describes those who claim to accept Allah ﷻ but reject His messengers, or who accept most prophets but reject others, or who say they follow revelation but not the messenger who delivered it. They're willing to acknowledge God's existence but question why they should follow the Prophet ﷺ. "We'll follow the Quran, but why should we follow him? Show us where the Quran says to do this. Why should we accept the Sunnah?"

They articulate it explicitly: وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ - "and say, 'We believe in some and disbelieve in others.'" This part is acceptable to us, but that part isn't. We need a reformed, enlightened, revised understanding of religion that eliminates certain "backwards" elements.

This discourse sounds remarkably contemporary. The call for "progressive" or "reformed" Islam that reinterprets or eliminates uncomfortable teachings, that distinguishes sharply between Quran and Hadith, that questions the character or reliability of the Prophet ﷺ or earlier prophets - these aren't new ideas. They echo the attitude of Bani Israel, who continuously challenged and disrespected their prophets.

The modern version might begin subtly: "Let's focus on universal values all religions share - mercy, justice, compassion. Let's not emphasize what divides us, like specific prophetic teachings." Then it evolves: "Actually, these prophets may have manipulated people. Religion is man-made." Eventually: "Hadith is unreliable. The Prophet ﷺ had personal preferences that aren't binding. The Quran is what matters, and it's open to interpretation."

Allah ﷻ identifies their goal: وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا - "and wish to adopt a way in between."

They want a middle path that doesn't require full submission. Once you separate the Prophet ﷺ from the Quran, you can interpret the Book however you wish. The Quran, like any text, becomes subject to endless reinterpretation without the grounding context of the Seerah and the prophetic implementation. You can "find a way" to avoid submitting to anything you find uncomfortable.

The verdict is unequivocal: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ حَقًّا - "Those are the disbelievers, truly."

The word حَقًّا - "truly" - emphasizes that these are genuine disbelievers, not borderline cases. They are betrayers of revelation itself.

An important clarification: this doesn't mean everyone who holds different interpretations or asks difficult questions is a disbeliever. People can genuinely be convinced of ideas through sincere study and struggle. We live in an age of unprecedented access to diverse viewpoints and interpretations. Genuine disagreement in understanding doesn't equal kufr.

The issue is intention. When someone's goal is to separate Allah ﷻ from His Messenger ﷺ specifically to avoid submission, to create intellectual justification for living however they want while still claiming religious legitimacy - that's when this ayah applies.

The punishment matches the crime: وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا - "And We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment."

The word مُّهِينًا means humiliating, degrading. Those who tried to degrade revelation, who attempted to pick and choose from Allah's guidance according to their preferences, who sought to humiliate the status of prophethood - they will face humiliation in return.

These ayahs serve as a powerful warning about maintaining the inseparable link between Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, between revelation and its divinely appointed teacher. To accept one without the other is to reject both.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve in Allah and His messengers and wish to discriminate between Allah and His messengers and say, "We believe in some and disbelieve in others," and wish to adopt a way in between
Those are the disbelievers, truly. And We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment
But they who believe in Allah and His messengers and do not discriminate between any of them - to those He is going to give their rewards. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُفَرِّقُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَوْفَ يُؤْتِيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

In stark contrast to those who pick and choose among Allah's messengers, this ayah describes the believers who maintain proper faith. والذين آمنوا بالله ورسله - "And those who believed in Allah and His messengers." They accepted all of them without distinction or discrimination.

ولم يفرقوا بين أحد منهم - "and they didn't distinguish between anyone from among them." This means they took all of the messengers as truth. They never separated Allah ﷻ and His messengers, never created a hierarchy where some prophets were accepted and others rejected based on their own desires or tribal affiliations.

أولئك سوف يؤتيهم أجورهم - "Those soon He will give them their compensations." The reward is coming, it's guaranteed. The word سوف indicates something in the near future, giving reassurance to the believers.

وكان الله غفورًا رحيمًا - "And certainly Allah has always been Forgiving, constantly Merciful." This ending is particularly meaningful. Even those people who have the correct foundational beliefs are still going to make mistakes. They're still going to be looking for forgiveness. Just because they didn't make this ideological, belief-based mistake of rejecting some messengers, that doesn't mean they don't make other mistakes. But because they have the right faith, they're qualified for Allah's forgiveness and mercy.

translation: The People of the Scripture ask you to bring down to them a book from the heaven. But they had asked of Moses [even] greater than that and said, "Show us Allah outright," so the thunderbolt struck them for their wrongdoing. Then they took the calf [for worship] after clear evidences had come to them, and We pardoned that. And We gave Moses a clear authority
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُكَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا۟ مُوسَىٰٓ أَكْبَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَرِنَا ٱللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ فَعَفَوْنَا عَن ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا

After discussing hypocrites and disbelievers, Allah ﷻ now addresses the People of the Book. In all three groups, there's a common thread: a refusal to be convinced of the validity of the messenger.

يسألك أهل الكتاب أن تنزل عليهم كتابًا من السماء - "The People of the Book ask you to send down upon them a book from the sky." They were skeptical about whether the Prophet ﷺ was really a messenger. If he really was, why couldn't he just send a book directly from the sky for them to see?

Notice the parallel: the hypocrites aren't so sure that what the Prophet ﷺ is saying is from Allah ﷻ. That sounds remarkably like the People of the Book who are asking, "Why don't you send a book directly from the sky for us?"

فقد سألوا موسى أكبر من ذلك - "But they had already asked Musa something much bigger than that." You think it's outrageous that they're asking you this way? What they asked Musa (AS) was far more extreme.

فقالوا أرنا الله جهرة (faqalu arina Allaha jahratan) - "They said, 'Show us Allah face to face.' Expose Allah to us directly." This was their demand to Musa (AS).

فأخذتهم الصاعقة بظلمهم - "Then a loud explosion took them on account of their wrongdoing." The consequence was immediate and severe. They were trying to be obnoxious with Allah ﷻ through His messenger Musa (AS).

ثم اتخذوا العجل - "Then, even after that, they took the calf for worship." Notice the word ثم - "then, even after that." After experiencing that divine punishment, after the explosion came because of their wrongdoing, what did they do? They worshipped the calf.

من بعد ما جاءتهم البينات - "even though the clearest proofs had already come to them." They had witnessed miracles, they had clear evidence, yet they still fell into this grave sin.

فعفونا عن ذلك - "Then We pardoned from all of that." Despite these serious transgressions, Allah's mercy prevailed. The doors of pardon were opened.

The message to the current People of the Book is clear: I've pardoned before. Yes, your ancestors messed up seriously, and what they did was grave. But people have made serious mistakes before, and I still forgave. So you need to get back in line and be loyal to the messenger.

وآتينا موسى سلطانًا مبينًا - "And We gave Musa an open, clear authority." By describing the authority of Musa (AS), Allah ﷻ is also, by proxy, describing the authority of Muhammad ﷺ. Look, We pardoned them, and We had given Musa authority, just as We have given Muhammad ﷺ the authority.

translation: And We raised over them the mount for [refusal of] their covenant; and We said to them, "Enter the gate bowing humbly", and We said to them, "Do not transgress on the sabbath", and We took from them a solemn covenant
commentary:
وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَهُمُ ٱلطُّورَ بِمِيثَـٰقِهِمْ وَقُلْنَا لَهُمُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا وَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ لَا تَعْدُوا۟ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا

ورفعنا فوقهم الطور بميثاقهم - "And We elevated the mountain of Tur above them on account of the contract We were going to take with them." This refers to the ميثاق - a covenant or agreement that both sides understand is extremely serious.

They really understood how serious this contract was because the mountain was hovering above their heads as they were being told to take this agreement seriously. I can't think of another situation where you would take something more seriously. As described in Surah Al-A'raf, the mountain was above them like a cloud, and they were convinced it was going to fall on them. In that moment of absolute certainty, they said, "Okay, we know we messed up before. We're not doing that again."

وقلنا لهم ادخلوا الباب سجدًا - "And then they were told, 'Enter the doors of the city in the state of prostration.'" This was the command as they entered Jericho. When they didn't make prostration as commanded, that information is already been described elsewhere in the Quran.

وقلنا لهم لا تعدوا في السبت - "And We said to them, 'Don't violate the Sabbath.'" The word تعدوا means don't cross the line, don't go beyond the fence when it comes to the Sabbath regulations.

وأخذنا منهم ميثاقًا غليظًا - "And We took from them a very serious, harsh, tough agreement." The word غليظ emphasizes how weighty this covenant was. This was not another agreement to be taken lightly.

translation: And [We cursed them] for their breaking of the covenant and their disbelief in the signs of Allah and their killing of the prophets without right and their saying, "Our hearts are wrapped". Rather, Allah has sealed them because of their disbelief, so they believe not, except for a few
commentary:
فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَكُفْرِهِم بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَتْلِهِمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَقَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ ۚ بَلْ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهَا بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

To understand this ayah properly, you need to grasp an important grammatical concept. When you have multiple جار ومجرور (prepositional phrases) stacked together, you're building up to something. The خبر (the predicate, the main point) is coming. It's like someone saying, "Because you didn't show up, because you missed three exams, because you refused to do your homework, because you were playing video games in class..." What are you expecting after all these "because" statements? You're waiting for the consequence. What's going to happen now?

Let me give you another example. Imagine two boxers trash-talking before a match: "I'm gonna mess up your face. Your mom won't recognize you when I'm done with you." Then the boxing match starts, and as the guy is landing punches, he's saying, "This is for that first comment. Because you said this, because you said that, because you said the other." Now, what's the main point? "Because you said this..." What? The punch itself. He doesn't have to spell out "because you did this, I'm busting up your face." The act itself becomes the subject of the sentence.

Similarly here, scholars have suggested the khabar could be أخذناهم (akhadhnakhum) - "We seized them" - or عذبناهم - "We punished them," because they deserve punishment. But there's another profound interpretation: the khabar is left unstated. The punishment itself becomes implicit. It's like Allah ﷻ is saying, "Because of all this..." and leaving it there. Just feel the weight of the consequences.

Imagine a teacher who says, "Because you've done this, and you've done this, and you've done this, and you've done this, as a result of all of these things..." and then stops and walks away. Would that leave you terrified? Why? What's he going to do? Did he tell you what he's going to do? No. Does he usually tell you what the punishments are? Yes. But if he doesn't even tell you what the punishment is, doesn't even spell out the consequences, they must be beyond words. They must be serious.

That's what's happening in this ayah. It's a means of instilling profound awareness of the gravity of these sins.

The structure itself is powerful. In Arabic, the جار (preposition) is ب ("because of"), and normally you'd have بنقضهم. But Allah ﷻ adds ما for emphasis: فبما نقضهم. The only function of ma here is to add weight and emphasis to the statement.

Let me translate without the emphasis particle first: بنقضهم ميثاقهم - "because of their violating the contract." But look at what Allah ﷻ actually says: فبما نقضهم ميثاقهم - "Because of what amazing violations they made to the contract, how horribly they violated the contract they owed." The word نقض alone wasn't going to do justice. The particle ما had to be added to emphasize the enormity of this betrayal.

وكفرهم بآيات الله - "And on account of what amazing disbelief and disregard they had for the miraculous signs of Allah."

وقتلهم الأنبياء بغير حق - "And what amazing and vicious murdering of the prophets they engaged in, without any justification whatsoever."

وقولهم قلوبنا غلف - "And because of how obnoxiously they were willing to say, 'Our hearts are enveloped, they're protected. You can't get to us. Our hearts are safe.'"

Now notice the transition. They're speaking about their hearts being protected, claiming immunity. Allah ﷻ responds to this claim directly:

بل طبع الله عليها بكفرهم - "Rather, Allah had placed a seal upon their hearts because of their disbelief." Their hearts weren't protected at all. In fact, they were sealed off from guidance as a consequence of their own rejection.

فلا يؤمنون إلا قليلاً - "Then they're not going to be believing except very, very little."

This was a momentary transition focused on addressing their specific claim about their hearts. But the main list of transgressions continues into the next ayah.

translation: And [We cursed them] for their disbelief and their saying against Mary a great slander
commentary:
وَبِكُفْرِهِمْ وَقَوْلِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ بُهْتَـٰنًا عَظِيمًا

The list continues: وبكفرهم - "And because of their disbelief" - the preposition ب returns, picking up the grammatical structure from ayah 155.

وقولهم على مريم بهتانًا عظيمًا - "And their statements made against Maryam, a tremendous false allegation."

What statements against Maryam (AS)? The accusation against her chastity, claiming that the child was born out of wedlock. This is called بهتان - a serious false allegation that can devastate someone's reputation and honor.

You can accuse someone of small things - you took my pencil, you were late. But when you accuse someone of murder or adultery, that's بهتان. It's a massive false charge that destroys lives. They made this enormous buhtan against Maryam (AS).

translation: And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain
commentary:
وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَـٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا

Before examining this ayah, let me explain the word المسيح, which has deep linguistic roots.

The root م-س-ح has several related meanings. مسح means to take a liquid and pour it over something to remove dirt - to wipe over something for purification. In English, this became "the Anointed One." To anoint means to take oil or water for ritualistic purposes, to physically and spiritually purify. That's one meaning of Masih.

مسح also means to erase - to erase the traces of dirt and filth from your body. In prayer, we say: اللهم امسح عني ذنوبي - "O Allah, erase from me my sins, my difficulties, my challenges, my sickness."

In classical Arabic, مسح also refers to someone characterized by something. المسيح الكريم - the one characterized by nobility. The word مسيح can imply someone who has very salient, distinct, outstanding qualities. The فعيل pattern can carry the meaning of one who has been given honor.

Interestingly, مسح also means to slaughter or slay. They say مسحهم - he slaughtered them easily, just like wiping is easy and effortless.

There's also مسح الإبل - when a camel travels all day long. If this meaning applies, then Masih refers to one who has gone on a very long journey, one who has traveled a serious distance.

These are the linguistic origins of the word Masih. The most common meaning in religious context is "the Anointed One," the purified one. Interestingly, in hadith literature describing when Isa (AS) returns, his hair will be wet, as though liquid has been passed over it.

Now let's examine the ayah:

وقولهم إنا قتلنا المسيح عيسى ابن مريم رسول الله - "And because of their statement: 'We killed Al-Masih Isa, the son of Maryam, the Messenger of Allah.'"

Notice something critical here. When they say "Rasul Allah" (Messenger of Allah), they're not actually acknowledging him as a true messenger. Rather, this is attributed to them sarcastically or mockingly. They're essentially saying, "We killed him. He thinks he's a messenger of God. We killed him." Their implication is: messengers can't be killed, and we killed him, therefore he's not a messenger.

If we take رسول الله as their actual words, this is the second time such stylistic sarcasm appears in this surah. Remember earlier, Allah ﷻ said: "Whatever good comes to you is from Allah, and whatever bad happens is from yourself." There was a shift in the Arabic particles there - from عندك ("at you," showing possession) to من نفسك ("from yourself," showing source but not possession). The correction removed their claim to ownership over events, because only Allah ﷻ has ultimate ownership over what occurs.

وما قتلوه وما صلبوه - "And they didn't kill him at all, and they didn't crucify him at all."

The word صلب refers to crucifixion. The practice involved wooden planks with nails coming out of them. The person was tied to them until the nails pierced through their body. They didn't do that to Isa (AS) at all, despite what they claimed and despite what later became a central narrative.

ولكن شبه لهم - "However, it was made to appear to them." It looked very similar to that for them. The word شبه also carries the meaning of confusion - this matter was made confusing to them. It became unclear, ambiguous.

وإن الذين اختلفوا فيه لفي شك منه - "And those who have a disagreement about it, no doubt about it, I swear to it, they are in doubt about it."

Whether about the crucifixion itself or about Isa (AS), they are in doubt. This is a remarkable statement about the uncertainty that surrounds this event.

Consider what scholars of Christianity itself have discovered. Professors like Gerald Dirks and Laurence Brown - former Christian scholars who studied Biblical texts at the highest academic levels - point to something stunning. In Biblical scholarship, there's a method called text sourcing, similar to our علم الإسناد where we trace narratives back to their original sources.

According to these scholars, when you apply rigorous text sourcing to the Bible, there are three or four things that simply cannot find reliable textual sources: the virgin birth as a miraculous event, Jesus being the literal son of God, the crucifixion, and the Trinity. These fundamental Christian doctrines lack solid textual foundation.

Professor Dirks, who was a Methodist Deacon, explains that many top-level Christian scholars, ministers and preachers giving sermons to thousands every Sunday, are afraid to say what they actually believe based on their scholarship. They're afraid because people won't be able to handle it. Many of these top-level ministers don't actually believe that Jesus is the son of God or that he was crucified. They don't consider that reliable information based on the evidence. They don't believe in the Trinity anymore.

ما لهم به من علم إلا اتباع الظن - "They have no knowledge whatsoever about it except the pursuit of assumption, the following of conjecture."

وما قتلوه يقينًا - "And they did not kill him, certainly. They did not kill him at all."

The emphasis on يقين (certainty) is powerful. There is absolute certainty that this did not happen.

translation: Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
بَل رَّفَعَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا

بل رفعه الله إليه - "Rather, they didn't kill him at all. Rather, Allah raised him towards Himself."

This ayah makes it absolutely clear that Isa (AS) did not die, because an alternative is offered. If the alternative was that he died naturally, the ayah would say: ما قتلوه بل مات - "They didn't kill him, rather he died." But that's not what's said.

Some interpreters claim that رفعه الله means Allah ﷻ raised him in rank, in honor, in spiritual status, and that eventually he died a natural death. But this interpretation has a fundamental flaw.

When you use the word رفع (to raise), you must qualify it with something if you mean a figurative raising. رفعت الكتاب - "I raised the book" is physical. رفعتك في المقام - "I elevated you in terms of status" - I've qualified it with "in terms of status," so now you know it's figurative.

When Allah ﷻ says He raised the mountain above them, is that figurative? No, it's literal. If He raises somebody in some abstract way, He qualifies it. يرفع الله الذين آمنوا منكم والذين أوتوا العلم درجات - "Allah raises those who believe among you and those given knowledge in degrees." The qualification is درجات - "in degrees, in ranks."

Here, there's no qualification. رفعه الله إليه - just "He raised him to Himself." That's it. This is our belief: that Isa (AS) was physically raised to Allah ﷻ and did not die.

وما قتلوه يقينًا - "They didn't kill him, absolutely." There is absolute certainty that this did not happen.

وكان الله عزيزًا حكيمًا - "And Allah has always been and always will be the Ultimate Authority, All-Wise."

The choice of these two names is significant. عزيز means the One with ultimate power and authority. Allah ﷻ had the power to save His messenger from this humiliation. حكيم means All-Wise. There is wisdom in how this unfolded, wisdom in the confusion that was created, wisdom in raising Isa (AS) when He did.

These ayahs establish a clear Islamic position on one of the most contentious issues between Muslims and Christians. They affirm the honor of both Maryam (AS) and Isa (AS), reject the crucifixion narrative, and declare with certainty that Isa (AS) was raised to Allah ﷻ and will return.

translation: And there is none from the People of the Scripture but that he will surely believe in Jesus before his death. And on the Day of Resurrection he will be against them a witness
commentary:
**وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا**

*And there is not one from among the People of the Book except that he will surely believe in him before his death, and on the Day of Judgment he will be a witness against them.*

This ayah has been interpreted in two significant ways by the scholars. The key difference revolves around understanding the pronoun "his" in the phrase "before his death."

The first interpretation holds that every member of the People of the Book will believe in Isa (AS) before their own death. According to this view, even if someone dies as a Christian or Jew without apparently accepting Isa (AS) as the messenger of Allah ﷻ, in those final moments before death—when the soul is departing and they're in that state between consciousness and the unseen—Allah ﷻ reveals the truth to them. They realize who Isa (AS) truly is. However, this realization comes too late to benefit them, as faith accepted at the moment of death is not accepted.

The stronger and more widely held opinion among scholars is that the pronoun refers to Isa (AS) himself. This interpretation completely changes the meaning of the ayah. It tells us that there will not be a single member of the People of the Book left except that they will believe in him before Isa (AS) dies. This points to a future reality: Isa (AS) will return, and when he comes back, he will live until his natural death, which hasn't occurred yet. By the time he passes away, every remaining member of the People of the Book will have become Muslim or will have come to believe in him as they should have from the beginning.

Notice the grammatical construction here: إِن at the beginning followed by إِلَّا. This structure creates an emphatic negative—there is absolutely nothing except this scenario. It's not going to be even a single member of the People of the Book who remains outside this reality.

The phrase أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (People of the Book) itself is worth reflecting upon. This term, which we often use interchangeably with "those who were given the Book," is actually quite positive in its connotation. The word أَهْل suggests people who are worthy, qualified and truly knowledgeable in their scripture. Those who are genuinely versed in the Book—when they encounter Isa (AS) toward the end, there will be no choice left for them except to believe.

The ayah concludes with the statement that on the Day of Judgment, Isa (AS) will be a witness against them. For those who rejected him or distorted his message, he will stand as a witness to what they did and what the truth actually was.

translation: For wrongdoing on the part of the Jews, We made unlawful for them [certain] good foods which had been lawful to them, and for their averting from the way of Allah many [people]
commentary:
**فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَـٰتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ وَبِصَدِّهِمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا**

*So for wrongdoing on the part of those who were Jewish, We made unlawful for them good things which had been lawful to them, and for their turning away many from the way of Allah.*

Allah ﷻ now shifts to discuss additional crimes and their consequences. Because of wrongdoing that came from the Jewish people, Allah ﷻ forbade them from certain good and pure things that were originally permissible. Some of the Companions (RA) who had been Jewish before accepting Islam explained this ayah from their own experience. One former rabbi described how they understood the Sabbath restrictions as a punishment that was given to them because of their transgressions.

The word حَرَّمْنَا (We made forbidden) carries two meanings that both apply here. It means to make something unlawful, but it also means to deprive. A person who is مَحْرُوم is someone deprived of something. So this isn't just about certain foods or practices becoming impermissible—it's about Allah ﷻ removing access, depriving them of good things, luxuries and comforts in life that they originally had. The word حَلَّ (permissible) similarly has a dual meaning: it means lawful, but it also means accessible. So the parallel meaning is powerful: things that were both permissible and accessible to them became forbidden and were stripped away.

The ayah continues: وَبِصَدِّهِمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا—and because of their obstruction from the path of Allah ﷻ, abundantly. The word صَدّ is particularly meaningful because it functions both transitively and intransitively. It means they stopped themselves from Allah's path, and it means they stopped others from Allah's path. Both dimensions are captured in this single word.

There's a grammatical consideration here: this phrase could be a continuation of the previous statement about wrongdoing, or it could be starting a new sentence. If it's a new sentence, then the punishment for this particular crime—stopping people from Allah's path—remains unmentioned, left to our imagination. The very act of turning people away from guidance is so serious that its consequence is beyond what words can capture.

translation: And [for] their taking of usury while they had been forbidden from it, and their consuming of the people's wealth unjustly. And we have prepared for the disbelievers among them a painful punishment
commentary:
**وَأَخْذِهِمُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ وَقَدْ نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ ۚ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا**

*And for their taking of interest when they had been forbidden from it, and their consuming of people's wealth unjustly. And We have prepared for the disbelievers among them a painful punishment.*

Notice that this ayah lacks the بِ prefix that appeared in the previous statements. This grammatical detail suggests the second and third accusations are connected to each other, making the interpretation that these form a new sentence the stronger position.

The crime of الرِّبَا (interest/usury) is mentioned here with an important clarification: وَقَدْ نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ—they had already been forbidden from it. The prohibition against riba is not something new to Islam or unique to the message given to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The Jewish people were already forbidden from this financial crime in their own revelation.

What follows is particularly striking: وَأَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ—their consuming of people's wealth through falsehood. The fact that Allah ﷻ mentions riba and immediately follows it with consuming people's wealth unjustly is significant. If you examine the relationship between an economy founded on interest-based lending and people being swindled out of their savings, you find disturbing parallels.

Consider how this works in practice: someone buys a house for $50,000 with a 30-year mortgage. By the time they finish paying, they've paid $200,000 for that property. But the house itself is now 30 years old—the materials, foundation and systems are all worth less than they were originally. Yet when they sell it, they need to recover at least what they paid, so they sell it for $250,000. The next buyer takes out a mortgage and will pay even more over their 30 years. As generations pass, that same piece of property—same bricks, same wood, same land—keeps inflating in price not because of its actual value, but because of the system itself.

Or consider what happened with currency and gold: citizens were once required to surrender their gold to the Treasury in exchange for cash. A brick of gold was exchanged for $10,000 in 1910. A hundred years later, that $10,000 in paper money sitting in a safe can't buy back anywhere near that brick of gold. The fluctuating value of currency, the foreign exchange system, the way life savings can become worthless overnight—these are forms of robbery that have become so normalized that we accept them as just how things work.

When Muslim families got caught up in the housing bubble, paying $300,000 for houses actually worth $150,000, then finding themselves owing $450,000 when the market collapsed for a house now worth $120,000—they're trapped in debt for something that doesn't exist, an artificial loan of no value. If that's not theft, what is?

The more complicated a contract needs to be, the more legal terminology and loopholes and tiny font required—that's usually a good indication that if it were written plainly and clearly, everyone would recognize it as theft. This is the nature of much of our modern financial system.

What's remarkable is that some people have come to Islam purely from recognizing the corruption in these systems. One brother shared that simply learning about the financial system made him realize something was fundamentally wrong—not just from a halal-haram perspective, but because it was destroying people's lives. He recognized it as a cancer eating away at society, and that recognition led him to search for truth.

The ayah concludes: وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا—We have prepared for the disbelievers among them a painful punishment. The consequence awaits those who persist in rejection.

translation: But those firm in knowledge among them and the believers believe in what has been revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what was revealed before you. And the establishers of prayer [especially] and the givers of zakah and the believers in Allah and the Last Day - those We will give a great reward
commentary:
**لَّـٰكِنِ ٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ مِنْهُمْ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ وَٱلْمُقِيمِينَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَٱلْمُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا**

*But those firmly grounded in knowledge among them and the believers - they believe in what has been revealed to you and what was revealed before you, and those who establish prayer and those who give zakah and the believers in Allah and the Last Day - those We will give a great reward.*

After describing the corruption and crimes, Allah ﷻ now presents the other side: الرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ—those deeply rooted in knowledge. These are people among the People of the Book who are genuinely and sincerely versed in revelation. When such people come into genuine contact with the Quran, they don't hesitate to accept it.

The description وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (and the believers) refers to the Muslims, so this ayah is speaking about both groups: those from the People of the Book who are truly knowledgeable, and the Muslim believers. Both groups يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ—believe in what has been sent down to you, meaning the Quran, وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ—and what was revealed much before, meaning the previous scriptures.

Then comes something grammatically remarkable: وَٱلْمُقِيمِينَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ. The grammatical case suddenly shifts from marfu' (nominative) to mansub (accusative). Some critics have foolishly claimed this is a grammatical error in the Quran. In reality, this is a well-known rhetorical device in Arabic called الْقَطْع—a deliberate case shift within a list to highlight something of particular importance. It's like saying, "and especially those who establish prayer constantly."

The difference between وَٱلْمُقِيمِينَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ (those who establish prayer) and a phrase like يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ (they establish prayer) is significant. المُقِيمِينَ emphasizes constancy and permanence. These are people for whom establishing salah is not occasional but continuous, a defining characteristic.

Similarly, وَٱلْمُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—those who are constantly giving zakah. This isn't just about the annual obligation but suggests an ongoing state of generosity and fulfillment of this pillar.

The ayah concludes by returning to the foundation: وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ—those who are strong and permanent in their faith in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day. These are the ones, أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا—those We will soon compensate with a tremendous reward.

translation: Indeed, We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him. And we revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, the Descendants, Jesus, Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon, and to David We gave the book [of Psalms]
commentary:
**۞ إِنَّآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ كَمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَعِيسَىٰ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُونُسَ وَهَـٰرُونَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا**

*Indeed, We have revealed to you as We revealed to Nuh and the prophets after him. And We revealed to Ibrahim, Ismail, Ishaq, Ya'qub, the Descendants, Isa, Ayyub, Yunus, Harun and Sulaiman, and to Dawud We gave the Zabur.*

This ayah contains a small collection of prophets mentioned by name. In the entire Quran, Allah ﷻ mentions only a handful of prophets explicitly. If you compare this to the Biblical collection, you'll find literally hundreds of names and extensive historical accounts. The Quran's style is completely different—it doesn't provide elaborate historical narratives or excessive detail.

Even events from the Prophet Muhammad's ﷺ own life are mentioned in passing. For instance, the Battle of Ahzab is referenced with جَاءُوكُم مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ—"they came at you from above you and from below you." Allah ﷻ doesn't explain which border of Madinah they approached from, how many came from each direction or the tactical details. The Quran is not concerned with that level of historical documentation.

Does mentioning only a few prophets mean these are the only ones? Absolutely not. Prophets have been sent throughout history across all nations. The Quran tells us that every nation received messengers. This is important when considering other ancient traditions—the Greek tradition, the Zoroastrian tradition and others. We don't have the names of their prophets, but we know they must have been sent guidance.

Sometimes in comparative religion courses, you'll encounter ideas in other faiths that are remarkably close to Islamic teachings. Orientalists use this to suggest that Islam borrowed from these other traditions. For example, the Greeks had a concept of seven heavens and seven earths. Some claim Muslims took this idea from Greek philosophy. Zoroastrians have a concept of a bridge that must be crossed on Judgment Day—if you fall, you enter hellfire; if you cross successfully, you reach paradise. We have a similar concept with the Sirat. Again, critics claim this was borrowed.

The reality is exactly the opposite: the fact that these concepts exist in other religious traditions means they contain traces of Islam left from their own prophets who taught these truths. When it comes to core beliefs—the nature of Allah ﷻ, the afterlife and the fate of nations—these have never changed. The Jews weren't given a different aqeedah than we have. The Christians weren't originally told about a different Jannah by Isa (AS). The fundamental beliefs have always been the same.

We may have more details than they currently possess, or they may have had details that were later lost. In fact, Jewish history shows that at one point, they didn't even have a concept of the afterlife remaining in their tradition. Many scholars believe they recovered it through exposure to Zoroastrians, who maintained this belief. And while we commonly think of Zoroastrians as fire worshippers, many of them were actually monotheists who believed in One God. Their rituals involved fire, but they didn't worship it. These traditions are more complex than surface assumptions suggest.

The point is that Allah ﷻ mentions only a handful of prophets—just enough for us to have appreciation, and significantly, keeping in mind who the audience is. The People of the Book are the audience here, so which prophets does Allah ﷻ mention? The ones that ring a bell for them. The ones they've heard about, the ones they know from their own scriptures and traditions.

إِنَّآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ—No doubt We have sent revelation to you, كَمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ—just as We sent revelation to Nuh (AS) وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ—and the prophets that came much after him. Then Allah ﷻ provides examples: We sent revelation to Ibrahim (AS), Ismail (AS), Ishaq (AS), Ya'qub (AS), al-Asbat (the descendants/tribes), Isa (AS), Ayyub (AS)—going back to this ancient prophet—Yunus (AS), Harun (AS) and Sulaiman (AS).

The ayah concludes with special mention: وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا—and to Dawud (AS) We gave the Zabur. The contemporary term for this is the Psalms of David. This scripture is highlighted particularly because the entire Zabur was dedicated to praising Allah ﷻ. The Jews had received laws, and their hearts were becoming hard, so Allah ﷻ sent them revelation specifically designed to soften their hearts through constant praise and remembrance of Him.

For us, it's different. The Book we received combines both dimensions—the laws of the Torah and the praises of the Zabur are together in the Quran. We have both the structure and guidance of law and the heart-softening remembrance and praise integrated into one revelation.

translation: And [We sent] messengers about whom We have related [their stories] to you before and messengers about whom We have not related to you. And Allah spoke to Moses with [direct] speech
commentary:
وَرُسُلًا قَدْ قَصَصْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَرُسُلًا لَّمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ ۚ وَكَلَّمَ ٱللَّهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا

This ayah establishes a profound reality about the scope of prophethood throughout human history. Allah ﷻ tells us there are messengers whose stories He has narrated in detail, and messengers He has not mentioned to us at all. The Arabic construction here is significant: رُسُلًا قَدْ قَصَصْنَـٰهُمْ (messengers We have narrated to you) refers to the prophets whose stories we find elaborated in the Quran. But then Allah ﷻ says وَرُسُلًا لَّمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ (and messengers We have not narrated to you).

These aren't lesser messengers. The emphasis in the Arabic suggests these were remarkable prophets whose stories simply weren't included in the Quran. Every prophet sent by Allah ﷻ carried tremendous responsibility and faced unique challenges in their time and place. The fact that we don't know their names or stories doesn't diminish their significance in Allah's plan.

The ayah concludes with a special mention: وَكَلَّمَ ٱللَّهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا (and Allah spoke to Musa, a direct conversation). The emphasis through تَكْلِيمًا shows this wasn't indirect communication or inspiration alone. Allah ﷻ engaged Prophet Musa (AS) in actual dialogue, back-and-forth conversation. This unique honor distinguishes Musa (AS) among the prophets, earning him the title Kalimullah, the one who spoke with Allah ﷻ.

translation: [We sent] messengers as bringers of good tidings and warners so that mankind will have no argument against Allah after the messengers. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
رُّسُلًا مُّبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ حُجَّةٌۢ بَعْدَ ٱلرُّسُلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا

This ayah addresses a fundamental question many people struggle with today: Why do we need prophets at all? If God wants to guide people, why doesn't He just send revelation directly to everyone? Why must it come through human messengers?

The answer lies in understanding Allah's complete system of guidance. Before any messenger was sent, Allah ﷻ had already equipped human beings with everything they need to recognize truth. He gave us intellect—the ability to hear, see and process information. He gave us the ruh, the soul, which carries within it an innate recognition of its Creator. He gave us the nafs with its built-in moral compass, that inner voice distinguishing right from wrong. These weren't added after messengers came; they were part of our original design.

Beyond our internal faculties, Allah ﷻ filled creation with ayat, signs pointing to Him. The natural world is full of clues for those who observe and reflect. So technically, we already had what we needed to arrive at the truth of Allah's existence and our accountability to Him.

But then Allah ﷻ, in His wisdom and mercy, added something He didn't have to: He sent messengers. These prophets came as مُّبَشِّرِينَ (bearers of good news) and مُنذِرِينَ (warners). They reminded people of what they already possessed inside themselves. They pointed to the signs people were overlooking. They revived the fitra that had been buried under layers of distraction and misguidance.

The purpose is stated clearly: لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ حُجَّةٌۢ بَعْدَ ٱلرُّسُلِ (so that people would have no argument against Allah after the messengers). The word حُجَّة (hujjah) refers to a legal argument, a defense case. On the Day of Judgment, no one can say, "I didn't know. No one told me. I had no way of finding out." The messengers removed that excuse completely.

Allah ﷻ is saying: I gave you intellect, soul and conscience. I surrounded you with signs. And on top of all that, I sent messengers to make it crystal clear. What excuse do you have left?

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (and Allah is Ever-Mighty, Ever-Wise). He is Aziz—He had the authority to hold people accountable even without sending messengers. But He is also Hakim—His wisdom moved Him to send prophets anyway, sealing off every possible excuse and making guidance accessible to all.

translation: But Allah bears witness to that which He has revealed to you. He has sent it down with His knowledge, and the angels bear witness [as well]. And sufficient is Allah as Witness
commentary:
لَّـٰكِنِ ٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ أَنزَلَهُۥ بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا

When people question the authenticity of revelation, when they doubt whether the Quran truly comes from Allah ﷻ, this ayah provides the ultimate response. لَّـٰكِنِ ٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكَ (But Allah testifies to what He has revealed to you). Allah ﷻ Himself bears witness to the truth of this Book.

This testimony isn't just a claim—it manifests in the Quran itself. When someone approaches the Quran with genuine sincerity, seeking truth rather than seeking to criticize, the Book validates itself. Its depth, its coherence, its guidance, its impact on the human heart—all of this serves as Allah's testimony to its divine origin.

أَنزَلَهُۥ بِعِلْمِهِۦ (He sent it down with His knowledge). This is one of the most beautiful phrases in the Quran. Allah ﷻ sent this Book with full knowledge of who would receive it, when they would receive it and what they would need until the end of time. Every ayah, every word, every letter was placed with complete awareness of its relevance across all generations and circumstances.

People sometimes say, "If the Quran were revealed today, it would be different. Times have changed." This phrase responds: أَنزَلَهُۥ بِعِلْمِهِۦ. Allah ﷻ knew exactly what He was sending and who He was sending it for. He knew the challenges of the 7th century and the 21st century and every century in between and beyond. His knowledge encompassed it all when He revealed this Book.

وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ (and the angels bear witness). The angels who carried this revelation, who witnessed its descent, who see its effects—they all testify to its truth. Yet even their witness isn't necessary, because وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (Allah is sufficient as a witness). When Allah ﷻ testifies to something, no additional proof is needed.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah have certainly gone far astray
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا

This ayah describes a particular category of disbelievers—not just those who reject faith for themselves, but those who actively obstruct others from the path of Allah ﷻ. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (Indeed, those who disbelieve and obstruct from the way of Allah).

The word صَدُّوا۟ means to block, to prevent, to turn people away. These are individuals who encountered the truth, recognized it for what it was, and still chose to reject it. Worse, they made it their mission to stop others from finding that truth. They see someone exploring Islam and intervene: "What are you doing? Are you going to worship the devil? Are you going to follow those terrorists?" They find a Quran under their family member's pillow and lose their minds, doing everything possible to prevent that person from continuing their search.

This isn't mere disbelief—it's active opposition to guidance. The result? قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (they have strayed in extreme error). The repetition of the word ضَلَـٰلًۢا (going astray) followed by بَعِيدًا (far, distant) emphasizes just how lost they are. They haven't just wandered off the path; they've gone so far in the wrong direction that finding their way back becomes nearly impossible.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve and commit wrong [or injustice] - never will Allah forgive them, nor will He guide them to a path
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَظَلَمُوا۟ لَمْ يَكُنِ ٱللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقًا

The severity intensifies. Those described in the previous ayah—who disbelieved and obstructed—are now described as those who كَفَرُوا۟ وَظَلَمُوا۟ (disbelieved and did wrong). The word ظَلَمُوا۟ means they committed dhulm, wrongdoing or oppression. Disbelief is already a form of injustice against oneself, but this refers to something beyond—they went on the attack against Islam, against Muslims, against the message of truth.

For such people, the ayah states something sobering: لَمْ يَكُنِ ٱللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ (Allah would not be one to forgive them). The phrasing لَمْ يَكُنِ indicates it's not befitting Allah ﷻ to forgive them in their current state. This doesn't mean Allah ﷻ is incapable of forgiveness—He is the Most Merciful. But for those who persist in disbelief and active opposition until death, forgiveness doesn't apply.

وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقًا (nor to guide them to any path). They've closed themselves off to guidance so completely that Allah ﷻ will not guide them to any road of goodness.

translation: Except the path of Hell; they will abide therein forever. And that, for Allah, is [always] easy
commentary:
إِلَّا طَرِيقَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا

The exception comes: إِلَّا طَرِيقَ جَهَنَّمَ (except the path of Hell). The only path they'll be guided to is the one leading to Jahannam. They chose obstruction over truth, aggression over reflection, and that choice has its consequence: خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (remaining in it eternally, forever).

Then comes a phrase that shocks some people: وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (and that is easy for Allah). Some extreme groups in Islamic history actually claimed that Hell is just a scare tactic—that Allah ﷻ wouldn't really punish people that severely. They compared it to a mother threatening her child: "I'll put you in the blender if you don't come downstairs!" Obviously, she won't actually do it. It's just to get the child to behave.

But Allah ﷻ explicitly addresses this delusion. Punishing the wicked isn't difficult for Him. He doesn't find it burdensome or troubling. If justice demands it, وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا—it's easy for Him to carry out. This isn't a bluff or an empty threat. The descriptions of Hell in the Quran are real, and we should never delude ourselves into thinking otherwise.

translation: O Mankind, the Messenger has come to you with the truth from your Lord, so believe; it is better for you. But if you disbelieve - then indeed, to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and earth. And ever is Allah Knowing and Wise
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِٱلْحَقِّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

Throughout this surah, Allah ﷻ has addressed different audiences: believers, hypocrites, People of the Book, and polytheists. Now everyone is gathered together under one address: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity).

The message is direct: قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِٱلْحَقِّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ (The Messenger has come to you with truth from your Master). This isn't speculation or philosophy. This is حَقِّ —truth, reality. And it comes مِن رَّبِّكُمْ (from your Rabb), the One who created you, sustains you and knows what's best for you.

فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ (So believe—it's better for you). The phrasing is almost casual, like giving friendly advice. Believe, and it will be good for you. This is for your own benefit, not for Allah's sake.

وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (And if you disbelieve, then indeed to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth). The implication is clear: Your disbelief doesn't hurt Allah ﷻ. He doesn't need your faith. He already owns everything in existence. If you choose to reject the truth, you're only harming yourself.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (And Allah is Ever-Knowing, Ever-Wise). He knows exactly what you're thinking, what excuses you're making and what's really in your heart. You won't outsmart Him or find some loophole in His wisdom.

translation: O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا

The surah returns to directly addressing the People of the Book with crucial guidance. When Allah ﷻ corrects the mistakes of previous communities, Muslims must pay close attention. Their failures can easily become ours if we're not careful.

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ (O People of the Book, do not go to extremes in your religion). The word تَغْلُوا۟ comes from ghuluw, meaning to become expensive, to go too far, to exceed proper bounds. When something becomes ghali, it means its price has shot up excessively. When applied to religion, it means going overboard, becoming extreme, losing balance.

The warning against extremism is profound. Islam is fundamentally about balance—balancing our responsibilities, our priorities, our understanding. We have obligations to Allah ﷻ, to ourselves, to our families, to society. We have to care for our bodies, our souls, our intellects and our time. When we take one aspect of religion and make it everything, we've fallen into ghuluw.

Consider the story of Qarun, whose wealth came from his intelligence and business acumen. When people advised him, Allah ﷻ deemed their counsel so valuable that He preserved it in the Quran. They told him: Use what Allah has given you to build your home in the Hereafter, but don't forget your portion in this world. Excel in the way Allah has excelled toward you. The message was balance—don't abandon this world, and don't abandon the next.

Extremism manifests in many ways. Someone becomes obsessed with halal meat to the point where they refuse to eat at anyone's home, they travel across continents to verify slaughterhouses and compile lists of restaurants they've deemed impermissible. Yes, halal is important—but there's a line. Another person becomes fixated on appearance: beard length, pants height, missed sunnah prayers. These matters have importance, but when they consume all your attention, you've lost balance.

Ghuluw happens in beliefs too. Someone loves the Prophet ﷺ deeply—as we all should. They want to praise him constantly. Then it goes further: they have no time left for remembering Allah ﷻ because they're only remembering the Prophet ﷺ. Then further: they praise him in ways that elevate him beyond his station. When someone tries to advise caution, they're labeled as hating the Prophet ﷺ. This progression—this is ghuluw.

The Christians fell into this trap with Isa (AS). It started with genuine love and reverence. It progressed to excessive praise. Eventually, it led to claiming divinity for him. Allah ﷻ corrects this directly: إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ (The Messiah, Isa son of Maryam, was only a messenger of Allah).

That's what he is—a messenger. Noble, blessed, miraculous in his birth, but a messenger nonetheless. وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ (and His word which He cast to Maryam). Allah's command "Be!" resulted in Isa's miraculous conception. He came into existence through Allah's word, through His creative power.

وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ (and a spirit from Him). This doesn't mean Isa (AS) is part of Allah ﷻ—it means his soul came from Allah, just as every human soul comes from Allah. But his creation was unique, miraculous, a sign of Allah's power.

فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ (So believe in Allah and His messengers). Don't elevate the messenger to the status of the One who sent him.

وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ (And do not say 'Three'). Don't claim trinity. Don't divide Allah's essence or assign partners to Him. ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ (Desist—it's better for you). Stop this belief. Stop this teaching. It will be better for you to abandon it.

إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ (Indeed, Allah is but one God). This is the fundamental truth, the bedrock of faith. He is One in His essence, His attributes, His right to be worshipped.

سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ (Exalted is He above having a son). The concept of Allah ﷻ having a child is so far beneath His majesty that we declare Him سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ—utterly free from such an imperfection. The Creator of all things doesn't reproduce. He doesn't need offspring. He doesn't share His dominion.

لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth). Everything already belongs to Him exclusively. What need would He have for a son?

وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (And sufficient is Allah as a Disposer of affairs). The ayah ends by addressing another Christian claim—that Isa (AS) serves as an intercessor, someone to take care of their problems, someone to manage their affairs. Allah ﷻ responds: I am enough as your Wakil. I am sufficient to handle everything you need. You don't need an intermediary. You don't need someone else to take care of you. Allah ﷻ is enough.

translation: Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant - He will gather them to Himself all together
commentary:
لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا

Allah ﷻ introduces a powerful concept in this ayah through the word يَسْتَنكِفَ. In Quranic Arabic, there are two distinct words for embarrassment: الاستحياء and الاستنكاف. The word istihya appears earlier in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ says He is not embarrassed to present an example, even of a mosquito. But istinkaf represents a different kind of embarrassment altogether.

Istinkaf comes from the root word أنف, meaning nose. The concept evolved from the image of someone raising their nose, being snotty or snobbish. When you practice istinkaf, you think something is clearly beneath you. You've been asked to do something that you consider below your station or dignity. Perhaps you're the president of the masjid and someone asks you to vacuum. "I don't vacuum," you might think. Or you're a doctor and someone asks you to help organize shoes at the entrance. "That's beneath me. What are you asking me to do? It's not in my job description."

Allah ﷻ declares that لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ (the Messiah will never think it beneath him). The use of لَن is significant - it doesn't just indicate that Isa (AS) wasn't embarrassed in the past, but that he won't ever be embarrassed. He isn't now and he won't be in the future. When he returns, he will still proudly declare himself a slave of Allah ﷻ, not claim lordship.

The ayah continues: أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ (to be a slave to Allah). The beauty here lies in how Isa (AS) positions himself. He doesn't even claim to be a special slave of Allah ﷻ - just عَبْدًا (a slave), any slave among slaves, simply blending in. He made himself humble in declaring this slavery, diminishing any notion of special status. "I'm just any slave of Allah ﷻ."
