وَلَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (nor the closest angels). Even the highest angels, those nearest to Allah ﷻ, don't think it's beneath them to declare themselves slaves. They embrace this identity with honor.

Consider what it means to be a slave in the truest sense. If there's one person who has no room for pride, who cannot say "that's beneath me," it's a slave. A slave occupies the lowest position imaginable. An employee can refuse certain tasks, citing their job description. But a slave? Nothing is beneath a slave because the slave is already at the bottom. Yet when it comes to being a slave of Allah ﷻ, this isn't an insult - it's the highest honor. The Christians would call this blasphemy, but Isa (AS) himself doesn't think so.

وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ (And whoever thinks it beneath themselves to worship Him and shows arrogance). The conjunction و (wa) here connects two concepts. Someone who considers worship beneath them is manifesting الاستكبار (arrogance). Or we can understand it as two separate but related attitudes: thinking worship is beneath you, and on top of that, displaying arrogance.

What does this look like practically? Imagine you're angry and someone reminds you of good manners taught by the Prophet ﷺ about lowering your voice or remaining calm. Your response? "Not right now. Don't bother me with that right now. I don't need to hear about being calm when I'm angry. Tell me about it when I've already calmed down." This is istinkaf - thinking that the command of Allah ﷻ doesn't apply to your current situation, that you're somehow above being corrected at this particular moment.

فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا (then He will gather them all toward Himself). Notice the choice of يَحْشُرُ rather than يَجْمَعُ. Both words mean "to gather," but hashr specifically refers to herding animals - creatures that are whipped and driven forward in fear. Jam'a is used for gathering human beings with dignity. By using hashr, Allah ﷻ indicates that those who were too proud to submit will be treated like animals on the Day of Judgment, herded without the dignity afforded to humans. You acted too proud to be My slave? I'll treat you like cattle instead.

translation: And as for those who believed and did righteous deeds, He will give them in full their rewards and grant them extra from His bounty. But as for those who disdained and were arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment, and they will not find for themselves besides Allah any protector or helper
commentary:
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَنكَفُوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا

Allah ﷻ presents the contrast: فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (As for those who believed and did righteous deeds). Either you're someone who thinks correction is beneath you, or you're a believer who acts on that belief. The key is found in that second part - وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (and did righteous deeds).

Being reminded to obey Allah ﷻ at the very moment we're in disobedience, and actually stopping ourselves and changing our course without making excuses - this is what Allah ﷻ will compensate. Consider how people apologize. Someone says, "I'm so sorry for what I did," but then the next three paragraphs explain why they're not really sorry. "But the situation was this and this and this. And you had done this and this and this." That's not an apology. When you're truly sorry, all you say is, "I'm sorry. I have no excuse. I take full responsibility." That's the end. You don't add, "But there are two sides to the story" or "Let me bring another perspective to the table."

This is our behavior toward each other, and especially concerning our obligations to Allah ﷻ. When it comes to fulfilling our commitments, we cannot present rationalizations. We simply fulfill them or genuinely apologize without qualification. True submission means changing course when corrected, immediately, without defensiveness.

فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ (then He will fulfill for them their full compensation). Allah ﷻ will give them exactly what they earned, held back nothing.

وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ (and increase them from His special favor). When you have to perform an act of submission to Allah ﷻ, you might think you're missing out on some worldly opportunity. But you haven't missed anything. Allah ﷻ will increase you from His special favor. The loss you thought you were taking? It becomes a gain beyond what you could have imagined.

وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَنكَفُوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (As for those who were disdainful and arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment). Those who considered themselves too high and holy, too embarrassed to submit themselves, will face painful consequences.

وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (and they will not find for themselves besides Allah any protector or helper). When that Day comes, there will be no one to turn to, no friend to protect them, no helper to rescue them. They will stand alone before Allah ﷻ.

translation: O mankind, there has come to you a conclusive proof from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a clear light
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُم بُرْهَـٰنٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ نُورًا مُّبِينًا

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity). Allah ﷻ addresses all people, not just believers. This is a universal declaration.

قَدْ جَآءَكُم بُرْهَـٰنٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ (there has certainly come to you decisive proof from your Lord). The word بُرْهَان comes from the root برح, meaning to be white and shiny. Some scholars trace it even further to the Persian word "burun," referring to a sword so white, sharp and shiny that it can cut through anything. A burhan is an undefeatable proof, an argument that cannot be countered or competed with. It cuts through all doubts like a sharp blade.

This decisive proof has come from رَّبِّكُمْ (your Lord) - your Master, the One who nurtures and sustains you.

وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ نُورًا مُّبِينًا (and We have sent down to you a clear light). Some scholars interpret the burhan as referring to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ himself, while the clear light refers to the Quran. Others say both refer to the Quran - it is both the decisive proof and the illuminating light. Still others suggest the burhan refers to the natural disposition (fitrah) within every human being that recognizes truth, and then the Quran was sent as an additional light.

Regardless of the specific interpretation, the message is clear: what has come to you isn't just clear in itself - نُورًا مُّبِينًا (a clarifying light) means it gives light to everything around you. It allows you to see all of your surroundings. The Quran doesn't just illuminate itself; it illuminates your entire life, showing you the way forward in every situation.

translation: So those who believe in Allah and hold fast to Him - He will admit them to mercy from Himself and bounty and guide them to Himself on a straight path
commentary:
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِى رَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلٍ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا

فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِهِۦ (As for those who believe in Allah and hold firmly to Him). This is a review of a concept presented earlier in the surah. Remember what was said about holding onto Allah ﷻ? The word اعتصام means to grip tightly, to cling with all your strength.

فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِى رَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ (He will admit them into mercy from Him). To understand رَحْمَة (mercy), we must return to its linguistic root. The word comes from رَحِم, the womb of the mother. What's the relationship between a mother's womb and mercy? In the womb, you are completely taken care of. You have no worries, no sadness, no grief, no concerns. Everything you could possibly need is provided. The one showing you this comprehensive care is your mother, extending every courtesy to you without you even having to ask.

When Allah ﷻ says He will enter them into mercy, it means He'll place them in a situation where they need nothing. Every single one of their needs will be taken care of completely. They won't even feel the need to ask - just like in the womb.

وَفَضْلٍ (and favor). On top of that complete mercy, He'll give them additional gifts, hook them up even further with blessings beyond what they could imagine or need.

وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (and guide them to Himself on a straight path). Some scholars interpret all three parts - the mercy, the favor, and the guidance - as referring to levels of Paradise. First, Allah ﷻ admits them into Paradise. Then He shows them additional, more luxurious quarters. Then, following a straight path, they get to meet Allah ﷻ Himself. The introductory Paradise is just the appetizer, then comes the deluxe package, the premium level, and finally the ultimate reward: meeting the Creator.

Others interpret this guidance as continuing in this world. For those who hold firmly to Allah ﷻ, He continues to guide them throughout their lives until they die. They simply have to maintain their grip on Him, and Allah ﷻ will ensure they remain guided. May Allah ﷻ make us among them.

translation: They request from you a [legal] ruling. Say, "Allah gives you a ruling concerning one having neither descendants nor ascendants [as heirs]." If a man dies, leaving no child but [only] a sister, she will have half of what he left. And he inherits from her if she [dies and] has no child. But if there are two sisters [or more], they will have two-thirds of what he left. If there are both brothers and sisters, the male will have the share of two females. Allah makes clear to you [His law], lest you go astray. And Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِى ٱلْكَلَـٰلَةِ ۚ إِنِ ٱمْرُؤٌا۟ هَلَكَ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أُخْتٌ فَلَهَا نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ ۚ وَهُوَ يَرِثُهَآ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهَا وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَتَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُمَا ٱلثُّلُثَانِ مِمَّا تَرَكَ ۚ وَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ إِخْوَةً رِّجَالًا وَنِسَآءً فَلِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۗ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ

This final ayah returns us to the discussion of inheritance that dominated much of the surah's beginning. It's fascinating that Allah ﷻ places this instruction here at the end. After everything He just told us about humility and submission, about not thinking we're above His commands, He gives us another legal ruling. Nobody should say, "Why is this here?" or "I'm above this." What kind of test is it? It's a test of humility. The placement itself teaches us that we don't question Allah's organization of His revelation.

يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ (They ask you for a legal ruling). Notice that Allah ﷻ is listening in on the conversation the Companions (RA) were having with the Prophet ﷺ. When people ask a fiqh question, it's rarely a one-liner. It's usually a long, detailed conversation. "What about al-kalalah? What does that mean exactly? Does it apply to this situation or that one?" Allah ﷻ listened to their entire discussion, and at the conclusion of it all says:

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِى ٱلْكَلَـٰلَةِ (Say, 'Allah gives you a ruling concerning al-kalalah'). In one statement, He summarizes and answers all their questions. Al-kalalah refers to someone who dies leaving neither parents nor children - no one in the direct line above or below.

From a linguistic perspective, there are two types of siblings in Arabic legal terminology. There are indirect siblings who share the same mother but have different fathers (perhaps from a previous marriage). Then there are direct siblings who share the same father, even if they have different mothers. The earlier ayah in the surah dealt with indirect siblings; this ayah clarifies the ruling for direct siblings.

إِنِ ٱمْرُؤٌا۟ هَلَكَ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أُخْتٌ فَلَهَا نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ (If a man dies, leaving no child but [having] a sister, she will have half of what he left). The sister receives half the estate when her brother dies without children.

وَهُوَ يَرِثُهَآ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهَا وَلَدٌ (And he inherits from her if she has no child). But if the situation is reversed - if the sister dies with no children and only has a brother - then he inherits her entire estate. The sister gets half; the brother gets everything. This reflects the financial responsibilities placed on men in Islamic law.

فَإِن كَانَتَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُمَا ٱلثُّلُثَانِ مِمَّا تَرَكَ (But if there are two sisters, they will have two-thirds of what he left). When there are two sisters, they collectively receive two-thirds, meaning each gets one-third.

وَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ إِخْوَةً رِّجَالًا وَنِسَآءً فَلِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ (But if there are brothers and sisters, the male will have the share of two females). When the deceased leaves behind multiple siblings - both brothers and sisters, with no parents or children - then the brothers receive twice the portion of the sisters.

يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ (Allah makes clear to you lest you go astray). The particle أَن here carries the meaning of لِأَلَّا - "so that you do not" or "lest you." Allah ﷻ clarifies these matters so that you remain on guidance. We could also understand it as: Allah ﷻ clarifies to you because without His clarification, you would have been misguided. These laws are not intuitive or based on human reasoning - they require divine revelation.

وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ (And Allah is Knowing of all things). He possesses complete knowledge of everything. He knows why He distributed inheritance this way. He knows the wisdom behind every share, every ratio, every condition. Even when we don't fully comprehend the reasoning, He does completely.

One of the profound benefits of separating this ayah and placing it at the surah's end is to help us appreciate that the entire surah is one unified discourse. Otherwise, we might think: that subject ended, this new subject started, that one ended, another began. But Allah ﷻ wants us to understand Surah An-Nisa as one comprehensive conversation. He brings back one subject, then returns to another, then circles back again. These are multiple threads weaving together within the same tapestry. This is how Allah ﷻ organizes much of the Quran - not in isolated, disconnected sections, but as an interwoven masterpiece where every part connects to and reinforces every other part.
